channel.title: Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Advice For Prospective Game Programmers
item.description:
0, 1 0 1 2, 1 0 1 0 1, 2. Wait, today I want to do advice for prospective game programmers. For those of you who are thinking about going into game programming, the TLDDW is. It’s different than other kind of programming — jobs you could get out.

I’m going to make this a fun Friday because it’s fun for me. May not be fun for you, but I like it. Bear in mind I’ve only done game programming, but I have lots of friends who are in other industries doing programming, including the military, and I did do one project for the Department of Defense. Uh, go look at the attached video: Troya’s Secret Fourth Project. So now let’s walk through all the ways. I think game programming is different than other programming that you could do for a living. I’ll get the beginning right, the easy part out of the way. The money is less and the stability is less.

item.title: Why I Don't Post On Forums Anymore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I don’t post on forums anymore.

This isn’t an answer to a direct question, but more of a response to I’ve seen people ask why game devs in general don’t interact more with game players And I’ve specifically seen things going. I wonder why Tim doesn’t post here much anymore, especially in places I used to post a lot. So I want to talk about that, because I used to be really, really active on in online forums And I posted to a lot of different ones, and I’m not even talking recently. Um, I used to before it was the web, when it was just the internet. I used to post on Usenet sites all the time, like compsysgames or recgamesfrp.

item.title: Dialog Quest Solutions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about dialogue quest solutions.

I’ve talked about these before It’s. What I mean by it is that’s when your character has an option to solve a quest through the conversation system. Now, yes, it usually means they have to have gone and found something or done some other things, but ultimately results the — the quest gets resolved by them.

item.title: Discovering D&D In 1979
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about discovering DnD in 1979.

Dungeons and Dragons was like nothing else that had ever been out before And I want to try to explain it to you. Because I was trying to explain it to a, coworker. Because if, you started playing DnD on a computer where there’s no DM, the computer handles it all. There are another players, There’s a tutorial that walks you through the basics so you have an idea of what to expect. You don’t have to learn the rules. I mean, you want to learn how things work, but you don’t have to learn how to run the rules.

item.title: Eight Level Design Guidelines
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about eight level design guidelines.

I don’t talk about level design much on this channel. I do have a level design playlist, which this will go on. And the reason for that is: like art, I don’t do level design very much.

However, as a game director, you are frequently expected to give guidelines to everyone on your team, not just people whose jobs you’ve done before. This is what happened in this one. I was asked to give some guidelines for what I wanted as level design. I think this was for The Outer Worlds, but it was based on something I had written back at uh Carbine for Wildstar. I wanted to make a top 10 list. I always like doing things in like, you know, top five or top 10, but I only came up with eight. So these are the eight you’re going to get. And I know that sounds disappointing, but if you have played any of my games, you know I’m not afraid to disappoint.

item.title: AD&D Design Pillars
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Advanced Dungeons and Dragons design pillars.

I’ve talked about design pillars on this channel. I have a whole video on it. I’ll link it below.

In short, these pillars are like the bedrock, the foundation of your game, from which all features and all content spring from and to which they serve. It’s basically you saying: “This is essentially what my game is about, and everything I’m going to talk about — from a mechanic to the storyline, to elements of the setting — will make sense with respect to those pillars. I want to talk about what AD and D’s design pillars were, why it was my first tabletop role playing game. It was the very first thing that told me what a role playing game could be, which, of course, very much influenced my career, but its design pillars were never clearly stated anywhere. The game itself sprang from strategy tabletop games, where they were essentially battle simulations and occasionally a hero character would like emerge from that. You’d have a bunch of battles and there was one surviving lieutenant or captain that survived battle after battle after battle and you kind, you were kind of starting to get attached to them and a uh, Dungeons and Dragons, and then later AD and D, which formalized a lot of uh, D and D’s rules, were people wanting to play characters from those sessions and have them become heroes where they would, you know, go into dungeons and fight villains and gain all kinds of loot and maybe get their castle and become a hero From all this. What I always took from Dn D as its fundamental pillar was, with reference to fantasy, because that’s what it was aimed at — is that a any character you could think of, whether you liked Lord of the Rings or whether you like the Hiboreian age, if you wanted to play Aragorn or you wanted.

item.title: The Future Of Generative AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the future of generative AI And already I know people’s hackles are rising.

But I’m going to make this a fun Friday because I want to talk about what I think is coming down the pipe. And I wanted to talk about this because, despite the fact that I know many of you hate it’s coming. Tech technology has never been bottled up once it’s been created. So it’s coming. It is going to arrive And I’m going to base what I think is going to show up in both the game industry and beyond on watching technology change in the game industry for the last 45 years and my own PhD work in artificial intelligence in the late 80s and early 90s. So I’ve already talked about artificial intelligence in general and my experience working on it in a video called artificial intelligence, which is linked in the description. I’ve also talked about the effects of artificial intelligence and technology in general on the game industry and other industries where it has actually disrupted them and eliminated them. There are industries that existed when I was a child that don’t exist anymore.

item.title: Carefree To Careful
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the game industry going from carefree to careful.

I’ve been in the industry since 1981. Most of you haven’t even been around since 1981. My career is older than most people who watch this channel And I’ve watched things change. So some of the things I’m going to talk about they’re not new. Um, what instigated this was I was looking through my idea book — This is one of many. The others are on my shelf behind me — And I look through them for ideas of just mechanics, settings, story lines, things I want to work on. And then I talk about them to co-workers.

item.title: Fallout vs. Arcanum Story States
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout versus Arcanum’s story states.

This is something I’ve mentioned many, many times on here. I always talk about Fallout having three and Arcanum having 27. Most recently, Uh, I did it in a video and Richard to the Argo asked: “Hey Tim, can you do a video breaking down what a 27 act story looks like? You’ve mentioned it a few times now and I can’t quite wrap my head around that number.

item.title: Would You Play A Game That...?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to ask you a question: Would you play a game?

That? What I mean by this is I’m going to describe several game ideas and I’m curious if you would play that game. I thought this would be a fun Friday to do, because each one I’m going to talk about a very specific feature and what it may mean about the game. And, by the way, in g, by game I mean role playing game. Well, I mean role playing video game. I have a whole video called what’s an, what is an RPG? That’s what I’m talking about here, Not what you call an RPG, what I call an RPG. And then consider that RPG with one of these features. By the way, some of these may sound like: hey, I played a game like that in this other genre. Great, I’m talking about when you play a character in an RPG and the play varies based on that character.

item.title: Toxic Positivity
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about toxic positivity and I really want to talk about this.

I put it off for a while. Seems weird to be talking about it now, with all the negativity in the world, but this channel is almost three years old and there’s always been a bunch of negativity in the world and it doesn’t seem to be going away. And I think this topic is important and I’m going to say the word. I think it’s very nuanced, which is why I kept putting it off. I know if I start talking about toxic positivity, people are going to think I’m talking about a particular game or developer. Publisher. Can’t stop that.

item.title: Evolution Of Design Documentation
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the evolution of design documentation.

This actually comes from a question from Noah Stavish, who asked: “Hey Tim, can you discuss what you expect out of design and technical documentation, both when you write it and when you review what others have written? Can you also touch on how teamwide expectations for documentation has changed over the past 40 years? What problems and solutions have you faced with these expectations?

item.title: Getting Older
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about getting older.

I know this is going to be a fun Friday so I’m going to keep it light, but I’m just going to talk to you about what getting older feels like, because I’ve gone from being the absolute youngest person on a gamedev team — because I started when I was 16 — to now being the oldest at 60. Now I don’t want to talk about agism. I have a whole video about that, so I don’t need to repeat all that here. You probably do want to watch it. I’ll link it in the description, because what surprises me the most about agism is we’re all getting older And when I watch someone be agist, I’m like you know you’re going to be in that group that you’re making fun of. And pretty soon some of you, in fact one person I’m like you’re in that group already. But I think it was more of a. I think they thought if they fought against the older person. They were fighting against their old, own aging.

item.title: It's OK To Change Your Mind
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about that.

It’s okay for you to change your mind. I’m wondering if this is going to be part of my series. It’s okay to, since I did one a few weeks ago about it’s okay to be proud, Had some very interesting comments from people who really took that as like: hey, you, want to be arrogant, You’re? You know, it’s that pride goeth before the fall. There were a lot of comments like that, Not what I meant, but okay, We’ll see how this one goes on. It’s okay to change your mind.

item.title: Archiving
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about archiving.

I’ve archived things for a long time, so I understand the importance of doing it, of tucking away things that you will want to look at later. I’ve done that for a lot of things. I’ve talked about my notes on this channel so many times and I I link a video below about my best note takingaking practices, which then links to all the other videos about my notes and even where me taking so many notes probably came from. But I keep a lot of stuff. This shirt is probably 20 years old. But I want to talk about code in spec, specifically because I have kept code for decades.

item.title: One More Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making one more game.

And I will get to what I’m talking about in a second, because it’s a fun Friday. So this is going to be a good fun Friday video. But I want to start with this. I’m on the record of saying I don’t know what gamers want or like in a game. I have a whole video called Do Devs Know What Gamers Like. Now in that video, a lot of people chose to misinterpret it, despite the fact that I was very clear. So let me restate this: I think that you know what you want, but I don’t think that most people — people, gamers or game devs — know what most people want.

item.title: Mini Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about mini games.

This comes from a very long set of questions from Mr Doomka. So let me read his questions first, then I want to talk about mini games. Hey, Tim, I understand your mistrust in miniames, but don’t they make the games more immersive? I feel like it does, for me, though the miniames are usually a gross o oversimplification of crafts, they let you feel like you’re actually doing something — lockpicking, hackpicking, etc.

item.title: Handling Advice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about handling advice.

This is when people tell you how to do something. People love to dispense advice, by the way.

Now, I’m not talking about criticism. Criticism is when people want to talk about what you did, whether it’s a method you used in your development of a game, say, or they want to talk about the game itself, what they think about the game. That’s criticism.

item.title: Lenses
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about lenses.

I’ve mentioned this before, and I’m going to make this a fun Friday video because it’s going to be kind of circular and in the middle of it I’m going to talk a lot about insomnia and then I’ll be back. So I’ve talked about lenses before. Lenses are the perspective that you and I and everyone tend to see the world through. Everybody has them, You have them. I talk mostly on this you know channel, about role playing games and player agency within them and nonlinearity of, because that’s my lens.

item.title: The Credits Problem, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the credits problem, part two.

This came from a comment or a question on my channel from Oman TF, tetra floroethylene. Anyway, Omen TF said: “Can you make a video experience as programmer of Star Trek Academy”. This a game back in the 90s. And.

My reply was: I wasn’t really a programmer on that team. I programmed ganal, which was my OS abstraction library. I have a whole video to that. I’ll link it in the description And I said my programming was on that, Even though they did come back and ask me for some new features to add to it and I did and it sped up their development time. They put my name in the credits but I wasn’t really part of the development team. By the way, I also appear in the credits for Atomic Bomberman and Max Mechanical Assault and Exploration.

item.title: Fallout: Destiny Or Preparation?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about whether Fallout was destined to happen or the result of massive preparation.

This came from a conversation I had with Matt Bal in his podcast, The Sewers of Paris. I will link it below.

It’s a really good podcast and I’m a fan of Matt’s YouTube channel because he talks a lot about LGBT representation in books and movies and TV shows way long time ago and back. I mean, he does modern day, but he goes really far back and shows how there were always gay people working in a lot of the movies and TV shows that you liked.

item.title: Scenes From Unretirement
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about scenes from unretirement.

I did a video recently — and I’ll link it below — about me being unretired And it reminded me that over a year ago I did scenes from my life in game development where I showed a bunch of photos I had of and a few videos of stuff I had that happened to me while I was had a game development career Occurred to me. I’m back into game development. I should continue that video, so that’s what this is. I’m going to go away and just show you images and some videos and talk over them, and I’ll be back at the end, All righty. So let’s start with. I arrived back at Obsidian, here I am outside the offices. This is the first day I got back, October 30th 2025..

item.title: Restricting Content Based On Player Choice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about restricting content based on player choice.

What do I mean by that? I’m talking about if you’ve ever played a game where there’s some content that you cannot see, based on something that you chose to do with your player. I’m not talking about restrictions on an options menu. I’m not talking about like you decided to play the lowest difficulty mode and because of that, you won’t see a certain hard boss monster. Or, when you do, they don’t use certain attacks with certain special effects, because that doesn’t occur unless you’re playing it on normal or hard difficulty.

item.title: Design Potholes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about design potholes.

This is something I mentioned in a video about I need it will be 5 weeks ago when you’re watching this. I mentioned it in my video on un-retirement, which I’ll link below. And I had a couple commenters on that video, namely Saber VS 7 and Leah from. There’s a lot of M’s in that name Basically wanted to know what I meant by design potholes and they said: can you talk about it? And, like me, talk about something crazy. So of course I’m going to talk about design potholes in detail. What I mean by the term is I call pothole a design pothole — really common design issues that can be avoided if you know to look for them. They’re like a pothole on a regular street. If you go slowly, you’re not really going to be hurt that much by a pothole, or your car is not going to be hurt. But if you’re cruising along and you hit a pothole, at a pretty decent speed, you could wreck your transmission or your, or you blow out a tire or something. But once you’ve been up and down that road a few times and you know those potholes are there, you know which lane is safe, or even in a lane where you can be positioned so that pothole is not a problem for you. That’s what these design potholes are. They’re really common design issues that are frequently occur in different lanes of design, whether they’re system design or narrative design or level design.

item.title: Great Developers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about great developers.

I know I’ve told stories on this channel about bad events and bad people And I’ve tried to explain that it’s because those are the learning moments in a lot of my stories. But it’s gotten to the point where some people went: “It sounds like you were surrounded by incompetence” And I’ve tried to explain how that isn’t the tr, isn’t true?

item.title: Fences
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fences.

It’s going to be interesting what I’m talking about because it came out of a comment on another video. That video was the spaghetti code video. I’ll link it below — where I talked about how code can become really not well structured because of frequent changes in the game. A commenter, Derek Posh, and I — he didn’t have a question, We were just talking back and forth about how that happens And I happened to mention that being both a designer and a programmer on the same game still led to spaghetti code, which means even eliminating any communication issues, because obviously programmer Tim knew what designer Tim wanted — still led to spaghetti code. And I talked about how that can happen because design changes And it led me to say that the most eyeopening experiences I’ve had in this industry have come from me being on both sides of a fence. So when I’m talking about fences, I’m not talking about fence sitting, I’m not talking about sitting on a fence and not deciding which one you want to come down on. I’m talking about actually being on both sides of a fence and understanding the issues on both sides and then trying to explain it to people who are firmly planted on one side.

item.title: Seeing The Game In The Prototype
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about seeing the game in the prototype — Now.

The alternate titles for this video were going to be [snorts]- should you be a game director? Or a litmus test for game directors, because I think this is such an important ability to be able to do that. Anyone at the game director level or above should be able to do this, and if they can’t.

item.title: Overanalyzing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about overanalyzing games.

This is something a lot of people do. They like to take a game and analyze it. But if I want to be less charitable, they like to take a game and they like to pick at it and just pick at it and pick at it. Sometimes they do that without even playing the game. They heard about it, They heard a description, they saw a trailer.

item.title: Catalyst Team Members
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about catalyst team members.

And by catalyst I’m not talking about the people whose life essences I absorb in order to stay youthful — Oh shoot, it’s not working anymore. No, I’m talking about people on the team that just seem to make the overall team better just by being on the team. And sometimes it’s really hard to identify how they’re doing it I eventually started calling these team members catalysts, because they’re like chemical catalysts that make reactions go faster or more efficiently or happen more readily at lower temperatures, but don’t themselves get used up in it.

item.title: Randomness In Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about randomness in games.

This is a question from Salute, the Captain, who said: “I was surprised to not find a video on randomness as a general mechanic on your channel. What are your thoughts on balancing out? Everything is too static and this feels unfair — weird random complaints”. So I did talk about how I’ve gone about in the past implementing features that used randomness and I’ll link that video below. The one thing that came out of it that I I could not stress highly enough in that video was that other game developers are terrible at what they mean by randomness. They will say randomness. That word will come out of their mouth repeatedly, even though I push back and I’m like “That’s not exactly what you want”. And I tried to explain to them what they want. They know I want it — random. Turns out they don’t want random. They want fairness and uniformity and cyclic behavior.

item.title: Stories I Won’t Tell
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about stories I won’t tell on this channel.

Let me get into why Figured this is a kind of funnish Friday video. First of all, about half of my channel’s content comes from questions that you guys ask. And you guys ask really good questions about games and also about other things like “Hey Tim, why do you think we haven’t detected a magnetic monopole” Or “Hey Tim, how did you develop your keen eye for fashion”. However, I’ve had to talk about questions that I can’t or won’t answer. I made an entire video about comments that I skip.

item.title: Tone In Video Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about pickling.

I want to talk about how you can pickle things at home to save. Specifically, I want to talk about pickling red onions and pickling garlic.

They’re both so easy to do that a baby could do it, Even a stupid baby. And we’ve all met one or two stupid babies, am I right? I mean, there’s, these babies know object permanence. They only remember things that they love or that they hate. I mean, come on, babies, there’s more up here than just an amygdala.

item.title: Taking Responsibility As A Lead
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about taking responsibility as a lead.

If you’re wondering what I mean by lead, I have a whole video on game development roles. I’ll link it below, and I even have a very specific video on roles if you’re in the programming department, because a lot of the examples I’m going to give when I talk about this are going to be programming examples where I was or was not a lead programmer. I also have videos if you’re interested. There’s also videos on how you might want to uh, behave if you’re a lead for the first time and also the important importance of being consistent if you’re a lead. I’ll link those videos below as well. I hope they help.

item.title: Game Development As An Identity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development as an identity And

this is probably going to be a fun Friday video because it’s going to be about game development, but it’s going to be kind of personal, Not 100% sure where it’s going to go, maybe a little rambly, Sorry about that. So 5 years ago — right now it’s January of 2026.. Sorry, there’s a fire truck outside. That’s interesting, so it’s January of 2026, and back in May of 2020, so over five years ago, I semi-retired And what that means is I went from being an employee at Obsidian to being a contractor And I consulted at other companies too — all part-time. It wasn’t what I was doing full-time. This let me see a lot of different game development processes. It was fascinating to do it I actually got to work on a lot of toys. I have two videos on semi-retirement, One talking about semi-retirement and the other one talking about why I even did it The whole time.

item.title: Creating Playtest Plans
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about creating play test plans.

This is something that’s really important if you’re making an RPG — And yes, this is going to be about be about making them for RPGs, because that’s what I make. I’ve been asked a lot about what procedure I follow And it’s been a little different for some of them and I’ll go over that. I spent a lot of time trying to find the plans. I know I wrote specific plans down. I remember writing the Temple of Elemental Evil play test plan, but I went through all my Troka note archive and I couldn’t find anything. Although I did find an email referencing the vampire play test plan, but it wasn’t attached to the email. So I don’t know, maybe someone else mailed them that and I was just talking with them about it. But in general, I make play test plans when the QA is going to be done out of house. Fallout was in-house and I met with that QA lead.

item.title: Resurrecting The Arcanum IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about resurrecting the Arcanum intellectual property.

I get asked a lot on this channel in the comment section about making more Arcanum. And I have videos on doing a remake of Arcanum, You know, bringing it up to modern standards — hardware code, UI. There’s a video on.

I’ll link it below. I’ve also talked about remastering Arcanum. I’ve also talked about which.

item.title: Toys And Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about toys and games.

This is a fun Friday. In fact, for me it’s New Year’s Day. It’s a very cold and rainy New Year’s Day here in Southern California, but it’s New Year’s Day, meaning you’re going to be seeing this at the end of January, which means my buffer has shrunk from two months to barely a month. Uh, for lots of reasons: the move, work life. I used to have a two-month buffer. Now I’ve got a one-month buffer, but that’s why I like having a buffer, because the it’s the.

item.title: Code Comments
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about code comments.

I made a video about a month ago where I called commenting on comments, which was about me talking about the comments left on this channel. But several people left comments on that commenting about comments video, thinking that I was going to be talking about commenting source code. And I thought, well, that’s a good idea. I’ve never done that.

item.title: Unretirement
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about unretirement.

That’s what I’m calling my coming out of retirement, back into being employed, rather than calling it re-employed or unretire. Well, I guess I do call it a retirement, That’s what I call it.

Several people asked: what is that? What’s going on, What’s it like?

I’ll have to explain a bit more about my semi-retirement to make the unretirement make sense, but I do have a few videos on me being semi-retired and I’ll link them below so you can watch those. But, in short, what happened was when I moved to Seattle in 2020, Obsidian told me that they weren’t prepared to have outofstate employees. So I got switched to being a contractor. And I was fine. I was fine with that. This was before I had my YouTube channel, and it was right in the middle of COVID. This was June of 2020., so what happened was, for a few months, I don’t think anybody knew my status had changed.

item.title: A Typical Day Of Making Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a typical day of making Fallout, kind of to give you an insight into what it was like.

And I’m going to do it in the form of a musical. A typical day of making fall. No, we’re not going to do that Scratch that, I’m just going to talk about it. This time I’m going to talk about, to me, a typical day for making Fallout. It’s going to be about a year or so into development.

item.title: Try Roleplaying
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about trying role playing.

You may be wondering what I’m saying, because you’re probably already thinking you’re doing that You’re playing role playing games. You’re playing a lot of RPGs and you’re playing games that say they have RPG elements. But I think in a lot of those people don’t really do role playing. At best they may be handed a role and go: “Okay, that’s what I’m going to play”. But let me walk through what I mean.

item.title: Spaghetti Code
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about spaghetti code, what it is, why it exists, what people say about it?

Many of you talk about spaghetti code, maybe without using that word: spaghetti code. What spaghetti code is when you look at a piece of code from a game and it’s just all over the place. It’s poorly structured, maybe there’s a lot of methods that have tons of different parameters in them. There’s methods that look very similar to each other. Maybe there’s duplicate methods. There’s methods in different modules that seem to be doing the same thing. Spaghetti code, Basically poorly structured code. Most people go “H, stupid lazy programmer”.

item.title: Being Real
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about being real.

I just had a friend say that he likes to watch my channel because I’m real And he says, you know, he knows me in real life and that I’m not doing an act for the channel, that I’m just being me telling my stories. And he said he can see it’s me, It’s that the channel is the same passion and gesticulations, and forgetting the names of people and games and techniques like subsurface scattering — that I do in real life. That’s me in real life too, so it was interesting because I at first, when he said this, though, I scratched my head over it because I’m doing a podcast here. Effectively, this is a podcast and that’s what everybody does, right. And he went on to explain. He goes “Look.

item.title: Quest Types
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about quest types.

What I mean by that is, in a role-playing game, you can often have a lot of different types of quests, and people talk about this a lot. Oh, I went on a kill quest and I went on a collect quest, or I went on a fetch quest. These are all different types of quests that are usually implemented in a role-playing game, and for different reasons. I’m going to talk about why, but I I thought about this because I recently made a video just about escort quests, which is one type of quest type. And I really doubled down on issues that arise from escort quests And I realized — even though, okay, I’ll admit, I’m not a huge fan of escort quests, but I realized — that a lot of these things apply to the other quest types. So I wanted to talk about them and explain, first of all, why all these fixes people were offering — they’re possible, but why they may not have.

item.title: It's OK To Be Proud
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why it’s okay for you to be proud, especially of your work and things you do.

And this is probably going to be a bit of a meandering video, so bear with me, but I think it deserves being said, especially in this day and age and on the internet and stuff like that. But I’ll get to it, if you’ve watched this channel for even a tiny amount of time, you will know that I tell a lot of stories. I have a lot of stories to tell. I’ve been doing this for a long time.

item.title: Arguing About Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arguing about games.

I suppose this might be considered a not so fun Friday video, but I just want to talk about it because it’s something that happens so much. There’s so much arguing. I mean, there’s so much arguing about everything on the on in life, but I make games. So let’s talk about arguing about games. I’ll start by saying people have always argued about games. They’ve always argued about games.

item.title: Finding Meaning In Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about finding meaning in games.

Let’s face it, I’ve made a lot of games. Look at this face. I’ve made a lot of games in the last 45 years. These games have very rich settings, as rich as I can come up with. I remember I always tell you go setting, story, system mechanics. So my games I try to give them as rich a setting as I can, given whatever technical, technological or other limitations that they may have had. And then I give a very long, involved main story but then a lot of NPCs and they all have lots of side stories too. So my games, because of that, my games touch on a lot of different things. There’s a lot of stuff going on and there’s a lot of themes in my games and I even have said there’s one theme that’s in common in all my games. I have a whole video about it, The.

item.title: Running Meetings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to talk about running meetings, something I know you all love.

Everyone’s always telling me, Tim, I love meetings, Talk more about them. Actually, I know you don’t like meetings, but I was having lunch with a friend of mine who’s also in the game industry, in a very different role than I have. And I mentioned to her that last week I was in a meeting and I got called out for not talking at all. We got to the end of the meeting — It was almost an hour long — and somebody said: “Well, Tim, you’ve been quiet, You haven’t said anything. What do you think”, and I even said my answer was: “Well, this is the first time I’ve been to this meeting that you guys have been having for a few months now. I just wanted to listen”.

item.title: Why Not Create A New IP Today?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. It’s a fun Friday, so I want to tell you a story called.

Why Are You Not Creating a New IP today? It’s a fun story and I will get to it. Let me give you some background.

I’ve created a lot of new IPs from scratch: Fallout, Arcanum, Wildstar, Pillars, Tyranny, The Outer Worlds. In all of those I was a team. So it was a whole bunch of people came together to make those.

item.title: Self-Consistent Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about self-consistent lore.

I’ve discussed realism and lore actually in a couple different videos. One I compared realism to fun and talked about how they’re not the same thing and I never consider them the same thing And for me fun trumps realism. So I’ll link that video in the description. But I’ve also talked about how realism isn’t even necessary. Um, it’s something that sometimes people comment on like this isn’t realistic and they’re playing a fantasy game with dragons and spells. I think part of the problem is realism gets conflated with consistency.

item.title: Why Troika Games Were So Buggy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why Troika games were so buggy.

I get asked this a lot and there are a lot of articles and videos talking about how Troika games, were just so incredibly buggy. And I do have a video on why RPGs in general are so buggy and I’ll link it in the description And I will re — remind you: Troika only RPGs. We made three RPGs. It’s not why we were called Troika: Um, but the short answer is we had a lot of feature ideas. We did not edit ourselves very much at all and we were a small team and that’s just combined to make very, very buggy RPGs.

item.title: Commenting On Comments
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do some commenting on comments.

It’s a fun Friday, Something that’s been bubbling in my brain case and shows up in my notes. I mean I take notes about my channel. Why It’s me?

Of course I take notes. I write notes about comments, now this video is going to be similar to the other videos I have about making a channel. Um, I don’t even consider myself a YouTuber, but my Wikipedia page now says I’m a game developer and YouTuber. So it must be true.

item.title: Christmas. Christmas Never Changes.
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Christmas.

Christmas never changes, Except it kind of does. For me, recording this, it’s the day after Thanksgiving here in the United States. So I am full of ham, not turkey. I don’t like turkey. I never really liked turkey. I thought it was dry and kind of tasted off. But a few years ago I miscooked the turkey and I got food poisoning and I’m like: why am I doing this? I don’t even like this, meat. So now I make ham, I make a lot of ham and I eat the ham and I love ham.

item.title: Game Developer Motivations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game developer motivations.

I see a lot of YouTube videos and game forum posts and even articles on game sites talking about game developers and why they make the games they make and why they do what they do and all kinds of stuff. And most of them are just wildly wrong, often in a very amusing way. When I watch movies that have a programmer usually called a hacker, I laugh at the portrayal because it is usually so funnily amusingly wrong that it’s just it’s funny to watch And of course it makes me go, “Oh, the writer has never actually written a line of code in their life. So this is what they think programmers do and what they’re like and what programmers think. Just by watching programmers”.

item.title: The Joy Of Doing
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the joy of doing.

I want to tell you a story today. Well, this, is going to go out as a fun Friday story. So it’s really just me telling a long story with a moral at the end, I guess [snorts]. But today is Monday, ironically, and this story just happened yesterday, which was a Sunday, which is kind of important to the story. But I’m digressing, I’m going to tell the story.

item.title: Escort Quests
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about escort quests.

A while back I did a video called RPG features. Nobody asked for and I included escort quests on that video. Why? I’ve never seen a review asking for more escort quests ever. Not once I’ve seen reviews going: “I wish there was more combat in here. I wish the combat was better. I wish there was more opportunity to do stealth quests. I wish there was more dialogue with NPCs. But not once have I seen a review going: “Do you know what This RPG needs?

item.title: What Is An Immersive Sim?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what is an immersive sim.

Definitions are hard. I almost didn’t want to make this video, but Shadow the Cat 7 actually joined my channel as a master member to not only have me answer this, but push it in the queue. So here we go.

Shadow the Cat 7 asks: “I was wondering what is Tim Kane’s definition of an immersive sim? I don’t think I’ve heard you mention them at all, but it seems like there’s a lot of debate around the subject”. There’s not just a lot of debate over what an immersive sim is. There’s a lot of debate about what anything is. I have an entire video devoted to what a cup is.

item.title: Brainstorming Example
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a brainstorming example.

And by that I mean two years ago I did a video on how I like to brainstorm. I used to run brainstorming meetings at almost every company I worked at, where I’d gather people together, cross-disciplinary people who wanted to, and it was all volunteer. If you didn’t want to go to the brainstorming session, you certainly didn’t have to, and we were going to talk about either an idea I was going to present or I was there to collect ideas. You know, pay people, give me examples of what kind of games you want to make. I mean, that’s how Fallout got started.

item.title: Game Engines And Feature Creep
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about game engines and feature creep.

This is an interesting thing brought up by commenter Lord of Cake, who wrote: “I wonder how much of this is feature creep problem related to using standard engines like Unity or Unreal. I’ve noticed that a lot of older games had very few programmers listed in the credits, that they managed to make a huge game and a custom engine completely from scratch. My hypothesis would be that it’s tempting to add features when they’re already built into the engine and you merely have to tweak them, Whereas having to build them into the engine itself is enough of a barrier to make you stop and think whether you really want or need this specific feature in the game.

item.title: Why Are Modern Games So Big?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

I’m frequently asked: why is it so big? And I’m like, excuse me, Let me back up. I’m frequently asked: why are modern games so much bigger than older games? This comes up a lot on the channel and I’ve answered it many times down in the comments. So I decided to finally make a video about it and talk about it in excruciating detail so that I can just point to people at this video and go, “This is why”. If you’re here for the short answer — why are modern games so big — I’ve got it for you: Art, tap out. Now We’re done. That’s pretty much the whole answer, if you want some nuance to it, if you want some detail on art, and also that other things have gotten bigger, just not as big as art — then that’s what I’m going to talk about for the rest of the video. So let’s dive into it. Let’s start with art, which is the biggest contributor to why the file sizes on a game. That you buy either physically or digitally, is so big.

item.title: Cain On Games Update
item.description: Hi everyone, it’s me. Tim Decided to do a Cain on Games update because, as you can see, things have changed, Lots of changes actually. So I I thought I’d spend a fun Friday walking through the changes so you guys can know what’s going on. First off, I have moved back to Southern California. I did that a little over a week ago my time, but remember I have a big queue — It’s like 6 weeks long, I think — on this channel. So, from your point of view, you kept seeing a bunch of videos that I filmed up in Seattle. Although you did see a video on Arcanum magic design that popped up in this place, These shelves had nothing in it I hadn’t unpacked It’s because someone who is a super mutant paid for a priority video and wanted to know about Arcanum magic design. So I dropped what I was doing and I talked about Arcanum, magic design, and colleges and magic tech and all that.

item.title: Institutional Knowledge
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about institutional knowledge, which is sometimes called tribal knowledge, but I don’t like that name.

Tribal knowledge It just it doesn’t really capture what I’m trying to say here. And institutional knowledge I will define as things you’re expected to know when you work at a particular company and on particular projects, but they’re not written down. Uh, I was. I had been planning this talk for about a week when I put up a video called RPG features. Nobody asked for, and one of the commenters, Scorbus, mentioned this very thing about: hey, do you think some of these RPG features keep getting put in because of loss of institutional knowledge? And I said yes, they do. And in fact I’m planning to talk about it. So let’s talk about it here [snorts]. And before I even start, let me just point out that that institutional knowledge is different from like preservation. Even I’m talking about like.

item.title: Temple Of Elemental Evil Design Highlights
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Temple of Elemental Evil design highlights.

This is a document I found in my archive. I was looking through it for something else, but I thought I’d share this because for all you people out there who are developing games, at some point you’re going to be asked to promote this game, maybe to press or maybe to fans, maybe at a conference or a convention, or maybe in a recorded interview or a playthrough. You should know what is important to talk about at such interviews and events, including what makes your game special or unique or why the press should be interested in it or why a player would want to play it I was asked by Atari to make this list, which they asked me. They said: “Could you make some design highlights”. This was because they were already planning for press events. This is dated May 13th 2003., there was a press event coming up in August, which is just a few months away. I wasn’t going to do that press event. That was going to be somebody else on the team. They had a family emergency. Someone else said they’d do it, but they didn’t have a passport that was valid, so I did it I have a video on this. It’s the Temple of Elemental Evil. Press event Took place in August 2003 in a castle south of London.

item.title: Let's Talk About Canon
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, let’s talk about Canon.

It’s a fun Friday. I want to talk about it, talk about different things, how I use it here, A lot of comments that I get about it And also, yeah, I see some boxes in the background.

Don’t worry about that, It’s that’s not what I’m talking about today. Just some boxes, some empty shelves.

Let’s move on, so. A lot of talking about canon on my channel and that’s what I want to talk about. Like, what is it? Who gets to decide it? What happens when people disagree? Wow, I see a lot of discussion of this.

item.title: Leaving Some Mystery
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about leaving some mystery in your games.

I almost called this building a mystery, and then I was going to have a guitar and an onk necklace and I was going to do that Sarah McLachlan song I know you’re thinking of. But let’s just say that it didn’t test well with the focus group and it would leave me open to copyright disputes. So you’re getting leaving a mystery. What do I mean by that? This is something that’s come up in a lot of my videos and it came up recently in um.

item.title: Arcanum Experience Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Arcanum experience point system, the design behind it and how it’s kind of contentious.

It was to the dev team back in the day and players today still talk about it. So let’s dive into it. This actually comes from a question from blah vlah, who asked: “Can you talk about the experience system in Arcanum? I’m not sure there’s another game with similar displacement of XP, being mostly on a per hit basis, with a pretty minor emphasis on the per kill point. This makes most solo builds very strong, but politician characters that collect a lot of followers or mages that specialize in summoning get very little experience. What was your design philosophy here? It’s a good question.

item.title: Insults and Backhanded Compliments
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about insults and backhanded compliments.

I kind of mean this in a fun way. Not sure this is going to be a fun Friday video or not, You know what. I’ll just let it go up whenever it goes up. But lately I’ve been going through all my notes and I guess what I should call my archive. Now I was looking for a particular thing, but it’s kind of vague, so searching wasn’t helping.

item.title: Non-Expository Arcanum Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about non-expository Arcanum lore.

This is a companion video to the non-expository Fallout lore that I did a while back, where Fath Daniel asked: “What is Arcanum, non-expository lore” And I thought, “Yeah, I should do that because that makes sense” And I It’s something that it’s interesting. Um, it was harder for me to do this for Arcanum than for Fallout And because Fallout was a smaller game in sense of scope, number of NPCs, number of dialogues, cutscenes, in-game books — So it was harder for me to find Arcanum lore that had never been, that had never appeared in the game already. Like, there’s a cut scene that mentions the lore or an NPC talks about it at length, and we had several NPCs that could talk about a lot of lore that you may not know, because it was hidden behind a, speech skill, or you had to have a certain reactivity level with them, or you just never dove down. In their dialogue tree to find it. And then there were lots and lots and lots of books. So I tried to find lore for Arcanum.

item.title: Lessons From Old Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about lessons from old games I’ve never talked about.

This Comes from a good question from you, Hasty 6779 — and I hope I pronounced your name right — Who asks: “Hi Tim, a question that hopefully hasn’t been asked already: What do you think can be learned from old games? Is there any lost wisdom out there”. Yes, there is Good question, Particularly good question because it’s clear and short and to the point. So I really like that question. I’m not going to say I thought I had talked about this, but I do have a lot of other lessons from videos.

item.title: Rags To Riches: A Review by Tom Decker
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to read to you a Rags to Rich’s review written by Tom Decker, the producer and designer of the game.

I was going through my archives and I realized I’ve hardly ever said anything about Rags to Riches. I think I have one video on the making of which I’ll link below, and I think I mentioned it in a couple other videos where it just as an aside — Oh, by the way, rags to rich is this and I’ll post a video or post a link at the bottom to um in the description to the rags to riches playlist, which doesn’t have many videos in it — Thought I’d fix that. Rags to Riches was the third game I made in my career and the second game I made at Interplay, and it was a business simulation which wasn’t exactly, you know, high in my list of things to do. I tried to get switched with another programmer, uh, Mark Whittley. He was doing Lord of.

item.title: Arcanum Magick Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum magic design.

But first let me address what’s going on. New digs haven’t had time to even unpack the books into the bookshelves when Anubis1101 asked some really good questions And, as a super mutant, he gets a priority video, which means I just dropped what I was doing. I said “Stop Hammer time”, well, stop Arcanum time And did this video And it’ll be up. Um, I think Monday’s is already up, so it’ll be up on Wednesday. But then expect things to go back to the old digs for a while, because I had a lot of video in the queue. But anyway, let’s get into Anubis’s questions. He asked: “Tim, I’m really curious about the magic colleges in Arcanum and how you would approach such a system for a new game.

item.title: RPG Features Nobody Asked For
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about RPG features nobody asked for This is kind of a video for my fellow RPG game developers, because I keep seeing these features in RPGs and I don’t think anybody wants them and I don’t know what’s going on.

And I’m wondering sometimes if there’s a junior designer out there who’s just never seen these features before, even though they been in a lot of RPGs already, or. There’s this designer that thinks “Yeah, I’ve seen this done a lot, but this time I’m going to get it right”? No, No, you’re not. These features keep going at RPGs. They don’t work, and either, I’m sure, either QA complains about it or, oh my god, it ships, and then these bad reviews come out and then everybody’s unhappy, The designer’s unhappy, the company’s unhappy.

item.title: AD&D Questions From Teenage Tim
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell you some — well, read you some advanced Dungeons and Dragons questions from teenage Tim.

What, what is this? Are you? », what? Last week’s fun Friday I had a list of questions that me and the team assembled that we asked Watsi about the Temple of Elemental Evil module that we’re making into a computer game. That made me wonder, because I know I had done the same thing. I think it was 1980, so I would have been 14,, maybe 15. And I had a set of questions that I sent to TSR, because when I was young I was super fearless about just sending mail to companies. I have a video on how to get a job where I show you the designs that I sent to Atari when I was 13,, I think.

item.title: Temporary Buffs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about temporary buffs.

And I mean. What I mean by that are the buffs you get for a short period of time for having a spell cast on you or drinking a potion or maybe eating food in an RPG. This is based on a large set of questions asked by future Astrominer. I’m going to read all their questions, then I’m going to talk about it a little bit and then I’m going to go through each question in order and answer it. This is going to be long and it’s going to reference a lot of videos, but that’s actually something I need to talk about after I read you the questions. So let’s start.

item.title: Arcanum Fate Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum Fate Points.

Yes, I want to talk about a very particular feature in a specific game that’s almost 25 years old. But this comes from a question from Beefy NFN. I know what that means. Wait, no, I don’t. I have no idea.

Let’s continue. Asked: “I’d really like to hear you go into detail about how you balanced Arcanum with fate points. As someone who has never seen gameplay of Arcanum, let alone played it, The idea of fate points is kind of offputting to me as a game mechanic. As a player, I tend to hoard items that are one use and only use them when absolutely necessary, which means I end the game with a bunch of set items. So I’m curious how that applies in Arcanum with Fate Points.

item.title: Questions To WotC About ToEE
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to share with you questions I sent to Wizards of the Coast about Temple of Elemental Evil.

This is a fun Friday, so this is not super serious, but it is important, I think, to dive into. Uh, let me tell you why I’m doing this. I’ve been looking for the test plan that I made for Tempo of Elemental Evil that I sent to Atari QA. I was planning to do a video on that. I’m still planning to do a video on that, but I can’t find the test plan And I know I wrote one.

item.title: Sound Effects
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about sound effects.

I have talked on this channel several times about music in games. I’ve talked about ambient music in general because I really love ambient music. I own a huge ambient music collection. I have a whole video about that. I also have talked specifically about Fallout’s music and the influences on it and how I specifically gave ambient music to our audio director, who passed it on to Mark Morgan, who made the most beautiful Fallout music Definitely should watch that video. And the reason for all this is, I feel like music is so important in establishing the tone of your game because it’s something that’s just there and it enters a whole different way than most of the stuff in your game. It’s enough said about that, but I wanted to talk about sound effects today because I realized I’ve never talked about it before I don’t have a whole lot to say about audio because, as if I have to remind you — all, I’m not a musician, Can we roll that clip? Okay, I think that proved I’m not a musician.

item.title: Physical vs. Digital Game Media
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about physical versus digital media.

This question comes from Daniel Dare 2640, who asked: “My question is: what do you think about the move away from physical game boxes and the special gifts you had sometimes, Whether it would be a figurine or a map or some little trinket that made you feel a little bit more real, especially, I’m thinking maps and manuals? I know you can buy special editions and that’s probably a good way to go, but it was common place at one stage.

item.title: Lessons As An Old Person
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to share lessons I’ve learned as an old person.

I’m going to put this up on a Friday. I’m not sure if it counts as a fun Friday, but it’s not as much game development as it’s lessons I’ve learned or have come to learn now that I’m older And since all I’ve ever done is game development, it’s game development stories Context. I turned 60 a few weeks ago. Many of you gave me happy birthday wishes. Thank you, Um.

item.title: Fungible Employees
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to talk about flem.

You know what flem is in the back of your throat. Some of you are swallowing it right now. Today I want to do a deep dive into the nature and chemistry of flem. So let’s start with: Oh wait, no, no.

Today I want to talk about funible employees: Fungeible. I like that word because it’s got the word fun in it And a lot of companies think employees are funible. What do we mean by the word fungeible? And I like it. I’m using it here in a very specific way.

item.title: Adding A Feature: Then And Now
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adding a feature to a game back in the day compared to today.

I almost called this why game development costs more, but I think that will attract a lot of angry attention. So what I want to talk about is development costs, and I want to talk about it in the context of why adding a particular feature is harder to do today than it was 20, 30 years ago. I see a lot of people talking about game development and how it takes longer and it costs more. But they often get the underlying reasons wrong, Um, and sometimes I think they deliberately get it wrong, Um, because they’re trying to promote an agenda or get clicks or views, and sometimes they just feel like insulting developers. So it’s like we’re talking about how much it costs and that’ll get people riled up and then they can be mad at developers.

item.title: Game Trailers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game trailers.

I’ve never talked about this and I have a caveat to give, but I will say this at the beginning: Game trailers are easy to make, but it’s really hard to make a good one. I’ll get into that, but this came from a question from viewer KMG9763 — KMG9763 - KMG9763, who asked: “Any tips on how to make a good game trailer? I feel like modern game trailers are either so artificial that they become boring and sterile, or so random that I can’t really tell what the game is about. Is it good to make multiple trailers — one for lore, one for gameplay, etc. Or should you make one big trailer and try to fit it all in? I’m from a time when games were advertised on TV at Me too. And I remember the first time I saw a game trailer as a preview on a DVD movie and I had never felt so invested in a game before.

item.title: The Importance Of Conversation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the importance of conversation.

I know to some people I might go. You know my secret weapon: it’s talking. Maybe that’s not so secret, since all I do on this channel is talk and talk and a lot of people I’ve worked with say “Wow, you talk a lot”.

But and also sometimes I worry that I babble like a babbling idiot. But recently I said that to a friend of mine and he said “No, you’re more like a babbling brook.

item.title: Why I Don’t Solo Dev
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about why I don’t solo dev.

I’ve been asked this. I’ve been asked: hey, now that you’re semi-retired and you’re only working part-time, why don’t you make games all by yourself? Why don’t you solo dev?

You could do anything you want. Why not do that? So I decided to talk about why I don’t do it. Um, and I’m going to tell you, because there’s actually a bunch of reasons.

item.title: Non-Expository Fallout Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, for a fun Friday, I wanted to talk about nonexpository Fallout lore.

Let me explain what I mean by that. First of all, I’m talking about lore from the first game only, not even from Fallout 2.. I’m talking about lore that the original team discussed among ourselves and decided was true, and then we made the game based on that. We never wrote this stuff down. In fact, there weren’t a lot of design documents back in the 90s. I think the vision document was one of the few truly laid out, organized, specific documents we wrote.

item.title: Passivity
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about passivity.

What I mean by that is people who work with zero passion about it. They just go with the flow. They don’t care very much about one way or the other. They don’t care about a particular feature or game element. Of course I’m talking about this in the game industry, but bear with me, I have examples of passivity from everything.

item.title: Game Developer Stage Limitations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game developer stage limitations, which is a somewhat complicated talk.

And yes, it’s dark because in Seattle, once summer ends, it starts getting darker earlier in the evening and it stays dark later in the morning And I get up around. You know, I’m up at this desk by 600..

Sun’s not coming up for another half hour. I’m not waiting for it. This topic on the limitations by game production stage is complicated. It wasn’t something I would have talked about a year or two ago because I had to lay a whole foundation. So I’m going to reference videos while I talk, Recommend you watch the ones I referenced, or at least have watched them, because I’m not going to redefine terms and get lost in talking about game development roles. But first I do want to talk about what I’m not talking about here, because I’ve had. I’ve talked about limitations before. I’ve talked about my own limitations. Um, I have.

item.title: Second Toy Post Mortem
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give you my second toy postmortem.

It’s for a little toy I was working on called Dungeons and Dices, which turned out was already the name in use by an existing game and also a burger restaurant in Brazil. So let that be a lesson to you: that you should always do a trademark search before you publish a game. Anyway, this is a game that I started over two years ago. I keep saying: game, it’s a toy. What’s the difference between a toy and a game? A game you play, a toy you play with. This is a toy that I started over two years ago. I had a video c that I called my insomnia video. I think it was called coding toys — insomnia edition.

item.title: Leading For The First Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about leading for the first time, In particular, the very first time you’re put in a lead role or even leading an entire project, a game director role.

I want to talk about that because it’s scary, I know. But one thing I want you to remember is that everyone did it for the first time And I thought about the first time I was in charge of an entire project. And this was a long time ago. This was in 1994., and I had been a lead programmer before And I’d been a lead programmer when there weren’t any other programmers. So there was no management involved, It was just I’m the only programmer and I did all the stuff.

item.title: When To Step Out Of The Way
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about when to step out of the way — or my alternate title, which was way too long to fit on the title card — Things I didn’t do as much as didn’t stop from happening, which isn’t something that I said.

It’s what somebody said to me It’s, I’ll give you. I’ll tell you that story that this, video, is probably about the single most important thing that you can learn how to do if you want to be a leader, if you want to be the lead programmer or lead artist or lead designer of a group, if you want to be a producer or, if it happens, you’re a game director. And that most important thing that you need to learn how to do, above everything else, is when to just get out of the way and let the people who are on your team do what they do best.

item.title: Early Morning Workers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about early morning workers.

It’s my fun Friday video. I’m talking to you, my fellow early morning people, because I know you don’t get the kudos that you deserve. Everyone’s talking about the people who work late. Everyone likes to talk about how they burn the midnight oil. Nobody talks about my peeps, who come in at 6:00 or 7:00 am and start working. So I wanted to talk about that.

item.title: Chats And Interviews
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about chats and interviews.

And by that I mean chats on this channel with other people, when I have someone come on and we chat for an hour or two, or interviews on other people’s channels, where I appear on someone else’s channel or I do an interview with some other site. I want to talk about why I’m not doing any more of the former and very few of the latter. And I get asked this a lot. Um, it’s probably not the most frequent or even in the top five, but it’s certainly in the top 10 type of question I get: Hey Tim, you should be on this guy’s channel, or hey Tim, why don’t you bring this person on and talk about you know some game you made with them. And I love that.

item.title: Keeping Engine & Game Separate In Arcanum
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about keeping the game and the engine separate in Arcanum.

This comes from a question from All Lucky 7 who asked, “Interesting, that the mechanics and the engine part of the game were kept separate. Would you mind explaining how that is or was done”. So if you’ve watched this channel and you know I’ve referenced TIG and Gnol — my operating system, abstraction libraries, a ton. There’s a video I’ll link below.

item.title: My Uncredited Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my uncredited game.

I’ve mentioned it obliquely here and there that there’s a game out there that you know I developed that does not have my name in the credits by my choice. So it’s a fun Friday. I thought I’d talk about it, talk about why I had my name removed, and it will be a story. So let me explain everything that happened. So this is back in the ‘9s and a company had — I’m going to keep the company out of — this Company had a shipped game. So they had a game that they already designed, coded, tested and shipped. I had nothing to do with it and they had done two patches on it, one pretty extensive.

item.title: Using Combat Skills Outside Of Combat
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using combat skills outside of combat.

This video can be seen as a companion piece to yesterday’s video about using non-combat skills in combat. And I’m filming this the day after the previous video, but the previous video is probably one you watched two days ago. I’ll link it below And I also want to remind you guys, it’s still June for me, so I haven’t seen your comments on that video. I thought I’d make this video before I saw your comments, so we can kind of compare our ideas on, in this case, usage making, utility for combat skills outside of combat. So you may want to watch the other one first. In any case, I’ll also start off by saying this has been done before. I know Fallout did it, I know The Outer Worlds did it.

item.title: Using Non Combat Skills In Combat
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using non-combat skills in combat.

This comes from a question from IE50 — IE50 - IE50, who asked: “I don’t know if you’ve done a video on this, but I was wondering about your thoughts on how traditionally non-combat skills can assist in combat. You’ve mentioned how skill-based games can result in players making dud characters because they end up sucking in combat. What about having those skills have related perks or actions that are valuable in combat? Or the more common option is having certain combat actions scale off. Non-combat skills Examples: intelligent scale skills, scale medicine, charisma, skills for team buffs, etc.

item.title: Maps
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about maps.

I’ve never really talked about maps before because I don’t really make the world maps that you see in my games, but I wanted to talk about them because it’s a fun Friday and I do have a reason for wanting to talk about maps, which I will get to in just a second. But I wanted to kind of do my kind of rambling preamble stuff. I love maps. I’ve always loved maps, Even as a kid. My room was full of maps. I had maps that I would get from when our family would take car trips and I. We would stop at a gas station and they would give you maps of the area. It might have been a road map. It might have been like: here’s a map to beautiful Laurier Ca Caverns, which is someplace we went because we all really wanted to see the rock formations that looked like eggs and bacon. I had theme park maps, there was a theme park in Virginia called King’s Dominion that we went to several times, but my family also went to Disney World a couple times and I had maps from th those places and I would just put them up on the wall and even had a globe. I I was one of those kids who liked looking at a globe, so I had a globe in my room.

item.title: Prioritizing Tasks
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about prioritizing tasks.

What do I mean by this? Doesn’t matter, I think, whether you’re in a big, you know hundred thousand person team, you’re in a small team or even if you’re doing solo dev, you have to prioritize the work tasks to get a game done — And I realize I’ve never really talked about this, Um it. Some of it is, I always thought, kind of self-evident, but there is some minutia that’s kind of interesting and some stuff I’ve learned after a long time. So I thought I’d talk about prioritizing tasks And one of the first questions is: why do I have to do that? Just get it all done, because you will not get it all done.

item.title: Psychology In Video Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about psychology in video games because a good designer thinks about how people think.

I think about what you think And I know what you’re thinking right now. And yes, I dress myself. I picked this shirt out myself because it’s got popsicles on it.

Some of you may have missed where I talked about the fact that I have a minor in psychology. Weirdly, when I wanted to get computer science at University of Virginia, it’s part of the engineering program And if you pick computer science as your major, they require you to pick a minor and it says related minor. I would say most of the people I knew in computer science took management information systems as their minor, which was in the business school, which made me realize it could be anywhere.

item.title: 3D Printing
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about 3D printing.

It’s a fun Friday. I thought I would show you one of my hobbies which, as you will see, even though it was 3D printing, I didn’t stray very far from gaming. Now you saw some of these already in my cabinets. So if you watch the videos which I’ll link below, where I go through everything in these cabinets, you’ve already seen a few of them, but I dug up all of them because I wanted to show what I did when, a few years ago, for Christmas, Robert got me a 3D printer And I’d always wanted to play around with it and I wanted to see how they worked. So the first thing I did when I got it is I set it up, uh, strung in some — and this according to my notes — I wrote it down blue filament and I ran a test print And let me just tell you it was really loud: [Music]. It’s making something called a test spiral. Estimated completion: I hope that’s 2 minutes, but it might be 2 hours. [Music].

item.title: I'm Not A Typical Gamer
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how I’m not a typical gamer.

I know some of you are going “Duh, Tim, you’re not a typical anything”. You ever do anything and immediately regret doing it that was that. I saw an article this morning. I was browsing news sites and the article said that most players now on Steam — so the majority of players on Steam — buy games they don’t play. I was like what? And so I looked into the article and apparently there’s a lot of people out there that just collect games.

item.title: Endings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about endings.

Endings are hard. Beginnings are hard too. In fact, I have a whole video about beginnings and I’ll link it below. But this video is going to come out on my birthday. On August 25th, It’s my big 6 birthday. So of course I’m thinking a lot about beginnings and also about endings. One of the things I was thinking about was the first TV show I ever watched that had an ending that hit me really hard. And this was MASH, and I think this particular one happened in the 70s.

item.title: Game Development As A Family Car Trip
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game development.

As a family car trip, Bear with me on this. I’ve done a lot of talking about game development over the last couple years. I’ve talked about teams and all, how to organize them and how to look for good team members. I’ve talked about game directors and how to be a good one and pitfalls of being a game director. I’ve talked about design pillars. I’ve talked about, I’ve even dived in like intent, like this is people’s intent in making a game as opposed to people’s interpretation. I’ve even tried to explain how design choices are, even made at the most fundamental level.

item.title: Timelessness
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about timelessness.

I’m going to start off by talking about how and why I’ve made efforts to make this channel timeless. And then I want to talk about the extent to which I tried to make my games timeless.

It didn’t always succeed. A lot of places, a lot of elements of games, you literally can’t succeed. But you should try, just because you can’t succeed at something doesn’t mean you shouldn’t try. The actual journey is the important part, not the goal.

item.title: Planning For A Sequel
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about planning for a sequel.

Okay, sorry about that. I » got a new microphone there. My voice is normal again, It’s got voice modulation and a bunch of fun stuff. It was a birthday gift from Robert, so I I won’t do that again, but I just want to have fun. And, yes, this video is going up on my birthday because the planning for a sequel question came from a master member. So it’s getting snuck into the queue, Otherwise it would have shown up in October, Anyway, matt P asked: “A new studio is building their first RPG with a brand new IP.

item.title: To My Fellow Game Developers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to my fellow game developers.

I know this is Friday. This is not going to be my typical fun Friday video. It’s not sad, It’s just a little more serious. And also I’m not talking about the game development process. What I what I want to do is talk to my fellow game developers — developers, - about kind of mindset. I don’t want to talk about game development process. I don’t want to give you ideas for games or tips on how to organize your design or smooth out the development process.

item.title: Min Player Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about min player spec.

You may wonder what I’m talking about. This is a complicated topic. I’ve put off talking about it literally for years because it is complex and it is nuanced And I’ve thrown out those words before in previous video topics that I thought were had had that nuance to it. People still took the wrong thing, so I’m going to be very clear and careful. Also, this is a topic I don’t talk about with a lot of other game developers because it requires a certain amount of experience actually making games before you get why this is important. I don’t hear game players talk about this much at all.

item.title: Respec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Funky Town, directions 2 and subloations within.
