But oh no, today I’m talking about respec because I’m answering a question from protect one cloud, who asked: “Do you have a video on player respec, resetting talents, stats, character, investments? If not, might make a good video to go over”. Protect one cloud, You’re right, I haven’t made a video on this. I’ve mentioned it here and there in different videos, usually as an example of a feature that some people love, some people hate, or is there for balance reasons or meeting a design goal or whatever. But let me let’s talk about respec specifically and that way I can talk about all that in one spot and then point people to the one video. I think before I start, I should probably define respspec, because it means slightly different things to a lot of people. I’m talking about respec. My definition for respec is when a game lets you get back all of your skill perk attribute spell. Points, any combination of those, to respend them. Uh, some games let you change class too. I think that is a little rare and a little more extreme For purposes of this video. Think of respec as you’re allowed to rele the character that you’ve made, as if they were gaining all those levels again for the first time. Now, historically, respect didn’t used to exist.

item.title: Deleted Channel Content
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to show you some deleted content from this channel.

I’ve been doing this channel for more than two years now And, while it’s unscripted and I don’t edit anything that goes up, there has been enough deleted content, consisting mostly of failed intros that I didn’t continue with some bloopers, just some deleted content that I didn’t want to go with. And I thought you know what. This stuff never made it onto the channel until now. So enjoy. Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. Welcome to a Insomnia edition of KON Games. Today, I’m going to talk about a design I made for an MMO set in the world of Arcanum, Arcanum — Arcanum that I called Arcanum MMO. Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about my neverreleased Doctor Who game. Hi everyone, it’s me and Tim. Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim.

item.title: How To Talk To Press
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about how to talk to press.

This is something I’ve never talked about before. The closest I’ve ever gotten is I have a video, which I’ll link below, where I talk about press interview questions I tend to get, and the ones I like getting and the ones I don’t like getting. But I never talked about how to actually talk to press when you’re doing interviews about your games. And a colleague mentioned it to me and it was. He didn’t really tell me like this in the sense of you should do a video on it. He just said: you know you’re really good at talking to press. You never really mentioned that on your channel. And then he went on and talked about other things and I’m like hm, it just stuck with me and I’m like maybe I’ll talk about this, because here’s the thing and I’m talking to everyone on my channel who either makes games or wants to make games, eventually you’re. Going to talk to press about your game and no one’s going to teach you how to do it. Well, I should say usually you, will be completely untrained in it I have worked at some companies that have trained people.

item.title: Game Punishments
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game punishments, where the player does something in the game and gets punished for it.

This is from a question that someone left in the comments and I’ll read it in a second. But I could have sworn I’d done this. I was absolutely positive. I spent five or 10 minutes searching my channel so I could just link him to the video. Couldn’t find it, and that’s when I realized I think I was insane in the membrane. I’ve never done a video on game punishment.

item.title: Zen And Game Development
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Zen and game development.

I almost called this Zen and the art of game development after that book I like. But game development is not an art. I’ve talked about that. It’s art and science smooshed together. No side is trumps the other, so I just want to call it game development. Also, I’m gonna talk about Zen, but I want to point out that I’m not religious, Wasn’t raised religiously. I call myself secular, but I did take a lot of religion classes. My last year in undergraduate school I mentioned I was an engineer and I had six general education electives which I had used for things like astrophysics — you know what an engineer thinks as a hobby. But I was so interested in religion that I used several of them. I took a Greek mythology class. By the way, religious people get kind of upset when you think of mythology as religion, but deal with it I took a, comparative religion class. And then I really, really liked Buddhism, really liked Buddhism, and I took a class just on Buddhism. I have some of my books from that but most of them I’ve lost or given away over the years And then related to that. One of my favorite channels to watch is a philosophy channel.

item.title: Game Rights
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game rights, Specifically, who owns the rights to games that you’re playing and how the individuals who worked on those games, what they own and what they don’t own.

And, as usual, I want to talk to you first about why I’m doing this video, A frequently asked com question or comment that I get on this channel. I’m asked to review games and sometimes people couch it, as I just wanted you to give your thoughts on this game. It could be a game that has just come out, which is very frequent, a game that is similar to a game I’ve made in the past, or even sometimes people send me links to their own published games.

item.title: Why People Quit
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why people quit.

I recently did a video called eating your own dog food — about if you make a product, in particular, a game, you should use it, You should play it, You should eat your own dog food. In the comment section, a lot of people were asking about people who don’t eat their own dog food and, more particularly, why those people would even be in the industry or stay in the game industry if they didn’t play games. And I thought I’d address that in this video, like drill down on it, because I’ve been in the industry.

item.title: Challenges Facing An Arcanum Remaster
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about challenges facing an, Arcanum remaster.

I did a video like this — challenges facing a Fallout remaster — couple months ago. It was popular, there was a lot of comments and many of them said, “Hey, I’d rather have an Arcanum remaster” And I thought: well, I made an Arcanum remake video, which I’ll also link below. But this is a remaster. This is about: hey, let’s take the original code and just fix bugs and make it work on modern hardware and get it optimized, not let’s make a 3D version of Arcanum with lasers H, let me write down lasers. Okay, this video is probably going to be kind of long because whoever chooses to do this remaster of Arcanum, you got your work cut out for you. Also, it’s long because I can be very, very specific. I own the code. I’ve looked at the code. I looked at the code for a few hours before making this video because I wanted to make sure.

item.title: Telegraphing Consequences
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about telegraphing consequences.

This is something I’ve talked about a ton on this channel, yet I don’t have a video about it I can’t point anybody to any one place and go: “Oh, that’s where I talk about it”. In fact, uh, viewer Soon Hull asked: “You mentioned telegraphing long-term playability. How is that done And how are other? What are other things to telegraph for the player. And have you done that in the past?

item.title: Single vs. Multiplayer RPG Designs
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about getting jiggy with it or, more specifically, the application of jiggy oriented procedures to a variety of activities In particular.

Wait, actually today I’m answering a question about single versus multiplayer RPG designs. This question comes from Games by Mark Wolf, who asked: “Here’s a question for you, Tim: What are the pros and cons of solo RPGs versus multiplayer RPGs? Is there a difference? Further discussion point. My answer to the second question is that I would argue a solo RPG, if it amounts to nothing else, is an incomplete design.

item.title: Fallout Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout lore exploration.

This comes from a really good question from Joseé Luis Lopez 7511, who asked: “What part of the Fallout universe needs to be expanded upon or what story or stories deserve to be continued? Perhaps take a bit from each game and weave the evolution of the formula”. This is an interesting question. Um, I debated whether to answer it because whenever I talk about Fallout, some people go crazy. But there’s a lot of me in Fallout. I’ve said this a lot.

item.title: Anemoia
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about anamoya, which is feeling nostalgia for a time and place that you’ve never known — And yes

, I had to look that term up. You see, I watch a lot of documentaries. What would be a Tim Kaine video without a story? So I was watching a particular documentary on Roman buildings that have been continuously occupied since they were built centuries, millennia ago. And it was just really interesting to me because I — I I don’t really, you know, want to live in an old Roman building, but it was an interesting documentary And they were showing different buildings and showing what they had originally been built for and what they were be being used for now and how much of the original uh architecture was still exposed, because sometimes they had been plastered over And also some of the buildings were used for different things, but a lot of times they focused on buildings that were still being used for exactly what they had been built to be. And they happened to mention a building in Capidoshia, which, of course, made me think of this. But I suppose I should thank you. After all, it led me to my true calling, Ceil.

item.title: The Blame Problem
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the blame problem.

I have a video called the credit problem And this video is the opposite. Let’s call it a companion video, so in the credit problem which I’m link below, and I I really, really recommend you watch it. But I’m going to have to summarize some things to put things into context, for this video I establish how hard it is to give credit for a feature or anything you’re trying to do credit for. It’s not easy at all And the problem is when people ask me: “Hey, hey, Tim, who did Feature X”. It means different things to different people.

item.title: Taking Risks
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about taking risks.

I’m going to do this on a fun Friday because it’s not directly about game development, but of course all my stories are game development related because it’s all I’ve ever done. But what I’ve noticed is, especially recently, more and more people are going developers need to take more risks. Why aren’t they taking more risks? A lot of them say it, say it very flippantly, like: come on, just take a risk. Why don’t you? Why, are you guys so cautious?

item.title: Fallout's Memory Model
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout’s memory model.

I had a good time talking about the foot sliding problem in the previous video and that was kind of where programming and art came together. This talk is purely programming. It is how we addressed memory use in Fallout — Fallout one. For those of you who are using numbers, It’s a an incredibly good example of game development in the 90s. In fact, it’s such a 90s story that if you listen carefully in the background you can probably hear Germany reunifying, what you need to know as context. Well, I’m going to give a lot of context.

item.title: Fallout Foot Sliding: A Case Study in R&D
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout Footsliding, which I think is a good case study of R&D, especially in game development in the 1980s and 1990s.

I’ve talked about the Fallout foot sliding problem a lot in a number of different videos. Most recently I did it in a video about the difference between first and third person And Koala Brownie asked if I would be said: “I would be interested to know why the foot slip took so much programming time. If there’s any story to tell As a TV animator, that would seem like more of an art issue”. You’re right, and but I want to talk about it I have to give a lot of context before I dive into the exact problem, which was Thorny And I decided to make a video because people have asked about this foot sighting problem a lot, because I bring it up a lot and I wanted a video that I could point to and go here I talk about it all.

item.title: Silver Linings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about silver linings.

This is something I’ve been thinking about lately, just situations where it seemed tough. But then I found a better way of thinking about the situation. I kind of reframed it and it was a better way to approach it What made me think of this is I did a video recently — for me, but probably a while back for when this will post — about doing a hypothetical game jam. I’ll do a link below — And during that I pointed out that if I was in a game jam where I had a limited amount of time, I already know what I’m good at and plus I know a bunch of techniques. So I pointed out that if I was going to do that game jam, I would not try to do a game that had a lot of art requirements or a lot of UI, because I’m not very good at making art and I know UI is in a tremendous time sync.

item.title: Inventory UI: Grid vs. List
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about inventory UI and specifically, gridbased versus listbased user interfaces.

This comes from a question from KZA 89, who asked: “Please do a video on grid versus listbased inventory. Isn’t grid always better”. You know, I’m not going to say it’s better.

I’m going to talk about the pros and cons of grid versus list. I almost didn’t do this video — and I know, KZA, you asked this a long time ago — because I never feel like UX is my forte. In fact, I have a whole UX video where I talk about how it used to be nothing, and then it was user interface UI, then it was user experience UX. Now there are people who — that’s their title on the game is I’m the UX designer, or I’m the UX programmer, or I’m the UX artist. It and this is so anothetical to my earlier experience where there were just a bunch of generalists and one of the programmers went “Well, I guess I’ll.

item.title: Attributes Limiting Skills
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about attributes, limiting skills or, more specifically, what a.

A commenter asked a question about contradictions between attributes and skills. So let me let me give the question first and then I’ll dive into my response. The question was from Deja Vu6196, who asked: “Hi Tim, what are your thoughts on contradictions between attributes and skills? How can a player with a one intelligence reach 100 in the science skill? Should they be allowed to, or should the attribute cap the max skill they can reach”. Taking a specific example from Fallout New Vegas, the same one character who has a dumb dialogue can somehow reach speech 100 and logically talk legate Lanias into retreating, which also seems contradictory.

item.title: "Right Mistake-Making"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about right mistakemaking.

It’s a fun Friday, so I kind of wanted to dive into something that recently occurred to me while I was rereading my favorite book, Lord of Light by Roger Zilasny. I have a video about how it’s one of my most influential books and how I wanted to make an RPG out of it years ago. But what I wanted to do now is read a passage to you from page 253 of the Eastn Press edition. I noticed I have three copies of the book and they’re all have slightly different page numbers.

item.title: Playing Games As Intended
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about playing games as intended.

Sorry, I’ve been watching way too much food wishes with Chef John. I will stop talking like that, I promise I want to talk about playing games as intended. This came from a question I got and I answered it, and then I thought I need to go into more detail. So Abel Anderson asked: “Tim, I have a serious question.

item.title: Tiles, Walking, And Projection
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about tiles, walking and projection And just off the top, let me just say

this is a. I’m talking about 2D because. In 3D, you know, when everything’s polygon, you can do whatever you want. You can choose whatever camera angle you want. You can let players do different angles. I’m talking about 2D games: how you pick how you want your tiles to work, which can be both the tiles that put down the terrain and then the tiles that you walk on, Frequently different, usually different.

item.title: My Game Asset Preservation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my game asset preservation.

If you remember, a few months ago I did a video on game preservation — I’ll link it below — And in that video I talked a lot about how companies have lost things and kind of sounded — sounded mad about it In a way. I am mainly because if you take the authority to keep these things and tell other people not to — and they have no right to, then you also have to take the responsibility to keep them. And it just kind of makes me mad when, repeatedly, companies, and especially people high up at companies, take authority but not responsibility.

item.title: "Digital Stealing" And Fairness
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about digital stealing in quotes and fairness.

And I want to explain what I mean by that. But before I do, I want to give some background Love, giving context on these. I’ve made several videos that received a great deal of reaction, strong reaction to the extent that made me rethink my own beliefs. I was basically looking at my own beliefs and going: maybe I shouldn’t think it this way and, you know, was there any hypo hypocritical thinking here? Those videos were my video on capitalism, By the way, I’ll link these all below — And then I did a video on game development caution. I did two parts, part one and two. And then I did kind of two linked videos about, uh, what do gamers want, and do developers even know what gamers want?

item.title: Topic vs. Branched Based Dialogs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about topic versus branchb-based dialogue in RPGs.

This is a question that came from Simon Dah 3196, who asked: “What are the key differences between a topic-based dialogue system and a branching kind of system, especially if the goal is depth, with game state conditions and speech attributes or something along those lines — lines? This is a good question, Not only because it’s a good question, It’s something I’ I’ve never talked about, but also I’ve never even thought about talking about this before. And I should start by saying I’ve only done a topicbased dialogue system one time, and that was in Fallout, and it was hybridized with a branching system. So if you played the first Fallout, you know that there was a branch. When you clicked on NBC up popped a dialogue, a branching dialogue. But over to the side you was tell me about or ask about, there was a little box and you could type in whatever topic you wanted. So it was kind of topic based, but we didn’t display the topics to You, as you learn things in the game, you could ask NPCs about it. You could ask them about Brahmen, you could ask them about mutants, you could ask them about ghouls. So it was one of these things that as the player went along, they could just bring up different topics to NPCs to see if any one of them said something new or different, and it led to anything. And this was our way of trying to kind of hide everything to the from the player and not obviously displaying it. But you had to guess a keyword to try to open up something.

item.title: Neutral Factions
item.description:
Hello everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about neutral factions.

I can’t keep talking like that. I want to talk about neutral factions because I think they’re really interesting and it’s a fun Friday, and this is really a talk on narrative design, which I’m not an expert on, but I’m good at coming up with narrative ideas as long as I work with really good narrative designers who can then write way better than I can. And also I want to, you know, admit I know, alignment is over. All the new games coming out are like alignment is so 1980s.

item.title: Action Points, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about action points, part two.

I did a video a few weeks ago on action points, Generated a lot of really good discussion in the comment section, including this comment from Matamwit who asked: I love this topic. I would like to ask what everyone thinks about, and then there’s five topics. What One?

What advantages and disadvantages are there if we separate, split movement points and action points in two separate pools? Number two: what do you think about barring action points from the next turn? Three: what about allowing one character to sacrifice their own points for another party member to take action with? Four: what do you think about skills and spells that require more action points than you currently have available and the character has to spend multiple turns if they want to perform the action? This is a good opportunity to lock more powerful skills behind multiple turn blocks and force opponents to focus the character that is preparing a bigger scout — uh, bigger spell. And number five: higher action point pools which allow for perks and items that increase the pool by a small amount without impacting game balance.

item.title: Staying Healthy While Developing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about recent findings in fossilized endothermic theorapsids.

Now, these studies are really exciting because — oh, wait a minute, Nope, that’ll have to wait. I want to answer a question from a master member about staying healthy while developing games. This came from master member RoboCg, who literally asked this in my previous video about being happy, and he asked: “Do you have ideas about being healthy? From what I can see, a lot of my fellow game devs — mostly artists — are not having a healthy work life balance, which has caused some serious health rellated issues. Maybe you have some stories — Ergonomics might be a side theme for that. It feels like most people neglect it.

item.title: Being Happy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about being happy.

Yeah, I just want to talk about being happy. It’s a fun Friday, It’s in the middle of June, It’s Pride month. Happy Pride everybody, and yes, I will mention being gay a little bit at the end of the video. Just to warn the Pearl Clutchers — especially the people who said I talk about being gay too much, which I checked — I’ve only had three Pride videos and very few videos where I talk about being gay out of over 500.. In fact, out of 565 videos, so I think you can handle it.

item.title: The Premade Character
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pre-made character.

This comes from a question from Wes 9353, who asked: “Hey, Uncle Tim, do you have a video on the preset character? What determines the stats of the preset character and who determines the preset character”. I call these pre-made characters. Um, it’s just the term I use. I tried to do this with saying preset and I kept saying pre-made. So I finally decided I’m just going to call this the pre-made character and say why I’m not calling it preset. I don’t do this a lot, if you’ve played a lot of my RPGs, you’ll find that most of them do not have pre-made characters. I encourage people to make their own.

item.title: Industry Pendulum Swings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about industry pendulum swings.

Now, what do I mean by that? I’m not talking about Trends. I’m not talking about people going, hey, action RPGs are so hot right now. But I’m also not talking about repeated predictions, like I’m told like every five to seven years that PC gaming is dying again. And it’s not.

What I’m talking about is, over the last four and a half decades I’ve watched things come into the industry and go out of the industry and then come back into the industry and it’s odd to watch these things. And it’s not just game features, it’s Production Way the production’s done, they the way the whole industry is run. So I’m going to talk about some of those, because I kind of want to put some stuff in context so you guys can see that whenever something starts happening in the industry, you have to realize it’s happened before and it’s going to happen again, because these things just go in. And out and in and out, so and out and in and out and in and out. So let me start with one that I first noticed.

item.title: RPG Myths
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about RPG myths, and what do I mean by that?

I’m talking about lies that RPGs try to sell you, in other words, things that many — and I would even argue most — role playing games have in them, either as mechanics or just the way the story unfolds. That is not true in real life, just absolute fabrication.

item.title: Skill Trees
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about skill trees.

This is a question from every Nova, not any Nova. Every Nova who asked: “Can you please record a video about RPG skill trees? I’m interested specifically in the skill tree as a model for acquiring skills. Different types of skill trees, different point allocation models, even different shapes of skill trees as a different way to force the player into specific choices” sometimes.

item.title: Unneeded Realistic Designs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about unneeded realistic designs.

What I mean by this is I was talking to a friend who’s actually in the game industry — never worked with him. So industry distant colleague — and he asked me a very simple question. He said: “How come you’ve never really had weather in a lot of your games”. And I started saying things like: well, it wasn’t connected, I didn’t connect it to anything And we did other things for visual variation, but really full screen effects can be expensive, whether 2D or 3D, for different reasons, which I’ll talk about. And he said: well, you did time of day in a lot of your games. There was time of day in Fallout, there was time of day in Arcanum. And Arcanum one mattered.

item.title: Traditional Game Mechanics: What I Tried
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about traditional game mechanics and what I tried to do in video games based on those mechanics.

This comes from a question from sherner, who asked: I know You’ made a video game about board games that have influenced you, but I was wondering, since you mentioned reactivity, multiple solution puzzles and roleplay as defining elements of your games. As you said it yourself, all stem back to traditional ttrpg design choices, obviously D and D being our biggest reference. So what other elements of traditional gaming, IE non-digital formats such as board games, have influenced Your Design philosophy and style? Was it a conscious decision to implement them into your games or would you try to distance video games from traditional gaming? Finally, what other elements have you liked to experiment and transposed video games throughout your career, but you couldn’t figure out a way to do it?

item.title: "Eating Your Own Dog Food"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about eating your own dog food.

What do I mean by this? I did a video a while back on how much developers should play games. A lot of people thought I was talking about playing their own games, but I was talking about just playing games in general and one commenter said: no, developers should eat their own dog food, and this is a phrase I’ve heard for decades. I work with a producer who’s been saying it for forever and what it means is if you make a game, you should be playing that game. Often they mean before it ever ships. But I also think that people should play their own games after they ship, because you would be surprised at what remained unpolished or unfinished or just wrong, like a bad design or bad implementation. I’ve mentioned here that I have not won but two games that shipped an earlier build by their publisher, like I was literally holding a CD with a more recent build. That fixed bugs, in some case egregious bugs, in some case bugs that showed up in the very opening, cinematic. But publisher like, no, I’m shipping the earlier version because we got to get this thing out the door.

item.title: Pros And Cons Of Long RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pros and cons of long RPGs.

This comes from a very interesting question that I got on my game length video a couple months ago, asked by Aleandro Gambino, who asked: “Assuming that the vast majority of CRPGs are long games, in your classification some RPGs are longer than others. What are the pros and cons of a 100 plus hour RPG compared to a 30 to 40hour one in your experience? That’s a good question because they’re absolutely right. There are RPGs tend to be longer than a lot of other games, especially one full session, you know, compared to like a rogike or card games or whatever that or shoot them ups, One session can be very short in those. So I do want to talk about this, and whenever I say long in the in this video, I will mean a 100 hours or more. And anything shorter than that isn’t a long RPG, if you need a concrete example to think of. Let’s compare Arcanum and Fallout. Fallout was designed to be about 20 to 40 hours to play through and the story was three acts: get the water chip, uh.

item.title: Challenges Making A Fallout Remaster
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the challenges making of making a Fallout remaster.

I’m doing this on a fun Friday because I think it’ll be fun for you guys. It may not be fun for me because I had to think about this a lot and write a bunch of stuff down.

Now I’ve already done a video, and I’ll link it below, about REM, the remaking Fallout, or Fallout one or OG Fallout or whatever people want to refer to it is. I call the first fallout Fallout. That right there, by the way, is an issue, because people argue over what to call the first Fallout. I’ve been sitting on this video for a while because I did the one called the Remake and a lot of people were like no, that’s really, you’re talking about a remaster. So I want to be clear here.

item.title: First vs. Third Person
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about first versus third person.

And by that I mean the viewpoint you get in the games you play. And of course, I’m going to talk about role playing games here, because that’s my thing. I get asked this question a lot and I’ve answered it a lot, but always in comments. But most recently I got asked by RoboCg, who asked: “What are the pros and cons of making RPG games as isometric versus firstperson, and what features are working better or worth worse in both scenarios in your opinion”. Similarly, to turn-based combats being better in isometric.

item.title: Unpopular Game Opinions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about unpopular game opinions.

What do I mean by that? I’m talking about whether, as a gamer or a game developer, that you like certain things in your games that most people don’t seem to like, or, vice versa, you love certain things in your games that it seems a lot of people don’t like.

Let’s talk about that. I’ve already talked about why you sometimes see games with features that you personally don’t like, and it’s because — and this is hard to accept — it is because you’re in the small group — that there’s a much bigger group of people who really like those features and — trust me, I’ve been there. I tend to like certain features that aren’t the majority that people like, and I’ll talk about that. This may be something that you find hard to see. Maybe you know you don’t like this feature and none of your friends like it either, so why are they putting it in?

item.title: Gaming In The 1980s
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gaming in the 1980s.

Why do I want to talk about this? A whole bunch of reasons, but when I was doing my channel analytics a few weeks ago, I realized my biggest demographic are people between the ages of 25 and 35, which means, because it’s 2025 right now, you were all born in the 1990s, except a little sliver of you that in 2000 — let’s put that in perspective — by the end of the 9s, by 1999, I had a bachelor’s degree in computer science from an engineering school, a master’s degree in computer science specializing in Ai, and I was working on my PhD in aai, specializing in machine learning and, in particular, integrated learning. I’d also been in the game industry for almost 20 years, since 1981, and I had made several games already, including Fallout, and it’s only 1999, which is now 26 years ago. So I wanted to talk about what gaming was like in the 80s. Because it was wildly different than what it is now, and maybe by understanding some of this, or even just knowing it, you can understand some of the trends we see in gaming now. To do this, I’m going to have to probably, give contextual stories, or, for some of you, I’m going to ramble, because you have to understand gaming not only in the context of the ’ 80s, but also in context of all the other entertainment offerings in the80s, and this is going to not only talk about what the times were like, but also the social aspect of a lot of these entertainment choices and how much Focus each one got, how much time people spent in each one of these, because people have so many entertainment options now and you T talk — we’ve talked about this — all the different streaming options and all the different games. Coming out on Steam, there are so many things buying for your attention and buying for your focus that, even though you know steam is putting out, I think it we figured out was like 70 games a day.

item.title: "Dead Levels"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about cheese.

I really like cheese. Cheese is good on everything. I really want some cheese.

No wait, today we’re talking about dead levels. I want to answer a question from commenter Ender. I’m hoping answer. I’m pronouncing that right.

Ender asked: what are your thoughts on dead levels or level UPS or the equivalent, where characters get nothing at all or only the standard stuff for Levering, such as hit points, magic points, skill points, but no new spells, perks or notable character abilities? When are these dead levels wise to include, if at all, and how do they affect players, designers and World building? He goes on to say: when I said this was too complex of an this, I said this was too complex of an answer for me to leave a comment for. So I said I’d do a video. And he said: well, just to give you an example from Temple of Elemental Evil, meaning D and D 3.5, a fighter at level five is a dead level, as our most odd fighter levels. Sorcerer 2 is also mostly a dead level, since it doesn’t Grant any new spells or spell levels, class features. It’s a good question and it was very cheeky using one of my own games as an example, so I liked it, and also this question has a long enough, nuanced enough. I know I use that word a lot, but I’m basically saying I don’t like truly dead levels, but I have to explain, like where my cutof point is for me considering a level dead and of course, yeah, you can see games of mine that have dead levels. I’ll point out that Temple — and I think I’ve mentioned this before — it’s one of those games where I didn’t create the setting, the story or the system mechanics. So I did get a couple of my own ideas in there, but for the most, part that dead level was straight out of 3.5. And don’t misunderstand me, 3.5 is my favorite DN d game. I mentioned it on my D and video.

item.title: Numerical Item Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about numerical item design.

This comes from a very good question from homicidal pacifist who asked: can you talk a bit about item implementation? How do you decide what types of items go in games, especially those with more unique effects like the shrink ray from The Outer Worlds? For clarification — and I asked for clarification on this — I was thinking about the various ways that items are designed in games, from the more built crafting and grinding for 5% more fire damage approach, like Diablo, to more of your weapon type affects your play style approach, like Monster Hunter. I was wondering if you could maybe share your thoughts about the pros and cons of each for player or developer perspective.

item.title: Alternate Fallout Radiation Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about an alternate Fallout radiation design.

It’s a fun Friday. I mentioned last week in my video on my favorite shipped designs that I had an alternate radiation design for Fallout, but because it never — I never shipped that — it’s not in that list. It also wasn’t rejected.

In fact, this is something I made years later, because, full disclosure, I was never 100% satisfied with how we did radiation in Fallout. It was just damage, um, but later, fallouts — I was also not 100% satisfied. It became a DOT or a Max Health loss or something that was kind of like poison. I didn’t like any of this, so years later — and I’m not 100% sure when, because it’s not dated — it was after I left interplay. It might have even been after fall three came out. I don’t know. It was on loose leaf, undated paper — I came up with a different design that I would have loved to have used, so I thought I’d talk.

item.title: Recognizing Good Coworkers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about recognizing good co-workers — and by good I mean good for working with, for you.

And I wanted to talk about this because making games is very stressful. I’m sure that’s come out in my videos and you see all kinds of behaviors from people when they’re stressed. And I wanted to talk about that because, if you’re lucky, you can find some people that you really love working with and that together you make really good games with, and you can stick with those people for decades I have. Now, when people get stressed, there’s some people that fall into one of two camps: either they just start saying yes to everything, they’re just they’re yes, men, that’s a great idea, Tim. That’s a great idea. Let’s do it, and they’re doing this because they just want to avoid argument. They just tell me what to do, I’ll do it.

item.title: Game Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game time, and by that I mean timed quests, and even more than just timed quests, and also the concept of time in a game.

This comes from a question from fanda 6122, who asked: have you done a video on clocks and in-game time? Yet many modern crpgs don’t force your hand or have any sense of urgency, even the ones that do Overland travel. Well, good question. It raises a lot of — uh, bigger issues than just putting in timed quests, so I kind of wanted to start with that and then get to the bigger issues of time in game. So let’s start with time quests. We did this in Fallout. You know you had to go get the water chip, um, or game over.

item.title: My Favorite Shipped Designs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my favorite shipped designs.

It’s a fun Friday. I want to answer a question from ToastManDennis1218, who asked: could you please make the same format video with your ideas that were accepted, made it into your games and you are happy they did? This was on my video of my ideas that got rejected, which I link below. By the way, this video has a lot of videos in it, and I’ll link them all below. Um, I won’t always be able to give all the context. This might be a long video. I won’t always be able to give all the context for all the design ideas I’ve worked on, but there’s a ton of videos associated with this video that will give you a lot of background on them. So before I dive into my list, though, of things I designed that went into my game that I really liked, I’m going to limit it in two ways, because what would be a Tim?

item.title: "Impossible" Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about impossible ideas.

I am going to talk about what I mean by impossible at some point in the video and what it probably really means, but I think it’d be more fun for me to talk about examples of that before I say let’s dig into impossible. But this is a question from Fear the clown who, in my video for Unique roles for players video, which I’ll link below, asked: the idea of a boss having its own save game is wild. Can you go into more detail about your thought process for that boss fight?

item.title: NPC Reaction Pros And Cons
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC reaction pros and cons.

This comes from a question from badim pakkin, who said: Tim, I was so hoping you could talk about the broader philosophy behind implementing or not implementing NPC triggers, SL reactions. I think that would be even more interesting than just specific implementation examples. He was asking that on a video I did on NPC’s triggering each other and I was giving a lot of examples, and this is certainly something I can talk about and I will. But funnily enough, if I don’t give a lot of examples in these videos, I frequently get comments from people saying: could you give me examples of that could you talk about in your game or in another game where you see something like the mechanic you’re talking about? So that’s why I give a lot of examples, but this is different. This is going to be the philosophy of why you would do it.

item.title: Channel Second Anniversary!
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about this Channel’s second anniversary.

It’s a fun Friday, it’s April 25th- 2025, and this Sunday, April 27th, is the 2year anniversary of this channel. I think I’ve mentioned. I thought I’d start it, do a few dozen videos, have a few hundred people watch them and be done. That hasn’t happened, so let’s walk through that, because it’s very surprising.

item.title: Game Preservation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game preservation.

I got a really good question about this and I have never talked about it, and it’s an important topic. It’s one of those things that I’ve said before. It didn’t occur to me to talk about it until someone asked a question, so I’m glad people are asking good questions. This comes from netherly. Who asked: I’ve got a question for you. Okay, three questions, but they’re all related to the same thing: games preservation. So here go.

item.title: Real-Time Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about realtime Fallout.

Gkid 100 asked: can you make a video about what Fallout would have been like if you did make it realtime action? What would have been needed to come out of the game? What about the balance of the perks would have changed? Do you think multiplayer could have worked in the Fallout Universe? I’ve talked about remaking Fallout and also what would change today in two separate videos and I’ll link them below. So I just want to make it clear off the top that I would not have made Fallout real time, not back in 97, not today, in 2025, and this is despite — and I’ve mentioned this — interplay marketing approached me in 1996, a year before we came out, and they wanted Fallout to be me made real time because of Diablo. This they pushed several times and the way I finally got it — the stop — was just pointing out how much money I would need and time, and then they finally.

item.title: A Day In The Life Of Moon Man
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a day in the life of moonman.

It’s fun. Friday I was digging through some older photos I had that were industry related, and I have a whole Trove of obsidian ones, and there was one I found that was called day in the life of moonman and it was something I did back in 2019 and I think I sent it out to the team. Just did it for fun, being goofy, so I thought I’d share it here. So let’s get started. In [Music] 2019.

item.title: Action Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about action points.

This is an interesting question. It comes from tact DB who asks: where do you draw the line and how granular the action point should be for one’s game — AP resolution, if you will. Some RPGs use a base 100 AP for every character with equipment and status as MiFi modifiers. Everything you do, from opening your backpack to raising your weapon costs AP. Some actions even activate an AP drain, like moving with your Shield up costs more than just having it default.

item.title: Compromising For The Medium
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about compromising for the medium and basically

this is a talk about how you can’t have everything you want in your video game, because it’s a video game. The nature of video gaming prevent or should very much discourage you from trying to do certain things, and I want to talk about that because it falls under the bigger categories. You can’t have everything you want, now I’m not talking about the compromises that you may have to introduce because you’re working in a team and other people have different ideas and different things they want to try. I have a whole working in teams video I’ll link below. I’m not talking about compromise because you or the team members don’t have the skills needed to implement the design ideas that you’ve created. I have a whole video about matching projects to teams.

item.title: CounterBite: Bloodlines Multiplayer Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about counter bite, the Vampire: The Masquerade — Bloodlines multiplayer mode that we had suggested, that we had designed but never implemented for Vampire: The Masquerade — Bloodlines.

I’ve talked about it here and there in other videos. A brief recap — and remember I was not on Bloodlines team for the first two years — a brief recap — is part of the original design for Bloodlines that we were doing for Activision using valves engine from halflife was a multiplayer mode. It was written down, it was designed, but at some point when Activision was concerned about that — the single player game was really big and they wanted us to focus on it. They took multiplayer mode away from troa and gave it to another team — I don’t know if it was an in-house Activision team or another out of housee team. They made a whole new design, didn’t get very far with it I don’t know what happened. I don’t know if it was they weren’t working out or Activision just didn’t want to spend the. Money, but that whole thing got cancelled and bloodline shipped as a single player game. I did finally find our design document for what I always call counter Bite — part of the reason I had trouble finding it wasn’t called counter bite, it just says multiplayer. It’s chapter nine of the Vampire: The Masquerade — Bloodlines design document.

item.title: Player Power From Build vs. Items
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about player power versus from builds versus from items.

This is a question from Anubis 1101 who, as a super mutant, got a priority video. So I stopped what I was doing, which was organizing my collection of amusing socks and underwear, to talk about this. So let’s dive into it. Anubis1 asked — it’s very long, Tim.

Are you familiar with the myth of the gun versus the myth of the sword? It’s an ideological approach to fiction storytelling that I really love because it meshes well with game design. In essence, it describes where the character or player’s power comes from. The myth of the sword is when the character’s power comes from their own skill or stats. It emphasizes that power comes from within. Most fantasy settings lean heavily on this. The myth of the gun places the source of skills and abilities in the equipment, emphasizing finding and creating better gear.

item.title: Does More Dev Time Mean A Better Game?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about does more Dev time mean a better game?

This is a question from T4 nu1, who asked: hey, Tim, there’s a been a myth going around for ages that more development time equals a better product. Is that myth true? Also, what can’t be fixed with more development time? Ah yes, this ancient myth about more development time. I believe it’s from Mesopotamia 6 or 8 thousand years ago.

item.title: My Bookshelves
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to talk about these bookshelves.

It’s fun. Friday, it’s daylight, I’m still snowed in. Let’s do this.

The latest person to ask about this was blood mime, who asked: hi, Tim, my partner is interested in a Showcase of the items and books on your black shelves. She has a large collection of books, but, based on what we’re able to see, you have an interesting assortment of other items as well.

item.title: Sci-Fi vs. High Fantasy Settings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about sci-fi versus high F fantasy settings, specifically in a new IP.

This comes from a question from Hungry Hungry Hobo 196, who asked: hey, Tim, if you had your choice to make a new crpg, would you rather do a high fantasy or sci-fi? How do you feel about sci-fi with fantasy elements such as magic, and how hard is it to make a game that has guns or lasers and make a sword feel viable? Those are good questions and once again I find myself going: haven’t I already talked about that and I looked, and I haven’t talked about it in one of my videos, but this is a perfect example of the kind of discussions I’ve had with many other game developers. We talk about things like this all the time, and what’s fun about talking with your fellow game developers is you’re not trying to con like someone, like a publisher or someone above you, to make a game this could be like. A lunchtime thing where you’re just sitting around talking and you’re like, yeah, you know what I prefer between these two and here’s why. And then there’s a spirited discussion of the topic and no one’s necessarily trying to convince anyone, you’re just trying to go. Well, here’s what I think blah. I love discussions like that because it helps me kind of hash out my own feelings on the topic.

item.title: Hypothetical Game Jam
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a hypothetical game, Jam.

What’s happened? A master member on the channel asked me a question, so I had to stop what I was doing to answer it right in the middle of the afternoon. I was, I was doing some work and I said no, I got to stop.

Super duper asked: can you imagine you’re in a game Jam? You’ve got 48 Hours. What game are you making? What’s the story setting and mechanics? How do you keep your workload light while making a robust game? And just to complete the image, the theme of the game Jam is your hobbies, so it has to be a game about something you love, like chocolate or baking, or game design or whatever.

item.title: Why I Wanted Luck In Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I wanted luck in Fallout.

It’s for me early February, I’ve been snowed in for a few days and this is a fun Friday video and I figured why not? If you remember, I talked about when Fallout had to be converted from gerps to special. Chris Taylor had a home brew system he wanted to use, which we later added perks to when Brian Fargo said: hey, I want something for people to buy, besid skills, but the attributes were just strength, perception, endurance, Charisma, intelligence and Agility. There was no luck I I talk about this in the how we made Fallout special vid. I really, really wanted luck to be in there because I had had a ton of fun with luck based characters in gerps, especially GP supers, and in Champions and in a few other tabletop role playing games that had luck in them. Um, if you watch my interplay after hours video, I talk about a character I had called lucky boy who bought all the different luck, uh, advantages or powers that were in the tabletop I was using and was just insanely fun to play. It wasn’t that he was good at anything, it’s just he frequently got lucky and scored critical hits or Critical with the skill and then anyone who tried to attack him frequently messed up and hurt themselves and other people around them. It was fun, because of that. I wanted in Fallout, I wanted and something like that. First of all, I wanted it as an attribute, because I remember when I brought it up people were like, well, we’re just throw in perk, a luck perk and I’m like, no, as a perk that means some people won’t have it. So when you go, what’s the luck on them?

item.title: How Much Devs Should Play
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how much devs should play.

This is kind of a continuation of a video I made a month ago — month ago, almost a year ago — called playing games, and I’ll link it below. But it’s me answering a question from viewer Amir husin magudi 388, who asked: what is your opinion on keeping a clean pallet?

As in your working on something, don’t look at what other people are doing. If you do look at what other people are doing, it might narrow your creativity. If you do look at what they’re doing, you may learn things you never come up with. I guess this also goes for playing a game blind. Guess what.

item.title: Game Length
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game length in particular.

I want to answer a question from bone Bard 6178, who asked: it feels like a lot of people put value on Long games with a lot of content, while I avoid almost anything these days longer than 10 hours. Curious how you feel about the benefits and drawbacks of long and short games. That’s a very good question. I know I I don’t. I know I haven’t talked about this, I know, but various videos of mine I may mention um trying to reach a certain number of hours, because it’s something that used to be more frequently asked for, and I’ll talk about that the way I thought I’d structure this.

item.title: Games And Theater
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about video games and the theater.

This may sound like a weird thing, but I got a bunch of stories to tell. It’ll make sense and there’s even some stuff we can learn from it. But let’s just take a look at the video game business and theater business and look at how, at the very top, they’re very similar. Both are groups of people which are a mix of creatives — you know whether it’s designers and you know artists, vers versus. You know actors and set designers and a lot of technical people, whether it’s programmers or people who do lighting or people who just know how to manage, uh, stage business. Both groups require a lot of discipline and are really helped if they have a good director.

item.title: Arcanum Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. I want to talk to you today about Arcanum lore exploration.

What I mean by that is kind of a deep dive into some of the Arcanum lore that interests me. Now I did a video already on the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum, and there’s a video about it. I’ll link it below, and in that there was this idea for combining magic and tech. There was an ore that let you make Tech ological items that you could magically imbue, and I didn’t like it then. And now, 25 years later, I absolutely hate the idea, and the reason for that is I think it takes a fundamental pillar of the Arcanum setting, which is Magic and Tech are diametrically opposed, not because they don’t like each other, not because they don’t get along and you need to find, you know, a substrate to get them, you know, happy to be together because they work diametrically oppositely.

item.title: Botched Quests And Player Freedom
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about botched quests and player Freedom.

This comes from a question from Nick Berg, one who asked: can you share your ideas on botched, failed quests? Don’t these limit the freedom of making your own story and RPGs? I actually think it expands your freedom. So I think that’s a good question and I want to explain my answer. I think Nick is referring to a video I did on Quest implementation and I will link it below.

item.title: The Ultimate RPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the ultimate RPG.

Now, this is a fun Friday video, so what I want you to take from that is: I don’t know how to make this RPG, but I can certainly point you in the direction of this RPG. Hopefully one of you out there will find It’s a very simple question, I think, to answer, but way more difficult to execute. Basically, how can you make the RPG? It’s simple: make the nonviolence options in that RPG just as rich and fun as the violent ones and I think you have the ultimate RPG.

item.title: Collecting Feedback
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about collecting feedback, specifically how and when and what to do with the feedback that you get on the game you’re making.

So this is obviously aimed at game developers, but I think I thought a lot of people would like hearing about this. So here we go. I’m going to break this into feedback you get before you ship and feedback you get after you ship, so before you ship, they’re basically three sources of feedback. You can get feedback from the team itself. People will be playing the game and be working in different parts of the game and they’re starting to give you feedback saying, hey, I don’t think this is working.

item.title: Character Build Fairness
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about character build fairness.

Now let me give you some context. I got a question from a viewer, JP valheim, who asked about fairness in video games and I replied that I wasn’t sure what that meant in the context of mostly what I’ve made, which are single player RPGs. And I said, outside of accessibility features and making sure that any character build that the player makes can finish the game — that I didn’t really have an answer and he replied, and I quote: you mentioned single player games and the fairness of any build being able to complete the game. Could you elaborate on why exactly it is so bad if a certain build cannot complete the game? I would argue that it’s not the build not being able to complete the game. That’s the problem.

item.title: Having Fun With AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about having fun with AI.

I’ve talked before about how I view AI as being a tool and, yes, there’s concerns about how it’s going to affect the market. But I have never been able to do art, so I started playing around with meta AI. It’s the AI That’s part of your Facebook Messenger. There’s a tab in it you can click. I’d never use it. A friend told me about it and apparently it lets you can upload a few photos of yourself and let, and it can scan your face in 3D and then it will let you generate images just of yourself, and I thought, well, this could be a lot of fun. You know, as someone who’s never been able to get a single piece of artwork into any of his games, not even a mouse cursor — I wanted to try this and, as you will see, I’m going to show you images I created. It started mundane and simple, and then, because of the way my brain works, it got weird. So let’s take a look at it. This is a fun Friday. This is just for fun.

item.title: Onboarding New Team Members
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about onboarding new team members.

This is from a question from pooa 8730 who asked looking at Team growth. In those photos they were talking about a video I posted of uh development during Fallout. I’ll put — I’ll link it below: how difficult was it to onboard new people? You need to get new people up to speed as fast as possible and they clearly have no knowledge of existing implemented things, especially in Game Dev where deadlines are super tight. Interesting question. This is another one of those questions where I never would have thought to do a video on this until someone asked, because getting people up to speed is just part of what you always did and so, like a fish surrounded by water, it didn’t occur to me that I was wet. But I do want to say this — and this isn’t uh directed to the person who made the comment, but just something I’ve noticed is onboarding. When I hear that term, it always makes me smile a little bit because it’s one of those terms that have snuck into game development from other places and it makes me realize how much influence is coming in from these places. We never used to use onboarding as a term. In fact, I don’t even think I heard that term before 10 years ago, and I certainly didn’t hear it frequently until about 5 years ago — and suddenly everything’s about or onboarding.

item.title: Event Systems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Event Systems.

These are some that I mentioned a while back in a video on NPC reactions, which I’ll link below, and last Friday I even mentioned NPCs triggering off each other, which talks about Event Systems as well, while the first video LED Lemmings 19 to ask: would you be interested in doing a video focused on events, how they’re triggered, how they’re handled, how they work, exist in single player versus multiplayer games like wild star, caveats, issues, problems, technical or otherwise, and whatever else you think is worth talking about? Sure, this is going to be probably aimed at programmers. I’m not going to give you implementation details, but any programmer who’s had more than one data structures class will know what I’m talking about when I’m saying: hey, you need to make a list of events. Maybe you want an array, maybe you want a link list, maybe you want a doubly link list, maybe you want a stack and you probably don’t want to. Stack anyway. You might want to Hash anyway.

item.title: NPCs Triggering Each Other
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC’s triggering each other.

This is in reaction to well a commenter, odesia hh2 TD, saw my NPC reaction video, which I’ll link below, and asked any ideas on how to expand this to make NPC’s trigger reactions between themselves and thus create emergent Behavior. I left a short comment and they replied. I guess the Magic in this case would be picking good triggers that facilitate the emergence of fun events and interactions. So an idea for a fun Friday: brainstorming cool triggers and interaction ideas. That’s this fun Friday, so let’s do it.

item.title: Having Unique Rules For Players
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about having unique rules for players, and

this is me answering a question from shy robot, who asked: in many RPGs it feels like the player is governed by different rules than the rest of the world and its NPCs. What game systems and design processes, do you think really close this division? Let me just say, shy robot, I know what you’re talking about and it is an interesting question because not everybody agrees on even what you mean by the roles and what players can have and NPCs can have, and whether they even agree that players shouldn’t have unique roles. So let’s dive into that first of all. I know what you mean.

item.title: My Ideas That Got Rejected
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my ideas that got rejected from my own games, and I’m going to start all the way back with Fallout, which was now what?

Almost 30 years ago that that project got started and I was Project Lead or design director or game director on a lot of these titles and my own ideas didn’t make it in. So I’ve done a lot of videos where I talk about learning from failure and I talk about how you can learn from other people’s failures. So learn from mine, learn from ideas I wanted to put into games that got thrown out. Let’s start with Fallout.

item.title: Arcanum Backgrounds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about Arcanum backgrounds and I will talk a little bit about them.

I want to give you some context, but then I will answer this question from Feliciano frontado, 3134, who asked: fun Friday suggestion: what were your favorite backgrounds in Arcanum? This is fun to go look at because I loved backgrounds in Arcanum and in fact I kind of want give you a little background on backgrounds. See what I did there. It wasn’t funny.

item.title: When Your Game Doesn’t Sell
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about what you should do when your game doesn’t sell.

All been there? Well, I’ve been there. I did a whole video, in fact, on data about my games, talking about their sales figures and how it doesn’t always seem to correlate with critical reviews or team size or budget or any of that. The video is linked Below in the description: — description.

item.title: Damage Types
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about damage types.

I honestly thought that I had a video on damage types. I didn’t, but I sure have talked about them a lot. I recently did a video on localized damage systems — I think it was last week, um — where I talked about damage types and even mentioned like Elemental damage. I didn’t really talk about what those were, and this led Juma Fallout to ask Tim, following up on the lineup thought: how do you handle different damage types? Always comes to mind Fallout with specific armors, like the Tesla armor or the EMP damage that only did Splash damage to the PC.

item.title: Learning Design From Travel
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about learning design from travel.

May seem weird that I’m saying I learned game design lessons from traveling around the world, but I did, and this is one of a series of videos I’ve slid in to the channel. I’ve talked about learning design from failure, from board games, from Christmas gift exchanges, from sitcoms, from collecting fragrances, from other non-game disciplines, even lessons I learned from a bad teacher. Basically, what you should walk away from is you can learn game design from just about anything. So don’t restrict yourself like I did for years, because, let me tell you, I used to be a bad traveler, or, more specifically, I used to think that I hated traveling. Part of this, I think, came from my family, and not deliberately. My family went everywhere by car. We, every Easter, we went to Ohio to spend with my aunt. We took a couple trips to Disney World, which, if you don’t know, Orlando, is about 12 hours by car from Washington DC. I grew up right outside DC. That is a long trip, especially for loading a bunch of kids into a station wagon, and especially if you and some of the other kids get car sick. It was just not pleasant for me to the point where I actually said I didn’t want to go to Disney World the second time because I just didn’t want to go through that, and I basically was. I went against my will.

item.title: Status Effect Implementation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about status effect implementation.

Why am I talking about this? Somebody recently said, hey, I really liked your quest implementation video and I’m like I haven’t really done like a kind of a more nitty-gritty implementation video in a while. So let me talk about this.

Status effects are what I call any effect that’s placed on an object in the world. It’s usually a creature, like you know, you may throw a DOT on a creature, so every now then they have fire on them and every now and then they take fire damage, but not always. You might throw a spell on a door that says this door is being held, you know, like in place, like a, like a hold portal spell from DND. Or you may summon a weapon, or you may summon a zombie, and all those are what I call status effects, because they place an effect in the world that usually has a particular running time and then they cleans it up.

item.title: Localized Damage Systems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about localized damage systems.

What do I mean by that comes from a question from viewer Kan and Abel. Okay, putting Kane in there gives you one eyebrow rais, but the fact that you spelled AEL, AI, b, l gives you both eyebrows raised anyway. Kan and Abel asked: can you do a video on the pros and cons of localized damage? Like body parts have their own health pull and if, for instance, your leg is too injured, you can’t run or have a hard time walking. First of all, I chose this because this is a really fun topic to talk about, like it just opens up so many things when you’re talking about something so basic in a role playing game — and I’m going to talk about, in the context of a role playing game, something so basic as getting damaged. And I want to tell you, localized damage is way more than just talking about aim shots and crippling and stuff like that. In fact, it’s more than even having localized Health pools, because before I even talk about that, let’s talk about hit points for a second. Hit points are great.

item.title: Learning From Steam Data
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about learning from Steam data.

This is something that I saw — a video that, from my point of view, just came out a couple days ago, but I’m filming this in December, so from your point of view, it’s been a couple months. This video is awesome. It’s linked below. I highly recommend that you watch it. I’m going to repeat that s times because I think it’s super important to watch it. Also, I want to say that people who do videos like this one, that’s actual data and not, you know, someone’s reaction video or someone having the outrage of the week — these are the people I love. So, newbie Indie Game Dev, you’re my new favorite person.

item.title: Adapting TTRPGs vs. Other IPs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adapting tabletop role playing games into video games versus adapting a non-game IP into a video game.

I think the into the video game part should probably be implicit, because I’ve never adapted anything into anything else but a video game. So let’s talk about the pros and cons of having a tabletop RPG versus. Is a non-game RPG a non-game IP? This is an answer to a question from Dr John wubber, who asked: I’m curious, since you did adaptations of d and d and vampire, the Masquerade, but also South Park, what kinds of challenges did you find making a game based on another type of game versus making a game based on something non — interactive like a TV show? That is a really interesting question and no one has ever asked that before. It’s, by the way, that’s if you’re wondering, like what you can do. That’s a John’s question — is really interesting to me because it’s like: oh, I’ve literally done a video.

item.title: More Thoughts On Game Marketing
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give more thoughts on game marketing and look, I exist in daytime.

Thought I’d make a change. Now, I should point out, I’ve already done a marketing video, which I’ll link below, but recently I was looking at it and I realized I did not follow my own rules on how to give constructive feedback, which I did in a video which I’ll also link below, where I said: if you’re going to give comments and critique on something, make it constructive, make it actionable. And I realized in that marketing video when I talked about that I’ve worked with marketers that I liked and marketers I didn’t like. I never really gave them constructive feedback. Now, nobody on the video who watched the video said: hey, Tim, you’re not following your own rules.

item.title: Dynamic Quest Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Dynamic Quest Design.

This is a question from Anubis 111, who asked on my tabletop roleplaying vid. I would love to hear how you’d approach a radiant Quest system. You’ve mentioned it in passing, but your games have never made big use of them. How do you feel about them in general, and if you had to implement such a system, how would you bring it in line with Tim’s style of game design?

item.title: Showcasing Your Work
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about showcasing your work.

I’ve talked on this channel about making games, making demos for your games, making game design portfolios to try to get a job, but I’ve never really talked about how, exactly what they you, how you should put them together and what you should decide on to show in them. In fact, sh kovich Dev, during my game design portfolio video, left this comment: this is a good video, but I think what most people struggle with is how to effectively showcase all those things that you laid out. Would you do a video about it?

item.title: Writing My Own TTRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing my own TBL toop roleplaying game.

I’m actually answering a question from all Lucky Seven, who asked: would you like to write a pen and paper RPG? If so, considering your knowledge of computer game design, how would you approach it? What do you think you would make differently from someone who’s never done a crpg?

That’s an interesting question I’ve had. I’ve seen other people ask me like, hey, why don’t you make a tabletop RPG or make a pen and paper RPG? And I’m always like, eh, but this question made me think, oh yeah, making computer RPGs is kind of a different design space than tabletop, and that is an interesting question to me. So let me answer the base question right off the bat, which is, yes, I have thought off and on, but never seriously, about making a tabletop RPG. I think that, having made a bunch of computer RPGs and they’re different, and I’ll talk about that — I think I would have, based on that experience, quite a number of advantages and a few disadvantages if I tried to make a pen and P, pen and pencil, paper and pencil RPG.

item.title: Raising Cain
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell you the story of Raising Cane, or how I went from being a baby to the gorgeous super hunk you see before you today.

Hold all comments till the end. I see you leaning forward, wanting to type. Just stop, okay.

I’ve mentioned that I have thousands of family photos digitized. On more than one occasion someone in my family has contacted me and said: hey, can you send me all the pictures of Grandma Kaine or can you send me all the pictures of this cousin? And I’ve gone through all thousands of photos looking for the ones with those people in them. So eventually, one day, I realized that storage of these photos was Tiny. They’re digitized photos that are often, you know, tiny little, you know snapshots. So what I did was I made folders for all of my siblings, for my parents, for my grandparents, for cousins and I for any photo. I would make as a as many copies of it as there. Were people in the photo and then I put those f photos into all the folders for those people. So whenever asks me, hey, can you show me pictures of your sister, I have all of them in there, right there. These are all the photos I have. Because of that I took my own photos and because I knew what age I was in them, I made them Chron olical. How lucky for you, because now I’m going to show you pictures of me from my pupil beginnings of a year, age zero, all the way up to when I graduated from high school, and you can see where I came from.

item.title: Deckbuilder RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about deck builder RPGs.

Those are role playing games where your abilities, or sometimes even more than that, are given to you randomly from a shuffle deck. This is not my area of expertise, but wami Bonito 2558 asked: would you have any opinions on carddeck Builder based art pgs, where character abilities are represented by cards and are also not guaranteed to play out the same way every combat, since in some fights you might never draw a particular card? Yes, I will tell you something. My response — you guessed it — is nuanced. I originally wrote a short response to this comment, but it wasn’t expressing the way I felt and I Al also thought that in writing it looked negative, which I’ve been told. Sometimes emails, my Memoir, comes across as negative and then when I say it, people are like: oh, I like it’s not negative at all. So I figured I’d do that with my answer to this. So, deck builder RPGs. Let me tell you why I like them. I haven’t played that many, but I will tell you why I like them and also why I like the — the whole idea behind that mechanic.

item.title: A World Building Tip
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give you a World building tip and of course, before I do it, I kind of want to give you context and then I’ll give you the tip, which is a one sentence tip, and then I’ll talk about it at length.

So I’ve already talked in other videos about making settings. I have a whole video about making a good one, and it pretty much consists of questions you should be asking yourself as you put together your setting. I’ve got another video on a technique to make a setting, which I call my what if? Method. Which is You think of something that’s very grounded in reality, but then you say: what if I change this one thing?

item.title: The Million XP Dungeon
item.description:
