Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the million experience Point dungeon, or, based on how I’m dressed, this could also be called The Old Man and the C++- C++.

Anyway, the million XP dungeon is something I heard about over three decades ago. I think I heard about it in 1993. I was either at Gateway or orcon up in Los Angeles. Those were gaming conventions.

item.title: Code Refactoring
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about code refactoring.

B 892 asked in the games industry: how do you handle code refractors? Is there time for it? How do you approach it?

Are there dedicated time periods dedicated to refactoring or is it done in small bits throughout development? B? That’s a really good question.

I thought I did something, but when I looked, all I did is kind of mention code refractor when I did a video on optimization, which I’ll link below. But code refactoring is done for more than just optimization reasons, so let me get into it. First of all, what is code refactoring? That’s whenever you restructure existing code, so code that’s already written and works, to make it better — and I’ll talk about what better is. But usually it means you’re rewriting it because you want it to be cleaner and faster. It’s usually bug free before you start, but I’ve had some code refactors because there was a pernicious bug that I literally could not find and I refactored the code and it disappeared. So so let’s talk about why you would want to refactor code.

item.title: Do Devs Know What Gamers Want?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about do devs know what Gamers want.

This video is a direct followup to my video last week: do Gamers know what they like? Before I made that video I just mentioned, I was going to do it in a comment section almost two months ago and L Noir said personally, I think, do devs know what Gamers want? And is even a better question, and you know what they’re right. So both that video — so I made that video — and then I made this one pretty soon afterwards. Keep in mind these were made back in November, not even that late November, kind of mid November.

item.title: Shadowdark
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Shadow dark.

It is the new thing I’m into. It’s Friday. I figured I’d do a fun Friday about it. Let me tell you why I like it so much.

First of all, there’s been this osr trend. I think it stands for old school rules, but people argue what it stands for. I don’t care.

It’s things like Dungeon Crawl Classics, which I do have a few of. But what I love about o the osr trend is it’s starting to feel like people are looking towards the old days of how tabletop RPGs, RPGs used to be. And for everyone there’s a different reason. Some people like the rules, some people like the settings, some people like, um, just some of the more, some of the Simplicity of it. For whatever reason, I like it CU. For me it’s very nostalgic and when I was looking into osr, I mean I I got the Dungeon Crawl Classics right away. But looking into it, led me to Shadow dark.

item.title: Do Gamers Know What They Like?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the question: do game players even know what they like?

I almost called this game players don’t know what they like, but I figured it would lead to a better comment section if I posed it as a question. Let me tell you why I thought of this.

I was watching a comedian podcast, not a comedy podcast. I wasn’t watching comedians do stand up. I was talking about comedians talk with each other about being Comedians and one of them made a comment that it’s very hard to come up with jokes in a world where audience only know what they find unfunny and most of them Couldn’t Write a joke if their lives depended on it, especially a joke that you could say in standup. And I thought that was interesting because, first of all, I’ve met a lot of people who think they’re funny and they are not funny. But also, standup comedy is a perfect example of it, doesn’t require any equipment or anything.

item.title: NPC Reactions
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC reaction.

This comes from a question from AliceLittle1865, who asked: can you make a video, maybe on the topic of NPCs and companions? What I mean is how to create character reactions when they interact with the world or not, for example, how all the companions in New Vegas have some kind of reaction when on a mission or part of the map or player choice or faction. The same could be applied to a tabletop RPG. When the DM creates a character that the players have in their party or not, how would that character react to the interactions and development of the story and how do you prepare for that? I want to break this into two parts because the, at least for a video game, the complicated thing is how to set it up so you can do the reaction, and then I’ll talk very briefly on what things you might want to react to. Also, I call this NPC reaction. It is: commonly used for companion reaction, since companions are with you, are following you around and they see a lot of what you do. But I called this NPC reaction because you can really use it for anything in any character in the game who’s not the player. We’ve all played games where the NPCs react to things you do and say: my games all do that. To me that’s one of the just wonderful beautiful things about an RPG is reacting to what the player does and a big chunk of that reaction comes from having non-player characters say: I saw what you did, I saw what you said.

item.title: Photos From Fallout Development (1994-1997)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a collection of Fallout photos I have from 1994 through 1997.

It’s a fun Friday. I dug these up. I figured I’d show them here now. I’ve done two videos with photos on them. One was scenes from a life in game development.

The other was snapshot throughout my career and they’re below. I also did a Fallout timeline. I’ll link that below.

item.title: Violence As The Default In AAA RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about violence as the as the default in AAA RPGs.

It’s a very specific title because I mean all of this. I’m talking about violence. I’m talking about games that have violence as the default thing. That’s what they expect you to do, and I’m only talking about AAA RPGs because this came from a very good, thoughtful and well-written question by saber vs7, who asked: hi, Tim, I’m curious as to if you think games, while practically speaking, the RPG genre, specifically at the mainstream budget level — I know there’s plenty of Indies who are trying to be the change you want to see in this regard — will be able to evolve past the Paradigm of violence being the default way in which the player interacts with the world. Just so you know. This went on for a long time.

item.title: Changes 2024
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about changes — well, changes as of 2024.

If I’m figuring this out correctly, because I do these early, this particular video will go up on at the very last day of 2024, so it’s New Year’s Eve. There were a lot of changes this year: political ones, social ones, on cultural ones. A lot of people want these changes to stop. They want things to go back to how they were growing up. I was used to hearing this from older people. Now I’m hearing it from younger people.

item.title: Mentoring
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about mentoring.

Request for me to Mentor someone is probably the most frequent topic of emails and DMs. I get about this Channel and I turn them all down and I will tell you why later. But I totally understand the impulse to ask someone to give you advice, uh, to warn you of some of the pitfalls coming up, and I get it I I mentioned, I’ve mentioned several times on this channel that I wished IID had a mentor, both professionally and personally. When I was younger I really could have benefited from having someone who was older and wiser and more experienced to just tell me how some things work in life, in business, in game development. It all would have helped me a great deal and, interestingly, I think I was open to it I look back at my early projects and how they would have been better and I, toally, would have been open for someone to give me that advice. But my first project, when I worked at Pegasus software when I was 16 years old, later turned into cybron. I was 16 and everyone else who worked there — there were a few people in their 20s, most people were in their 30s and I think there was even one or two people in their 40s.

item.title: WizWar: A Study In Great Board Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about wiiz war, a study in great board game design.

I’m going to talk about this game. This is my copy of wiiz War. It’s the 1991 Edition, there were a lot of different editions out there and I want to talk about this because I think there’s a lot of good lessons you could learn about making a board game from this little teeny board game it’s. I’ve talked about playing this game before. It was in a video about after hours game playing at interplay. I will link it below.

item.title: The Cost Of Moddability
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the cost of modability, specifically the cost of making your video game modable by end users.

And this is a really good topic and it came from a really good question by pelicano 1987 who asked: how much in time money does it cost to make a game modable? Usually, players just think that releasing the dev tools is enough, but it isn’t. When is it viable to think of allowing easy to mod even something like custom maps or Sprites, 3D models, rules and mechanics? How much does a game lose when it changes Focus to allow for player generated content? I’m going to talk about this. I can’t go into specific cost because it varies.

item.title: A Christmas Poem By Ron Gilbert
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a little Christmas poem by Ron Gilbert.

If you don’t know who Ron Gilbert is, he’s best known for creating scum, which was the script system that was used to create the adventure game Maniac mansion, and from there he went on to create a lot of other awesome adventure games like the secret of Monkey Island, Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade. He went on to do other games to like total Annihilation and death spank and the cave. So you may not know Ron Gilbert. You’ve probably played some of his games and he certainly has been really influential in the game industry.

item.title: Editors Day Talk, April 2000
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to try to recreate the presentation I gave at editor’s day in April of 2000.

I have a video about it. I’ll link below. We did an Arcanum press event at the Magic Castle in April of 2000, about a year before Arcanum came out.

I found my notes on this. I thought they were lost, but I think I’ve told you I have so many paper backups, so many hard drive backups in different formats, that I finally found my notes.

item.title: Believable Reactivity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about believable reactivity and, in other words, how do you make NPCs react to player choices in a way that makes sense in the RPG that you’re making?

This was a good question from Chaser 7, 2886, who asked: I was wondering how do you make choices in RPGs believable? What I mean is how do NPCs know you made the choice you made without making it like NPC’s magically know you made whatever choice you made? Okay, first of all, this is a good question, and I know I say that a lot, but this is a good question for two different reasons. One, thank you, Chaser 7, for actually clarifying your question. When you just said how do you make choices in RPGs believable, I thought you were going to say how do you make the NPC asking the quest to give to the player, how do you make that sound like a believable ask, like would the king actually ask me to go find his missing?

item.title: The Outer Worlds Launch Party Video
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to share with you something special: The Outer Worlds launch party video.

Let me explain so, when The Outer Worlds launched, we had a big launch party for it on October 25th 2019. It was awesome.

Um, most of obsidian was there, the development team, the publisher came in from uh private division and some of the The Outer Worlds — um, some of the Take 2 folks. We had a lot of other guests.

item.title: Wildstar Class Summary (Nov 2008)
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to do the Wild Star class summary from November 2008.

I found this file from 2008 that I created at home because I’ve mentioned in my other wstar videos that I, when I became design director, I was given 90 days to completely design all the classes for the game, because there was a big meeting in November. And I actually have It’s November 13th 2018, so that was about 90 days after I started as design director with all except for one person — all new design hires.

item.title: My Rejected Star Trek TNG Script
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my rejected Star Trek next Generation script.

This is a question from Wes 9353, who asked: have you talked more about the Star Trek TNG episode that you co-wrote, or was that just on the podcast he’s referring to was an interview I did with pistol shrimp game and I’ll put the link below, and that was a fun talk. It was the erquan Masters guys. It was super fun. I encourage you to watch it — about 22 minutes in. I do mention that I co-wrote a script, a screenplay, for Star Trek next Generation. It got rejected, but let me talk to you about it, like how it came to be, what the plot was. And I thought it was a pretty good plot, was a very star tracki in plot.

item.title: Working In Another's Game IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about working in another’s game IP.

This is not me talking about work making a non, taking a non-game IP and turning it into a game. I have a whole video on that. I’m talking about you’re making a game in someone else’s Game World — maybe it’s a, tabletop, and you’re converting it to a video game, which is a slight change of medium. Or maybe you’re making a sequel in someone else’s game world. Or maybe you’re working in a game where someone else is doing most of the design work. I want to talk about that because it’s an interesting feeling and an interesting process to be involved in something like that. But before I do, I want to tell you guys a story. It wouldn’t be a kanong games video, would it, without a story.

item.title: D&D Influences (By Edition)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about DND influences by addition.

Mostly this is influences on me and my work, but in one case it’s going to be my work influencing back into D and D, which I think is really cool. Anyway, this is response to Y l333 who says: hey, Tim, I don’t know about others, but I would love to hear about which editions of D and you’ve played, especially in the TSR days, what D and D books you own and which additions are your favorite and have had the most influence on your games. I’ve done a lot of talking about D and D here and there and I’ve done D and D stories and all that I will talk about, and I’ve talked about influences, tabletop influences, of non D andd stuff. So let’s just talk about Dungeons and Dragons. I have mentioned several times that the way I got introduced to Dungeons and Dragons was a little unusual.

item.title: Game Lessons From Collecting Fragrances
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to talk about game lessons from collecting fragrances.

It’s fun. Friday I’ve talked about chocolate, talked about dice, so now I’m going to talk about fragrances, something I collect a lot of, and I will show you in a few seconds the entire collection. Little history: I had a few like aftershaves and stuff in college — all of them were gifts. A friend gave me some gray flannel which I really loved, and my stepmother gave me a like a sampler of different things and they were Hit or Miss, but there was a 4711 in there and I remember thinking this is really good.

item.title: Grind
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about grind specifically.

Raul jandes asked about grind and in fact he even said I thought you had done one. I looked at a lot of your videos. I couldn’t see it I thought I had done one too, and I think what’s happening is I’ve done so many videos now that buried in some video I have that’s on a completely different topic. I may go off on a tangent for a minute and talk about grind, but I haven’t actually done a video on grind, so let’s do it. First I’m going to Define it, because I’ve learned I need to Define my terms clearly. Then I’m going to talk about um, how it’s different for different people and why it even exists and if you can make a game without it.

item.title: Networking With Game Developers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about networking, something that I have never been good at but that I recommend you do.

First off, I got a dog that’s not feeling good lying on my feet and I just had gum surgery. So you may see some stitches poking out. Um, just something happens when you get older. Um, every it seems like when I go to the doctor, the dentists are always saying: well, Mr Kane, at your age, you need take this, or we need to do this, or we need to put a camera here. I basically, when the doctor before he started the surgery, I asked him if he could give me pouty lips like Renee zel wigger. How did he do anyway, going to talk past the stitches and the dog will, who will occasionally probably be shaking her head?

item.title: Fallout's Starting Date
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fallout’s starting date and specifically what I mean by that is: why is the original Fallout set 84 years after the war?

Now I remember us talking about this. I remember there being a meeting where we discussed it. Because I get into as much as I can remember from that discussion, because I remember taking notes, but I’ve gone through everything I’ve even had, have digitized my handwritten notes that I mostly had at home and there isn’t a writeup on that meeting now. It was probably — destroyed because interplay threatened me with a lawsuit after I left so I destroyed all of the stuff I had at home. I talked about how I took a CD that had Fallout code. It had g code, it had the initial graphics Library code that I built ganol from which I used for the gerp star system generator that I’ve talked about, and it probably had digitized meeting notes on it that thing was destroyed. It’s gone if there’s any record on it, was probably on my interplay computer, which by now is certainly destroyed. I heard it was found at some point. Maybe the notes are out there, so who knows?

item.title: Insecurity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about insecurity.

This going to be an interesting video because it may be a little rambly, but it’s something I’ve been thinking about for almost a year and let me let me tell you why. So I’ve talked on this channel on many different videos about why people have said they didn’t really like working with me. Um, I’ve heard back now a lot of people may not like working with me, but they didn’t tell me directly. I’ve heard this from people who ended up liking working with me but understood why others didn’t, or may have talked to people who didn’t. The two biggest ones that I’ve heard were that I get very passionate about my ideas and then, in doing so, other people’s ideas get dismissed. Whether I’m intending to do that or not, it doesn’t feel good when your idea gets brushed aside because I’m, you know, all excitedly talking about my idea and then, related to that, I tended to get more attenion, whether it was in a brainstorming meeting or in any other kind of meeting, or even when press would come in, I’d get excited, I’d want to talk about stuff, so I’d get more attention and people just got frustrated and I can see that I get really into stuff.

item.title: The Dog
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to talk about the dog.

By popular demand, many people have asked to see the dog. I’ve done shorts of the dog. I put a whole playlist of them in the description, but today it’s fun. Friday I’m going to talk about the dog. Um, I’m just going to talk about her and throw up pictures and videos as appropriate. I’ve got literally hundreds of photos of her, so let’s start at the beginning.

item.title: Definitions Are Hard (aka Why A Game Director Is Necessary)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how definitions are hard and I’m going to subtitle this — why game director is necessary.

What I’m going to talk about today is why it is so incredibly hard to define something, a term, a concept, an object. People frequently argue because — not because they believe different things, but because they’re using different definitions of words, of Concepts, and they’re kind of talking past each other, and this happens a lot in the game industry and some of you are probably going — it’s not hard to define a term. That’s how you start, you define your terms and then you move on from there. No, it is really hard to define something and people will disagree on the most basic, fundamental of definitions. So I want to tell you a story first before I dive into the game development parts of here. Really, Tim, tell a story, so the very first day of an AI class I had in college, the teacher posed a question to the class, and this was a 90-minute class and this is all we talked about.

item.title: My Games As Other Genres
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about modeling, constipation in role playing games e.

No, I’m not. Today I’m going to talk about realizing my games into other genres. This is something that I mentioned weeks ago in a video I made that was a postmortem of all my games combined together. It was called meta postmortem.

In the comment section of that video a lot of people mentioned that they wanted me to continue my thought on. I have a lot of ideas in my idea book about the IPS I’ve made, but making games with them that just weren’t role playing games, CU, I got a little, I said, I felt a little pigeon hold into making role playing games, not that I don’t like making them and I don’t have fun making them, but some of my IPS I thought would work better in other genres or just as well, and even though I don’t have any experience making them, I was like one day I wanted to have made them like that but I didn’t. I even got a request from someone in the business saying, hey, keep going with that thought. So this video is me keeping going with that thought.

item.title: Using An IP In A Different Medium
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using an IP in a different medium, and

this is what I mean specifically is by IP is a license to an intellectual property. Maybe it was a tabletop role playing game, maybe it’s a book, maybe it’s a movie. For me, it would be making a game from that, and I have a whole video on converting movies into games, and there’s a link below, but it’s a similar process. This, using an IP in a different medium, is a similar process that TV shows or movies go through when they try to convert a book or game into their medium. And I’m not talking about the Fallout TV show here.

item.title: Game Design Portfolio
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about your game design portfolio or, more specifically, what you, as a game designer, should include in a portfolio that you’re sending to a game company when you apply for a job.

This is me answering gamedev Tommy, who asked for — quote advice on what a game design portfolio should really consist of and how it should present, be presented. For someone who has not worked in a mainstream Studio yet, unquote — good question, um, so let me talk about exactly what I want to see. I’m going to divide it into what I expect to see and what I’d really like to see. So the expect — the expectation — part is: you really should include this. The I like part is: if you got this, it’s what I want to see, and it’ll move you to the top of the pile. Before you start, though, you should probably.

item.title: A Real Life Fallout Story With Lasers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell you a real life Fallout story with lasers.

Now, this is something it just happened last month, just a few weeks old, and during this story I will say the phrase: she used her foot to shoot me in the face with a laser, and it will make sense. So let me tell. Let me tell you the uh preamble to the story.

item.title: Stress Testing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about stress testing games.

This is another question from thequiz who, when giving me a few topic suggestions for upcoming videos, recommended stress testing games. Now let me tell you what that is. Um, this is about. It’s mostly about testing games against mspec machines, but a little more than that. It is not QA.

I’ve talked about quality assurance testing. Uh, I have a couple videos I’ll link below about how we did QA for The Outer Worlds, but this is about. When I talk about stress testing a game, it’s about making sure they work with a wide variety of machines Beyond just mspec, but frequently it is mspec, so the two are related. I do have a video about mpec as well. Um, it’s linked below: stress testing games. You have to think of all the variety of ways that you could make a game slow. You could make a game unreliable and, yes, even crashy.

item.title: Arcanum Optimization
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum optimization.

This comes from a set of questions from uh, a viewer, diiz, who asks a few topic suggestions for upcoming videos: how to optimize game code, how to even find what is wise to optimize, and different ways to measure results of optimization, and a bunch more questions too that didn’t have to do with optimization. So so I’m going to stop it right there. I may get the other ones later, but let me just talk about optimization, and I’ve decided to go back to Arcanum because I can talk about that code, I own that code, I can look at that code, I looked at that code and um it. Lets me just be much more specific about what I’m going to talk about now. I already have a general optimization video, which I’ll link below, which talks about basic techniques for code optimization, like when to do them, uh, when sometimes changing the design specification can lead to faster code or code that uses less memory. So sometimes the optimization is not in code, it’s change your design, even just a little bit, and you can end up with way better code. I also have a video on the specific memory savings that we got in Arcanum through object compaction. I will summarize it again here, but I recommend watching that video too.

item.title: Don't Forget Temple Of Elemental Evil 2003
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to ask you not to forget about Temple of Elemental Evil, my 2003 video game.

Now you’re probably going. I’m not going to forget about that. Why you? Why are you asking me not to forget about this?

Well, let me just say that I’ve talked about different reasons. I do this Channel and I think I found a new one.

I do not want this game to slip away into you — complete history — and no one ever mentions it or talks about it. Why am I bringing this up?

item.title: Solo vs. Party
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Solo versus party character control.

This is something that was asked most recently. I think it’s been asked several times, but it asked most recently by Tom FS, who asked: I was wondering if you could go over your thoughts on full party control versus only controlling your character. I view this separately from the full party creation versus individual character creation debate. What’s the lack of party control in Arcanum due to multiplayer? I know it was that case for never winner nights.

item.title: Toy Post Mortem
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a toy postmortem.

That’s hard postmortem. A toy postmortem that’s hard to say. People have asked me about post-mortems on here and I’ve talked about them. Um, I’ve done one meta postmortem where I took all my games and I said here’s what was consistent, good and bad about all my games together. But I don’t want to review other people’s games.

item.title: Naming Things
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about naming things.

What I mean by that is, when you start to put together a game you’re going to have to come up with so many names you’re going to have to name the game. You’re going to have to name items in the game, places in the game, other people in the game. Uh, if it’s a space game, you’re going to have to come up with — you know fanciful technology names and names for other aliens. If it’s a fantasy game, going to have to come up with magical sounding names and you know names that describe magical processes, like: oh no, the wizard has placed Wards on all of the glyph Stones. Whatever, what I want to talk about is how to come up with those names, and I think this process is something that’s often overlooked, and I’m going to share a couple examples of things — well known, I think, that are just off. I’m going to talk about how I did it. Now I’m going to start, though, with saying I’m kind of up and down with names, especially proper names.

item.title: Enemy AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about enemy Ai, and this is to answer a question from driver 3899, who asked: any chance you could talk a little bit about how you approach thinking about enemy AI?

Is there a sweet spot between too complex and too simple in terms of behavior? Can you accidentally make enemies too smart for players, or is the performance hit the limiting factor and how complex you can make their behaviors? What are the most important things to keep in mind when designing the enemy AI?

item.title: Rising Game Budgets
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Rising Game budgets.

This was a question from Irvin Chadwick 5046, who asked: would you be able to give a breakdown on why video games are so much more expensive to make now as compared to 10 years ago? First off, I can compare it to 10 years ago- 20, 30, 40 — and I will — um, I’m also going to assume that this question has nothing to do with sales price. Just to head that off. That’s a completely different discussion of why video games cost more now, or even if they do cost more now, adjusted for inflation, but that’s a whole different discussion. Going to not going to talk about sales price here, I’m going to talk about budget also. This is a general discussion. Notice all the caveats, because I’m just heading off comments.

item.title: Fast Travel
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fast travel.

Now this is a topic I I thought I had covered this, but when I went and looked through my videos, I basically briefly mentioned it in a video about Quest markers, which I’ll link below, but I didn’t actually talk about it that much and not into the detail that I think some people wanted. In fact, I got a question from a snow crash who asked: have you spoken about fast travel on RPGs and some games? I feel like you can use it to essentially bypass things like encumbrance, hardcore mechanics, Random Encounters, Etc. Yes, you are absolutely right, you can use it. Many games let you, especially after you buy a couple perks, avoid a lot of the mechanics that make the game more difficult. So let’s talk about that.

item.title: My Memorabilia Cabinets
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to walk you through my two glass cabinets of curio curios memorabilia, detris, from now, my 44th year in the game industry.

I’ve actually put things in there within the last month. People have been asking me to do this since pretty much I began the channel and to parap phrase: Chief wigam, what is your fascination with my forbidden closet of mystery? I get people want to see what’s in there. Um, for me, a lot of the stuff is just fun junk. I mean, some of it is really, you know, close to my heart, memorabilia from the games. Others are stuff I picked up online because I thought it was funny.

item.title: Story Telling And Player Agency
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about storytelling and player agency.

This is something I’ve touched on in a ton of videos. I will try to link as many as I can below, but there are a lot of them. I wanted to make a specific video on this because I kind of feel like the need to laser focus on the fact that storytelling and player agency are a little contradictory. They’re a little bit at odds and at some point if you want to make a computer role playing game, you need to decide, like consciously, which one of those takes precedence in your game. Now, of course, I have a bias here. I love player agency over all else.

item.title: Questions & Answers, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer more questions, so I’m going to do a question and answers — part two, which I haven’t done for a while.

I want to answer some questions that people have asked, and some of these were asked quite a while ago, so I apologize, but they were asked and they were. They needed an answer. That was longer than something I just wanted to type really quickly, but they weren’t really a whole video, so I waited until I had a few of these. I think I have four or five of them and I figured I’d just do one video and knock them all out.

item.title: Wading In The Shallows
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about waiting in the shallows.

I’ll tell you what I mean by this. I actually have an alternate title which I’ll give you at the end. Um, waiting in the shallows is what I’ve said to people in the past, when I want to express that you can’t really know something, you can’t really understand a topic after just a casual involvement in it. This you’ve heard people talk about like armchair quarterbacks, and you know people like to give medical advice because they looked up something on WebMD.

item.title: Game Simplification
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game simplification, and by this I’m talking about various ways games can be made easier to use but to some players feel handh holdy.

This is me answering a question from VA cus who asks: recently I saw some screenshots from games with very obvious Clues where to go next. I mean like obvious gray rock formations with obvious yellow protrusions to stick to ancient ladders painted in yellow, Etc. I don’t actually know from what games those were.

item.title: Worst RPG Ever
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the worst RPG ever.

I’m not going to talk about a specific RPG. I’m going to talk about what I would consider to be, and I will describe to you to be, the worst RPG you could possibly make. It’s a fun Friday. What I decided to do was I now have 400 videos. I’ve got hundreds of videos about what I consider to be the best way to make an RPG. Now, I don’t consider every decision I make in an RPG to be the best one or the best one for everyone. It’s just how I like to make an RPG. So I thought I’d flip this on the on its head and describe to you what an RPG would be like if you do the opposite of what I recommend on all those videos.

item.title: Adding New Features After Launch
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adding new features after launch, and by that I mean I’m going to talk about why it can be very difficult sometimes to add a new feature after the game has shipped.

And I know you’ve all had this experience where you get a new game and you’re playing it and you’re having a lot of fun, but within an hour you’re like I can’t believe this happens, I can’t believe I can’t do this, or I can’t believe this interface is like this, and you wonder why they just don’t change it. Like, in addition to all the bugs they may be fixing in the next patch, why don’t they just fix this one? Why don’t they just add this feature now?

item.title: Remote Work
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about remote work and specifically, this is an answer to a question from R3 M1 who asks: hey Tim, what’s your take on remote staff versus having the team work in an office?

Do you have a preference? What are the pros and cons, Etc. Good question.

I’m really glad the way you phrase it because, like everything, I think remote work has Pros and cons. Um, I don’t think there’s very little out there that I think is 100% good or 100% bad, and maybe that’s my programmer brain thinking, because there’s no perfect sorting algorithm. It all depends on what you’re sorting and how many, and how frequently it changes, and whether it’s already sorted when you go to Resort.

item.title: Great External Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about great external ideas, and by that I mean ideas that came from outside the development team, that were really good and worked and ended up in the game.

This actually was a question from brains and beats, who asked: has there been a time when some person from high up or just not on the dev or design team brought in an idea that was actually great, and I love this question because, while I frequently told stories that have people off the team not being shown in the best light, I love that this is a way to Showcase some really good ideas that came from people who weren’t on the development team. I’m going to define the development team as people who worked on the game as their daily job. You know every — everyone from the game director down to the most Junior person, is on the development team.

item.title: GURPS Everything
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gerps everything, which was an attempt of mine to run a gerps campaign in the 9s using the basic set and every single Source book that I owned — not the World Books, not the ones that had particular settings in them, but all the source books, like the extensions on, like magic and sanics and I’ll talk about that.

item.title: Perk Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about perk ideas.

This is a fun Friday video, so it’s kind of a me talking about ideas I’ve had in my idea book for years and I pulled out ones that were specific to perks. I wanted to do — and I’m going to talk about — reactions. I got to these perk ideas when I talked about them in the past and also why you don’t see these in any of my games. Get to all that, but first let’s just dive into some of the ideas I had for new perks. One of the ones I’ve always been noodling around is using your charisma attribute to replace your deck attribute for Armor class bonuses. Basically, whatever dexterity is providing to make you harder to hit, you can take a perk and say that bonus comes from my Charisma instead of my dexterity.

item.title: Cultural Changes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about cultural changes and I specifically want to get into eventually, because I’m going to tell some stories.

First of why you should be very aware of how fast the culture changes and how this should affect how you design and develop your video games, because you can quickly get stuck with a game that is seems laughable or AIC very quickly, and not because of the graphics but because of your inability to predict cultural changes. But I I will get to that now. I have a whole video about why I don’t like cultural references and I will link it below and just to give you the synopsis right now. I don’t like cultural references for the same reason, I don’t like a lot of humor of a certain kind. I don’t like anything in your game that makes the player feel like not getting it and are being excluded. I don’t like anything that makes the player feel like an other, like, oh shoot, this game is.

item.title: Intent
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about intent, and I will get to how this refers to G video games, but I wanted to talk about it because it’s come up a lot in a lot of my videos.

In fact, it’s often the subtext of many of my videos and I will often say upfront: you know, this is what I’m going to talk about and this is why — and I tell stories about why I’m telling these things, or I talk about why I’m saying something before I even go into what I’m saying and why I thought this was going to be a serious video. Of course, right when I’m setting everything up, the dog decides this is a perfect time to fall asleep at my feet. So you’re going to have to listen to my seriousness with the background of Darth Vader dog.

item.title: Communication Between Roles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about communication between roles, specifically, how to handle all the different roles of people on your development team and getting them to talk to each other.

This is actually a question from Boku, who asks, I wonder, about the different communication channels between roles, which roles are often together and such because they are closely related into velopment — you hinted at level designers and narratives being together, for example. Boku, this is a really good question. It’s also a really serious question because it has to do with how you’re going to manage a team, especially as teams get bigger. This gets more and more important — and get them to work together. Even though there are several of them, many of different types of people. Some people want to have these discussions, some people will dominate the discussions, other people don’t want to have discussions at all, but you need to get people to communicate, and at the high level. I would just answer and say this: communic, crossr communication.

item.title: Design Fallbacks
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about design fallbacks now.

I’ve talked about these before and in fact I’m answering a question from gkid 100 who asked: can you make a video about fallbacks? What is the process for designing a less resource heavy version of a system or a replacement for a system? I think I talked about this. I know I did design fallbacks heavily during The Outer Worlds, but I had them in previous games, just not so structured like. There literally was a section in the design document for the The Outer Worlds. It’s called design fallbacks and, by the way, when I talk about design document there are some people out there that still think I’m talking about I have a paper document or a word dock. The Outer Worlds one was spread across multiple, uh, web pages. I, we used Confluence that were constantly Ed, Ed and interl, so it was like a living document. So you know, if you went to a page you could not only see the design. But you could see people’s comments on it. You could see who was assigned to it. If you clicked on that, you could go through even the schedules and see idea of when this thing would be made.

item.title: Dice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about dice.

You see, I did a video, well, from your point of view, I did a px video a few weeks ago. From my point of view, I did it a few days ago because when I did the PX 2024 video, which I’ll link below, I moved it to the front of the queue because pxs have just happened and I don’t do topical stuff very often. So it was like, well, let’s put it in the front of the queue. This video isn’t topical, so I’m going to the end of the queue. So, even though it’s only been a few days for me, it’s weeks for you, which should give you an idea of how big my queue is. I got a lot of requests on that video to talk about my dice because, I mentioned, I have a lot of dice. I have a bit of an addiction to dice. I’ve been ever since I got my first polyhedral set 45 years ago.

item.title: Balancing Friction
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about balancing friction, and this comes from a question from padon Roso — I hope I’m pronouncing your name correctly — who asked: can you talk about balancing friction in RPGs and games?

It seems like quite the complex thing to do, when the friction points for the player and how they’re distributed around the gameplay Loop will most likely influence other already complex systems in the game. For example, I’m thinking about pacing, both the storytelling and the alternation of action, non-action faces. I’m also talking about game economy, as talked about in the encumbrance video, and the full effect of friction points seems to me like something you can only fully evaluate when the game is all playable, especially when the crossover with those other systems I was referring to earlier is taken into account. Oh wait, there’s more. Do you have any trick or story you can tell about this, about how you tackle this problem along your career?

item.title: Note-Taking Best Practices
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about note taking best practices.

I’ve gotten a lot of questions about my taking notes, which I’ve done for decades. In fact, in my video about a really bad College teacher I had, I speculated that’s when I started taking notes, because she was so critical of my notes for her class. Now I get questions all the time and I direct them to two videos. I have one C about my notes and one C about my notes part two, but for some people they want more, so I picked one of the questions. This is from MK gm8 yl who asks: I watched both of your note videos.

item.title: Converting A Movie Into A Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about converting, like a movie or TV show into a game.

I’m going to use an example. This is to answer a question from Andrew Williams, zn4, NP, who asked: uh, here’s a question idea. Have you ever watched a movie or show and thought that would make a fun game? If so, what movie or show would you do?

item.title: My Games’ Theme
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my games theme.

That’s games plural and theme singular, because I want to talk to you about how you can look at all the games I’ve made, especially the ones I was fundamentally involved with creating the original Ip, but also ones that I worked on, and how you can interpret them through the single lens of one theme. Now you may be thinking my, theme is choice and consequence, but I view that less as a theme and more as a mechanic. So I’ve talked about setting, story mechanics.

item.title: Galactic Coordinates
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. It’s Friday, Funday, so I wanted to pull something out of my idea book about Galactic coordinates.

This was a system that I made years ago, decades ago. Uh, it’s in my first book, which means it was at least — it’s at least- 15 years old, it’s. I needed a system, or I wanted a system, to name Stellar systems, son, planets, moons. I pulled this out of the idea book about eight years ago when we started working on The Outer Worlds, because I thought, well, maybe I finally have a chance to use this. Didn’t end up using it. We went with another system, um, and then, ironically, it never came up in the game. So the system for I for cataloging Stellar system in The Outer Worlds hasn’t yet hit lore.

item.title: Encumbrance, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about encumbrance again.

So this is encumbrance part two. The video — uh, the part one video, just called incumbrance, and I’ll link it below — generated a lot of really good discussion in the comment section. So if you haven’t watched it, you probably want to go watch that before you watch this, because I’m going to build on some things from the first part and I’m not really going to dive into details. So if you haven’t watched encumbrance, just stop this, go watch that and then come back here. The first thing I noticed was a lot of people were saying how I was talking about encumbrance as weight based. So items had weight, you could only carry a maximum weight and, um, there were things like perks and attributes that could increase that weight and they suggested doing it with slots and there’s multiple ways of doing it I mean, our Arcanum had a grid and you had to fit everything on that grid and sometimes you had to Tetris things around to get a new object, and there are other games that did you have like everything. Every item just fits into one slot, and you start with 20 slots, and maybe there are bags you can find that increase your slots. Maybe there are perks that say every bag you pick up has two extra slots, whatever it is the My point stands, though, that encumbrance is about imposing an inventory limit about how much the player can carry. So whether you do it with weight or whether you do it with slot, whether it’s multiple slots, whether it’s slot and weight, those are just limits. So we’re still talking about the same thing. It’s just: how is your encumbrance set up to limit the number of items that a player could carry at any given point? Then several of you said, well, just don’t have.

item.title: Romantasy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Romany, which is a new term that I learned and I will get into defining in a second.

But I want to give you some context. So I have a colleague — a work colleague — who on the side writes romance novels under, I believe, a pseudonym — I’m not sure — and I was really curious when I found this out, because I was asking them: you’re like why romance novels? And what they told me was it is one of the best selling genres of books, way outpacing other fiction genres like science fiction, fantasy, detective, you know novels and a lot of non-fiction as well. It’s just a very big genre and it apparently sells continuously. It doesn’t have Peaks for like around Christmas or summer, like I kind of thought that’s they were books that people kind of read in the summer. Nope, they apparently sell really well all the time and they’re fairly easy to write. So that, of course, made me think of the rise of romanceable Companions and RPGs, which I’m on record of saying.

item.title: Meta Post Mortem
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a meta postmortem of my games and I will explain what I mean by that.

So every game that I’ve made, starting with Fallout — I don’t think I did this for rags to riches and I know I didn’t do it for barale construction set or grand slam bridge — but starting with Fallout, every game I’ve done, I’ve written up some kind of postmortem notes to myself where I BAS basically looked at the shipping game, the game that went out the door, and wrote down what I thought went well and went poorly for that game. Mostly it was about the end state of the game, the features of the game, but sometimes it was about the game development process, um, or sometimes it was just here’s how I felt after it went out. By the way, it is normal to be a little sad when you’re game ships. I remember talking about this with someone at obsidian once after The Outer Worlds went out and he was like why am I sad? And I said, well, for years you devote yourself to this thing and then it’s done and it’s out of your hands and at this point you can’t make big changes to it. You can do bug fixing and stuff and balance tweaks, but it’s kind of done and it’s out there and there’s just this feeling of mild — it’s not depression, it’s just a slight sadness that you get when a game ships and that’s normal. So I would tend to write these postmortem. That was a weird diverge. Other people on the team often wrote them too and I would ask them for them and they were always worried I was going to be like: oh, I can’t believe you said this, but I liked seeing what they thought went right and wrong. It was as eye openening as my own post-mortems were to me years later. To see what other people on the team thought went right and wrong. Now I’ve also mentioned — I’ve even written these for game — I didn’t work on that were, but were part of ips I created — and I did that because I wanted to look at those post morms myself later and see if there was any Trends among the games.

item.title: Channel Analytics (August 2024)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Channel analytics.

Now, why am I talking about this? Well, I hit 160,000 Subs just a couple days ago — I’m filming this — at the end of August and a friend of mine saw me hit that and asked me about Channel analytics and I was like I don’t know what you’re referring to. He said: well, if you go into your YouTube, YouTu, Channel, you can dig around and pull up all these interesting numbers about your channel and I’m like, oh, that’s what that tab is for. I never looked at that tab. There’s like a, an earnings Tab and an analytics Tab and a copyright Tab and a um God. There’s a number of tabs that I either hardly look at or when I first started it, I opened them up and went huh, and then Clos them.

item.title: My First Day Of College
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell you a story about my first day of college.

It’s Friday fun day, so I thought I’d tell this story, and it’s really less about my first day of college and more about my mom, and a lot of people have commented that my mom seemed awesome. My mom was awesome. It’s hard to describe, you know I’m. A lot of people probably think their mom is really good. I just like to tell stories where she’s involved. You can see how cool and fun and, uh, just encouraging she was. So to get this story right, I’ve got to give you some context.

item.title: Technology Changes Jobs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how technology changes jobs.

Now this is going to be kind of a companion video to a couple videos I did before and they’re linked below. It’s called Uh — industry changes and artificial intelligence because it’s sort of an extension to those, and I’m going to refer to some stories I tell in those uh, so you may want to watch them, so you understand the references I give in this video because I don’t want to be too repetitive. I know I’ve been told I’m repetitive repeatedly but I’m trying to avoid that.

item.title: Awards
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Awards, specifically game awards, more specifically, game awards that my games have won and a few that I personally have won.

And then just one thing that’s always eluded me for the most part, with one exception. I want to just talk about it because some of you may be going: oh I, I know how you feel when I, when you get to the end of this, may be going, wow, how narcissistic and egotistical are you.

item.title: Feedback Needs Context
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how feedback needs context, because it’s hard to know what to do with feedback if you don’t have context.

In the absence of context, feedback is very difficult to do anything with. Now sidebar, I have done two videos on feedback: one on how to give good feedback and one on how to receive criticism, which just feedback. I will link those below. I am not going to repeat those videos here because there’s too much to go into and I would very much get sidetracked into the points I made in those videos. I highly recommend that you watch those two videos before watching this one so we don’t get into the Weeds on: well, what’s constructive feedback and what am I supposed to do with the feedback when I get it?

item.title: Learning Game Design From Old Sitcoms
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about learning game design from old sitcoms.

Now I’m going to tell you why I’m talking about this and then how it connects to video games — kind of at the end. But the last few years have been interesting. I probably watched more television in the last three years than I have in the last 30, and for a whole bunch of reasons. One, I was working part-time. Two to: I had moved to a new city where I didn’t know anybody, in a new state where I didn’t know anybody, and I did it that all in the middle of a pandemic. I moved up here in the middle of 2020, so I just had a lot of free time on my hands and nowhere I could go. So I ended up watching a lot of TV shows.

item.title: Toy Update, Part 5
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to give you toy update part five.

Now, I know I said last week that was probably going to be the last update for a while. This one really is, because I’m going to explain everything I did and then show you several minutes of game footage so you can see how it all turned out. But I’ve already archived the code and the assets into a compressed file. I threw it up on the cloud and I’m not going to be working this for a while. I just I want to do other things and you know, like I’ve said many times, I’m not on a schedule, I’m not beholding anybody, so I work on this. When it’s fun, it is fun.

item.title: Min Specs And Ratings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game minimum specifications and ratings.

This comes from a question from suin, who asks: how do games get their system requirements? Since Tim’s a programmer, I’d imagine understanding the system is pretty important. But is determining the requirements part of the programmer’s job, or QA, or marketing, or the producer? How much time you spent figuring that out? And is a game designed for a spec early on? For example, our game has to run on 480p and 128 Mega of Ram, or at the end, when the project is finalized, when everything that’s going in is in and the minimum is then gauged.

item.title: My Memoirs, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my Memoirs again, so consider this a my Memoirs video, part two.

So I’ve shown it before. It’s this big honken thing I wrote and got printed, sent to some people for feedback in that first video that I link below. You can watch it to learn why I wrote it, what’s inside and why I’m not going to publish it. So if you have questions about any of that, um, watch the first video, because I’m not going to talk about that here. But this Memoir and this channel has given me a lot of feedback on me and I’ve learned a lot, and these are things that are interesting and I’ll talk about a lot of them. But I want to really talk about the fact that, while I’ve gotten a lot of wonderful feedback, really nice things — especially some nice things from people I’ve worked with who could have said nothing that I haven’t talked to in 10, 15, in one case I heard from someone I haven’t talked to in, haven’t talked to, I haven’t talked to in 27 years, but they wanted to know that they liked working with me, which is really really sweet and really nice. But I’ve gotten a lot of negative feedback too, and I’ve gotten that negative feedback from people who meant it constructively and said: oh yeah, I loved working with You know you do this thing, though sometimes, and other people they were just angry and like, ah, you do this blah. But I heard it enough from both kinds of people that I’m like this stuff has got to be true. So let me tell you what the negative feedback I’ve gotten about me.

item.title: Understanding Someone Else’s POV
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about understanding someone else’s point of view.

Now this is going to sound like an odd topic and I will end the video on why I’m telling you about this on a game development Channel, but I want to talk about it because it’s something I’ve always liked doing and it gets me into trouble a lot, and I’m going to talk to you about why that is so. Understanding someone else’s point of view is something I really like doing. I like asking people: why do you like that game or why do you hate that game or movie or sometimes, political candidate, although on that one I often just back off and go. I don’t think people even know why they like the political candidate they do.

item.title: Sometimes I Hate VO
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how sometimes I hate voiceover, hate.

I hate what it does to me as a game designer: reaches deep into my game design soul and just punches it and nobody acknowledges it and everybody’s like: vo is great, wonderful. Now let me just say before I continue, there are times where I think voice over elevates games.

Trust me, I wrote the intro to Fallout that everybody likes, the one about war never changing, and all that did. I think what I wrote was fantastic, the, a perfect diamond of writing.

item.title: Toy Update, Part 4
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a toy update, part four, which is also an insomnia update, part two, because

I’ve been up since 2: am and my lack of sleep translated into new code in my toy. I did a bunch of changes to the shields that you guys asked for. I did a bunch of extra code that was floating around in my nogin and for some reason I can do math at 3:00 am. But once I hit the UI — some of the UI stuff — I was going to do the quote Marie condo. UI coding does not spark joy in me and that’s where I kind of stopped. But anyway, I want to show you what I did because it’s I think it’s pretty cool.

item.title: Localization Questions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about localization.

Localization is when you make a version of your game for outside, for distribution outside of whatever country you originally made your game for. Now, this is an answer to a question from Johnny Katswell, who asked: you mentioned adaptation. You mentioned adapting your games to different cultures and languages. I didn’t find a video on this topic on your channel, so let me ask my questions here. By the way, I really thought I had a video on this and I didn’t so good catch Johnny. Johnny had four questions.

item.title: Pros And Cons Of GURPS As A CRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pros and cons of gerps as a computer role playing game, specifically what I learned trying to implement gerps as a computer game for Fallout.

This is to answer a question from player one fanatic who asks about, quote, giving a deep dive on gerps that was originally in Fallout. What skills, advantages, disadvantages and other game mechanics were implemented?

item.title: Balancing Game Features
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about balancing game features.

Now this is a question from Josh and Parker who asked: could you go over the process of balancing new game features as they’re added to the game? How do you make sure new weapons or spells aren’t overpowered but the player is still able to get enough new content to stay entertained? How do you know how many hits an enemy should take to kill Etc?

item.title: Polarizing Stories
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about polarizing stories.

I basically told a lot of Stories on this channel. Almost every one of my videos is just me telling stories and many of them have caused people to immediately fall into one of two camps. Um, at least the people who are commenting. Some people think that was a great story, you’re awesome, I’d love working with you. And then there’s other people going: what a horrible story. You’re terrible. You’re the whole reason I wouldn’t want to work in the game industry. I’m not here to comment on which one of those people, which of those are right or which of those are wrong. Um, I will say that my caution videos, which I’ll link below, really did that at a level I did not expect, mostly because they got picked up and um made viral by people with far more followers and subscribers than I have. Um, and people did reaction videos to them and whether or not they agreed with me or not, because they had such a big audience, there were a lot more people falling into those two camps of he’s horrible, he’s awesome and so kind of. What I want to talk about today is, uh, I found in my notes two stories that I looked back and forth between them. They weren’t near each other. Um, in fact, I’m going to strip away identifying characteristics so you don’t know what company it was at. You don’t know who these people were.

item.title: Toy Update, Part 3
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim.

Are you ready for a toy update? This will be toy update number three. I’ll link part two and the first one down below. But this is because I, when I did toy update 2 and I was showing off my space game with some ideas I had — um, I got so many good ideas of what to do with the shields on enemy ships that I had to do them. So I spent a weekend learning how to do shaders in, and it’s not that bad. I thought I was going to have to do a lot more work than I did.

item.title: Matching Your Project And Team
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about matching your project and your team.

This is a topic I’ve wanted to talk about for a long time, but I’ve put it off because I know I’ve talked a lot about nuance and context on this channel, but I was trying to figure out a way of approaching this where people didn’t immediately think I was denigrating the team or any of the teams I’d worked with, despite all the wonderful things I’ve said. I know people will just jump there and go. He’s putting down the team, so let me just say this isn’t about the people. This is about a reality that I’ve talked about at length and it’s true for me, it’s true for you, it’s true for everybody.

item.title: Why I Don't Play Online Anymore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I don’t play online games much anymore, and I’m particularly talking about multiplayer, and I did it for a long time.

I mean, multiplayer games have been around for a long time. I’m not just talking about, you know, giant MMOs. Even the consoles that started coming out in the late ’70s, like the Atari BCS, it supported a lot of two-player games, and even a lot of four player games, and most of the consoles back then did They usually had two ports in the front, um or they, and frequently the second Port could have a tap that would allow multiple controllers. So you — that’s how you would play — two and four people games.

item.title: Horror In Non-Horror Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about horror and non horror games.

I want to answer a question from decode 666, who asked horror and non-horror video games — how to approach it properly, that one Mission which still ST, which still sends chills down your spine, for example, the Ocean House Hotel in vampire, the Masquerade bloodlines, or the atmosphere of Fallout 1 and two, like the at Ambience, music level, design and art, robbing the cradle in Thief, deadly Shadows, the Dunwich building in Fallout 3, ravenol and halflife 2. Those are a lot of good examples and I think I’ve played them all or been there when they were made. So I’m going to answer decode 666’s question, but first I want to point out decode 66 is the person who did the, wood art behind me, the one I did the video on. I’ll link it below, so so, hey, anyway, um, a lot of what you mentioned in your question, even if it was on games I worked on, I didn’t. Make it, so I’ve told this story many times, but Jason Anderson did the Ocean House level in Bloodlines.

item.title: Teaching
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about teaching, in terms of what lessons I learned by teaching and kind of how I applied some of those lessons to game development.

I’ve mentioned that I was a grad in graduate school for four years, four and a half um from late 87 to Mid 91, and during the time as a graduate student, uh, I was at University of California, Irvine UCI, their school of information and computer science. During those four years I had to be a teaching assistant — it’s they will pay you to be a TA and I ta a lot of classes. I te programming languages, uh, basic. I ta te um operating systems and that’s one I actually really liked. I operating systems through three times because I really like the professor, uh, Steven Franklin, and it was taught at 8:00 in the morning and it was an optional class, so it was full of students who really wanted to be there because they didn’t need to be and it was 8. In the morning so it kind of weeded out people who didn’t want to learn about operating systems.

item.title: Toy Update, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do part two of my toy update.

I did a video a few weeks ago where I did an update on some of the toys I code, which are the little games that I’m just having fun with, and this was my space combat game and I talked about how I wanted to do a few things to make it better. Namely, I wanted to make new Photon torpedo types and I wanted to change how the shields looked on enemy ships. So I did that and I kind of want to give you an update because I want to show you that even with toys, you know, you can play with them and they get progression and you can make them better over time.

item.title: This Channel And The Game Industry
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about this Channel and the game industry, kind of a meta video, a video about my videos, kind of to put in perspective like what

I’ve been trying to do here for a year and a half. Um, and I’ll get to that, but before I get there I I need to tell a couple stories shocker. So My Generation — I think I hate the labels, but I would be the very oldest you can be and still be Gen X — my generation was the one that first really got into computers. I had a computer in high school. I was going online. I was really into technology.

item.title: Hiring People
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about hiring people.

This is come up a lot. I’ve had a video half prepared for this for a long time. I’ve noticed people here and then when I talk about Staffing up a project or interview questions they would. They would ask me: well, how did you hire people, how did you go about hiring people? So I want to talk about how and when I did that, because it’s a little surprising how long it was before I ever had to do that for all my beginning games. I was simply placed in them, I was assigned to it. So, Grand Slam Bridge, BS tail construction set, Rags to Riches, I was just assigned.

item.title: Good Lessons From A Bad Teacher
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about good Lessons From a Bad Teacher.

This is something that I talked about really briefly almost a year ago in a video called School versus self-taught, where I was talking about the pros and cons of learning at a University versus self — teing, you know, teaching yourself and also — I apologize, the dog is snoring up a storm. Um, in that video I talked about an experience I had with a really bad teacher my first year of engineering school, my undergraduate degree. I have a link below not only to the video, but to the time marker where I start talking about that, because I want to talk about it in more detail so you can really understand how bad it was. And then I want to tell you the lessons I walked away from that with and sort of how I think this going to help you approach things that happen to you that aren’t the best thing in the world but you can take away. Something good from it, so let’s dive into this a little in more detail.

item.title: PAX 2024
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about pxs 2024.

Yes, I went to P here in Seattle over the Labor Day weekend, super fun, and I want to kind of talk about it now. I’ve been to a half a dozen PA conferences before. I’ve been to this one, which sometimes I think it’s called PA West or PAX Prime. I just call it PA. It’s like what I do with Fallout. The first one is just Fallout.

item.title: IP Idea: Unknown Powers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about an IP idea that I had for unknown Powers.

This is Friday, funay. I thought I’d throw out another idea that’s in my idea book and it’s something that’s bugged me for a while — for years actually — that many games that are in the magic or superhero or Supernatural game genre they just explain powers in like mundane, excruciating detail, as if your character was born with a manual and just goes: oh, I can fly 60 feet a second or something. And that takes away a lot of the — at least for me, a lot of the Mystique and weirdness and non scientificness of what’s going on. And I understand why tabletop games have to do this, but computer games don’t. The computer can understand exactly what you have without telling you. So that’s what I want to talk about. What if you made a game in one of those genres where you have a random power assigned to you when you make a? New game but you can’t use it right away, you it turns on at a random after a random time interval, or an event happens to you and I’ll explain all that, and then it only works under certain conditions.

item.title: Thinking "What If?" In Detail
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a technique I call thinking what if in detail?

This is primarily for setting designers out there, because, remember I said, the order I like design to go in is setting, followed by story, followed by System mechanics. This is a technique that I use to come up with new settings, and I’ll talk about that in a few minutes. But what I want to start with is: this came because I was looking at my notes, like I’m always doing, and there was a note about a lunch meeting at troa. It wasn’t an official meeting, it was a bunch of us were sitting around — I think it was during Temple. A bunch of us are sitting around eating lunch and someone had brought up trying to guess, um, what people from a century ago would be amazed at. And things were coming up like you know, cars, although cars have been around kind of for at least a century now, but more things like all the appliances. We have, you know: washers, dryers, refrigerators, the fact that we can have ice whenever we want, um, the fact that travel is a lot easier, um, and more common.

item.title: Flow
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about flow.

I’m not talking about Flo, The Sassy kiss my grits waitress from the TV sitcom Alice. I’m talking about Flo as in the The Zone, The Mind state that you get into that allows this really efficient, uninterrupted work output. If you know what flow is, you just know what it is. If you never Flow, then you probably are confused. Right now it’s hard to Define flow. Um, it takes a while to get into it.

item.title: Encumbrance
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about encumbrance.

I feel like I’ve talked about encumbrance a lot, but it turns out I talked about it here and there in a bunch of different videos, but I don’t actually have a video about incumbrance. And I want to answer a question from clown Hustler 5815 who asks: would you share your thoughts on carry weight mechanics? I’m thinking something on the conflict between considerations like limiting the players’s progression, economy, role playing versus realism, versus forcing the player to basically stop their game and play inventory Tetris, run around to Merchants meticulously, compare weight value ratios or go throw the item on a loot Goblin pile. That’s a long question. I do have answers.

item.title: Effect Of This Channel On Me
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the effect of this channel on me.

This has been. It was an interesting question. Um, I have another video about why a lot of people I know in the industry would never make a channel like this and you can go watch it. But this one was a response. This, video, is a response to a question I got asked by sherner, who asked: since you’ve started a YouTube channel, has it changed, your mind about any part of game development or reinforced your professional experiences? Yes, in fact, it’s done several of those, but I want to kick off the one that was the biggest surprise to me, which is a personal thing that had nothing to do with how I develop or how I think of my professional experiences, and that was very soon after making this channel. I realized that many people — not all — that’s the other thing.

item.title: Elemental Mage
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. It’s Friday funay, so today I wanted to talk about an idea I had for a class called the elemental Mage.

Now this comes from my idea book, which is always near me, although it’s actually in one of the older books behind me. I had this idea a long time ago. I wrote it down in detail after I left carbine because I had designed a class for wild star called the Elementalist. That had been scrapped, but I still noodled about that idea and I just wanted to kind of get the class concept written down.

item.title: Are You Above Average?
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me Tim. Are you above average? Let’s talk about that today. I want to talk about whether or not you think you are above average at something like: are you good at, really really good at — this thing better than most other people? So let’s set aside impostor syndrome — I have a whole video on that. I’m not talking about that — those times where you feel like, oh no, someone’s GNA find out that I’m terrible and fire me.

item.title: Contractor Contracts
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about contractor contracts.

Now what I mean by that is I want to talk a little bit about if you’re thinking of doing Contracting — and I’ve only had experience in the game industry and not the Indie space at the game industry. I’m just going to talk about some things you may want to think about when you’re reviewing the contract. Now I’ve already talked about being a contract contractor, like the difference between doing contract work on a game versus being an employed member of the team on the game, so I’ll link that video below.
