I’m not going to talk about comparing a an employed member of the team versus being a contractor, and there are differences you should be aware of, so I recommend watching that video. I’ve also talked about publishing contracts, um, which are very different than contractor contracts. Publishing contracts are when you’re making a game for publisher. Those have things like royalties and breach of contract terms and monthly rates and a. Lot of things like that, and I talk about things to look out for in that video. So if you’re thinking about doing a game for a publisher, watch that one also.

item.title: Consistency In Leadership
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about consistency in leadership, and maybe a better way to put that would be: if you’re going to be a good leader, you have to be consistent.

It’s what people expect. It’s prob of the first thing you should think about yourself. If you’re thinking about stepping into a lead role or being a director or being a producer, are you con consistent? The problem with not being consistent is, if you decide differently for different people, you will either be inconsistent, which will drive people crazy, or you’ll be perceived as inconsistent, which will still drive people crazy, even if you’re not being inconsistent.

item.title: Playing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about playing games now, probably one of the most frequent questions or question types I get on this channel.

If people want to know what games I’ve played or have I played a particular game, or will I play a particular game and live stream it, or have I played a particular mod, or have I played a particular tabletop game and what I think of it. Um, and I understand why people are asking me that you’ve played it and you either really like it or really hate it for some of you and you want to talk about it, and I get that. But the primary goal of this channel, as I’ve said multiple times, is I want to talk about what game development is like and what my experience with game development is like, in case you’re thinking about going into game development or you’re in game development, because there are just a lot of issues that arise and you find yourself second guessing yourself. You. Think you’re alone in all this and I want to point out you’re not.

item.title: Toy Update
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give you a toy update.

That — and by that I mean this — is an update on a video I did a year ago where I talked about toys I was making and those are little games and not even really don’t even want to call them games. They’re like little game ideas, mostly mechanics, things like a, 3D dice roller. Uh, turn that I started turning into to a card game dice roller thing or a procedural dungeon generator and uh, I look back at that.

item.title: Capitalism
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about capitalism, but this is going to be a very rambling video and when I start it’s not going to sound like I’m talking about capitalism at all, but I am.

So the other day I was watching a video complaining about the Fallout TV show — not going to tell you which one. There’s a lot of them. People have all kinds of problems with it and I get it and I even agree with some of the problems. I just don’t agree with the degree to which that problem degrades the show. So it’s a matter of taste. Talked about taste a lot, but I started reading the comments in this one and I saw a commenter that said I only like the show because I was paid to attend.

item.title: Editing Your Game Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Axion as a hypothetical particle to explain dark matter through their ability to violate CP symmetry in Quantum chromodynamics — wait, wrong Channel.

Today, I want to talk about editing your game story. I’m not a good writer — well, I’m not a writer, especially not a good one. However, I frequently find myself giving feedback to narrative designers because of my ability. My feedback doesn’t take the form of do this, I know better, but instead I give feedback in the form of: I know what you’re trying to do and it’s not coming through now. Sometimes I know what they’re trying to do because I’ve talked to them, or it’s one of the — uh, discussed pillars we had, for here’s what I want the characters to do. Sometimes it’s just I’m playing this character and I’m like this character seems to be trying to be my friend, but they’re just coming across as a sarcastic jerk that I don’t want anywhere near me.

item.title: Creativity Under Pressure
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about creativity Under Pressure.

Now, what made me think of this, of doing This relates to a bunch of videos I’ve done before and I’m going to mention them as I go along. But I read a quote from Carl BOS, who was a band member of craftwork, and he said — quote: creativity sometimes starts with a deadline and with a lack of money and with only a few possibilities, with not too many options. Wow I that’s a good expression of doing art in a medium where just making that art costs money and making that art requires a group and making that art is part of an industry.

item.title: Game Development Roles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development roles.

Now I could have sworn I had talked about this before, but the closest I got was programming — programming roles. But I have talked a lot about what makes a good game director, traits I look for in producers and challenges that face, or challenges that programmers, artists or designers have to face. So there’s a lot of related videos and I’ve linked them all in the description, but I’m going to do this one now. I’m answering Seth, the guy GD, who asks: I was wondering if you have a video explaining the job in game development process and what they do: artists, programmers, producers, leads, designers and all that. I was having questions about that. I know that every studio is different. Could you explain it to me? I know it might be silly, their names are self-explanatory but would mean a lot.

item.title: Being A Good Storyteller
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about being a good story teller.

This is actually a response to a question from Arda Osler 631, who asks: could we get a video going over how you got to be such a good Storyteller, if that’s the appropriate term? First off, Arta, thank you, that’s really sweet to say. Um, normally I guess I wouldn’t do a whole video on this, but it’s Friday fun day, so let’s have fun. Um, at least I’ll have fun. Talk about me, okay, so I have heard that for a long time I’ve had people tell me I tell good stories. I got it in, you know, high school and college during gaming sessions. I got it a lot.

item.title: Code The Rules, Script The Exceptions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about coding rules for your game and using scripts for the exceptions.

Now, this is something that I’ve talked about. A lot came up most recently in a video I did on using Game Mechanics for storytelling, and in the comments for that video, infinite Warrior, who I think is like all the other Warriors but uncountably bigger, asked me to explain — quote - how utilizing the mechanical as opposed to the scripted approach might be able to mitigate some of that seemingly necessary kind of wonkiness — unquote - basically talking about bizarre States you can get into in some games. Now I’ve linked that video Below on Mechanics for storytelling, but I’ve also linked my video on emergent game play and another one on how to do mul, how to get multiple Quest Solutions out of your game more easily, and they all basically rely on the approach I mentioned in the title, which is try to get your system mechanics coded as simply and as directly as possible. Try to cover almost all the cases so that the rare exception is what gets scripted, not tons of the game being scripted. Script should be used surgically, saying, oh, in this one case we’re going to do this thing.

item.title: Marketing
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about marketing.

I have a LoveHate relationship with marketing, and I’m going to talk about that and I kind of need to break it up. Part of it is I’ve loved working with some marketing folks and I haven’t loved working with some marketing folks and I’ve Loved some marketing campaigns that have been done and I haven’t loved some marketing campaigns that have been done, and I just want to talk about marketing kind of in general. Now I’m on record of saying I liked the marketing that was done for The Outer Worlds. I’ve got a video that I’ll link below called marketing The Outer Worlds. What I loved about that — a quick summary — is the people I got involved with, which was, you know, private Division and then parent company take to, and then they brought in a really cool uh company to do the trailers U.

item.title: An "Off The Rails" TTRPG Campaign
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about an off the rails tabletop RPG campaign.

This is me answering a question from Wes 9353, who asked: how far off the rail have you gotten a campaign to go? Now let me clarify: this wasn’t my campaign. Uh, someone else was being the GM in this and but I was a player. There were three players: me, Dave and John. I’m not going to tell you what the module was. It was a professional module purchased with money made by a professional game designer for a professionally produced tabletop role playing game system. All I’m going to tell you was superheroes. Good luck finding out which one.

item.title: The Outer Worlds Vertical Slice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I have a very special video to show you.

It is the The Outer Worlds’s vertical slice. Before I get in, let me give you some context, and actually, before I give you context, let me thank obsidian and especially Mikey Dowling for arranging this and letting me show this to you. I want to show you the vertical slice for many reasons, and let me let me explain what a vertical slice is first, if you watched my production stages video, which I link below, I talk about the different stages that a game goes through when it’s being developed. The vertical slice stage is when you make a level of the game or a map or, in this case, an outdoor map with a few connecting Maps. I think there was a town map and three dungeon Maps.

item.title: A Non-Game Design Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a noname design spec.

It’s Friday, Funday, so this has nothing to do with game development, but it does have to do with a specification, but it’s not for a game. I’ve talked about how, when I play games, I sometimes can’t turn off the design part of my brain. So I’ll be playing a game and i’ be like H, this UI isn’t very good, or uh, you know, I can’t believe they put this sound effect and it doesn’t match. Or sometimes, even though I’m not very much of an art person, I will occasionally see a piece of art, whether it’s a piece of scenery or a creature, and go really with all the other things they put in. They put in this thing which doesn’t seem to match.

item.title: Authority Without Responsibility
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Authority without responsibility.

Now I’m going to explain what I mean, but this is sort of a response to two of my own videos that have become very popular — my caution videos — and I will link them below where I talk about my concern that there’s a problem in the game industry: that developers are much too C cautious. They don’t spend time exploring different feature ideas. They overestimate their time on things. They want to polish things early.

item.title: My Worst Job Interview
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my worst job interview.

Now I’ve done videos where I’ve talked about job interviews from the hiring side, about questions I like to ask and tests I like to give. I’ve even talked about questions I’ve received while looking for a job and those videos are linked below. But today I want to talk about the absolute worst experience I ever had looking for a job, and this came out about because I was talking with colleagues in the industry about their worst job interviews and these horrible ones came up about, you know, sexist remarks and really long tests that were work for the project they were interviewing for — just bad questions. And it reminded me that I usually told one job interview story that happened just a few years ago, but I but I realized I had another really awful one that happened near the beginning of my career. So I’m tell you both of them.

item.title: Content As Reward
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about content as reward.

Now this is a really great topic. It was suggested by ulyses saeo, who said a suggestion for a video content as reward. This is a major overlooked thing in Game Dev, in my humble opinion, which I feel massively. Railroad player action: I agree. Uh, it’s.

I can’t believe I’ve never talked about this and you’re right, I’ve never. I never have, so let me talk about what it is, why I like it, why you don’t see it much, and a possible solution which you may not like. So let me Define what I think ulyses meant by this and what I’m going to take it as meaning.

item.title: How The Internet Changed Game Dev
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how did the internet change game development?

This was a question from oonu bogu- 812 — I hope I got that number right. Who said? Who asked: how did the internet change game development in the game industry in general? I remember you saying CD stories about the disc not working and that you made a lot of buggy games. So how would vampire Arcanum be coming out today? Or in what aspects are we better or worse?

item.title: Two Recent Mementos
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about two recent momentos I now have — and they’re behind me, and I’ll Point them out in a few minutes.

This is a Friday fun day so I thought I’d do something fun. I’ve had a lot of people ask about all the momentos that are in these two cabinets — memorabilia from my career days — and I’ve talked about a few of them. I talked about the Fallout foam core board, the really big one.

item.title: Arcanum "Dat" Files
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanum’s dat files.

Dat yesterday’s video was all about all the data that Arcanum had. I was talking about how much. We had a lot of data and we had to store it, and if you’ve ever tried to mess around in the Arcanum folders, you’re noticed that the data, most of the data — is kept in big monolithic files whose extension is dat for data. I want to talk about real quickly how we did that and then why we did that. Um, because it makes sense, if you’re just curious about making a game, or if you’re curious about modding a game or patching a game, because there’s a whole bunch of reasons.

item.title: Arcanum Data
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum data.

So the last few weeks — well, since I’ve I I posted this almost a year ago, I think — since I converted Arcanum to be a Windows, a modern Windows app, it’s way easier to debug it I can put break points anywhere. I can run the game in a window, you know, hit the break points wherever I want, look at what is going on, because you know it’s been 25 years more since code started on that game. I don’t remember how everything works and I didn’t write everything. There were three other programmers, so I’ve been going through it for various reasons, and one of them I was doing recently was I was trying to see how the data flow was handled, basically what was loaded when in the game. And first of all, let me just say there is a lot of data in this game. I look at it and I have to remind myself that the entire game was made by essentially 14 people. Yes, there’s a lot more people in the credits, but you know I’m not counting people who did localization and production and you know, uh made the boxes and handled advertisements and things like that. I’m just talking about the actual development team. It was 14 people.

item.title: Conflating Player Skill With Character Skill
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about conflating player skill with player character skill.

Now I’m answering a question by Vol forac, who asks how to resolve the conflict between player skill and their character’s ability in an action RPG. To elaborate with an example: Fallout in the past was all about skill checks and dice rolls, but starting with Fallout 3, the first person shooter aspect became more emphasized with each release. Where’s the line between letting the player be a good shot because the game mechanics allow them and the player being a good shot because they built a character? That is a good shot. Um, first of all, that’s a good question.

item.title: Morale Improvement
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about morale Improvement.

Specifically, I want to answer a question from rhinosaur 77 who asked: I’d love to see a video on how you would keep team morale up during stressful times at work. The chocolate tasty meetings you had were great and the bacon Ferry was a highlight during work. Even if the fun police didn’t like it, really helped us keep a bit more sane. So I’ve never done a video on morale, um, and I have a couple caveats about that. But let me just mentioned that rhinosaurus 77 and I work together at carbine, so he’s somebody who knows me and he’s also a master Channel member. So I’m filming this on a Thursday morning. Since Friday’s already unlocked, this will be Monday’s video anyway.

item.title: IP Idea: Magic Costs Dearly
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about another IP idea from my idea book.

Although I don’t think it’s in this one, I think it’s in one in my bookcase. This is the idea that magic should cost the Caster dearly. Now, this is an idea that’s been in my book since troa did the Lord of the Rings demo back in 2001, and some of you may already see what the connection is and I’ll talk about it when I when I get there. But what made me think about this IP idea was most fantasy RPGs have reduced magic to something almost mundane. It’s no different than a fighter swinging a sword or a thief picking a lock. It’s just a thing that someone does and it usually is just an excuse to have a different damage type and some particle effects. It’s — it’s explained away as in know magic, and I always thought that that was something that missed out a lot on what could be done with magic. And, to be fair, um, this has been like this since the original DN D. I mentioned that my original D and campaign was based on Roberty Howard’s Conan campaign, but that campaign was fairly low magic.

item.title: Fallout's Effect On Me
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fallout’s effect on me, which seems like a very personal, self-reflected, reflective, almost narcissistic topic.

But I’m answering someone’s question. Super duper asks: did you ever feel like sometimes your career was sort of defined by Fallout, like obviously it was your what we would call, in the film industry, breakout role, with it being such a massively successful and popular game? But do you ever feel like being tied to Fallout held you back or gave you opportunities you otherwise would not have had, and just for funsies, with it being such an impactful and influential game? Would you care toine on a world wherein Fallout had been cancelled and you think they might have, and how you think that might have affected our culture?

item.title: Lessons By Company
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what lessons I learned by company.

I’ve, in my entire 43 years now, I’ve only worked at five companies, um as an employee — and one of them was my own, and I’ve already done a couple videos on Lessons Learned. The two biggest ones were the biggest lesson I ever learned, which, just to you know, if you didn’t watch it’s that people lie and a lot of people will. Of people lie and a lot of people will lie right to your face. You going to go watch that video.

item.title: Beyond Fighting, Sneaking, and Talking
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to brainstorm ideas with you on ways RPGs can move past the fighting, sneaking, talking Triad that, let’s face it, has been done to death since the 90s.

I mean, yeah, that’s kind of what we pushed really hard with. Fallout was: can you fight past things, can you sneak past things, can you talk past things? But Fallout was made in 1997.

item.title: Game Development Tunnel Vision
item.description: Sal SE, Tim, par gotcha. I bet you right now some of you are reaching for that YouTube translate button and some of you were thinking, whoa, Tim’s French accent is terrible, trust me, that’s been said. No, I’m doing this video about game development, tunnel vision, because it’s one of the things that occurred to me. I get a lot of questions about a lot of things and early on, when there were less of you, I would answer and say, oh, that’s not something I know or I can’t talk about it I did do a video on things I can’t or won’t answer, but since then there’s been a lot more questions that Encompass a lot more things, even about game development or tangentially related game development, that I literally cannot answer. I either don’t know it or I’m not qualified to answer.

item.title: The Outer Worlds: Eight Playthroughs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about eight playthroughs of The Outer Worlds.

Now I do have a video where I talk about play testing The Outer Worlds and I say I did it 16 times and I give a very high level overview of the playthroughs I did. I was digging through archives and I found much more detail on the first eight playthroughs. I did so, if this seems familiar, I’ll link the other video below, which is a very high level, but I’m going to actually read what I wrote for that also when I’m done. I found a list of purchase requests. It was a document I kept.

item.title: Temple Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s do the Temple of Elemental Evil development timeline.

Now a lot of people liked my Fallout and Arcanum development timelines and they’re linked below. Although it’s interesting, a lot of people comment on them, comment on them, but they’re not well watched compared to my other videos. I don’t know if it’s because they’re long, but I just wanted to point out that a lot of you say you love them, but a lot of you don’t watch them, which is a perfect example of why sometimes the vocal people are not the majority and do not represent a majority opinion. Apparently there’s a lot of you out there who think these are boring, so let me give you a caveat ahead of time. I don’t know how long this is going to be. I made a lot of notes, but keep in mind that Temple was done in half the time of Fallout and Arcanum. Those two games took three and a half years. This game took a year and a half. Plus two months, so almost exactly 50% of the time. Also, for the LA, last 20% of this game’s development.

item.title: Gameplay Options
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gameplay options now.

A few months ago I did a video on game difficulty settings and talked about the pros and cons of different difficulty settings you should put in the game. But in the comment section a bigger discussion happened about just gameplay options in general — way more than just difficulty settings — and one of the things that came up was people talking about games that have a lot of gameplay settings. Gameplay options now there are people who like them and they like them for the obvious reasons of you know. I like to be able to customize the game exactly how, I want to play it, which I get, and I probably fall in that category a lot more than the people who do not like having lots of gameplay options.

item.title: The Business Side Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the business side of game development.

This is an answer to a question by Seth, the guy GD, who asks: I was wondering if you could explain the business side of the game industry. I know you started troa. I would love to hear the business stuff about it I know you have a video about troa, but could you go further into the money aspect of it? Could you explain starting up a studio and dealing with hiring people, choosing and approaching Publishers, getting funding for the game, Insurance, banking, PR, office space costs, advertising and other things people wouldn’t think of?

item.title: Publisher Contracts
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about contracts, specifically the contracts you make with Publishers to make games.

And this is a response to TS 7901 who asked I would like to, who said I would like to hear more of the business side of your stories. So here we go now. Let me put a big caveat at the beginning of this. Bear in mind I don’t think I’m a good businessman. I do not think that I particularly ran the side of tra well and not like the accounting, paying bills side, but the dealing with Publishers and getting good contract side. So take a lot of this with a grain of salt, but also take it with uh from The View that I often think I learn more from failure than success and I pretty much failed here. So I know some things to look out for. But anyway, let me start by talking about Publishers. Publishers are not your enemy. I know a lot of you like to immediately take the employer bad, publisher bad, everything, bad side, so let me just say they’re not your enemy, they are your partners. You are trying to make a product with them.

item.title: Game Flavor
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game flavor, and I’m deliberately doing this on a Friday, Funday, because what I’m going to talk about is very subjective.

So kind of view: this, as a Tim talks about game development at another, very high level of description. Uh, and I almost didn’t do this video because what I mean by game flavor is really hard to describe. I’m going to try to do it, but it’s hard.

item.title: Core Game Loops
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about core game Loops.

This is something that’s absolutely fundamental to game development. I’m incredibly embarrassed that I’ve had this channel for more than a year and I’ve never talked about this. And I bring it UPC. I bring that up because it’s so fundamental. I don’t even think about it.

I’ve mentioned this a few times about other things that it doesn’t occur to me to talk about certain things because it’s just so like endemic to the process and I’ve been doing it for so long. I don’t even doesn’t even occur to me that this is something I should mention. But I was looking at something online and they were talking about core game loops and I’m like, oh my goodness, I have not even mentioned core game Loops. So let’s talk about this, because this is a core game Loop.

item.title: High Level Design Direction
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about highlevel design Direction.

This is something that the game director, creative director, whatever it’s called — on your project. This is probably the single most important task that the person in this role does. I’m going to talk about it, but it’s also something that I think is misunderstood or underestimated by a lot of people who aren’t in the role and by people who first step into the role. Um, and what I mean by highle Design direction is you basically have to watch everything going into the game, everything: system mechanics, story elements, quests, Maps, dialogue, NPC, the names of things, lore, text, everything — to make sure it all fits. And that highle — whatever view, whatever you know your take on — your vision of this game should be: all that stuff has to fit and serve that Vision.

item.title: Personality Roleplay
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about personality roleplay.

This is the answer to a question from a channel member. Interestingly, they asked the question and I wanted to think about it because I wanted to make sure I answered it correctly, and also since I don’t write dialogues much anymore, I wanted to make sure I’d framed my answer correctly. Anyway, vasenkov asked: lately I’ve been thinking a lot, like a lot, about something I call personality roleplay. Perhaps designers have their own term for it. I’m talking about conversations with multiple reply options that ultimately or immediately lead to the same outcome and are basically just different flavors of the same reply.

item.title: Writing Evil Characters
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing evil characters.

Specifically, I want to answer a question, a second question, uh, from Nash kruka, who asks: how, where do you find yourself wanting to draw the line for evil options in an RPG? Similarly to how I find myself having a hard time choosing the evil option in a game, I find myself having a hard time writing them? Most games I’ve played have far fewer Bad Karma options than good. Maybe one evil dialogue to the half dozen good dialogue options. How do you weigh in on this? Okay, Nash, I know you’re asking about how to write dialogue for the player where it’s evil. Um, the way I think about this is: how would I write a good evil NPC?

item.title: The Number Of Vaults In Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the number of vaults in Fallout.

It’s Friday, fun day, so I want to talk about some fun. This is just me talking about It’s not canon — things I say aren’t Canon, but I thought it’ be fun to revisit this after my video months ago about the Vault being used as experiments, which I’ll get to. So the original design for vaults was that there was going to be a thousand, and the only reason this pin was even placed in the original game was when Leonard was doing the Vault suit. He asked me how many digits he’d need to keep on the back, because we had vault 13. But he wanted to know, you know, are they going to be Vault? You know 106, is there going to be Vault?

item.title: Selling A Game Design Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about selling a game design spec.

This is actually from a question I got from Nash kruka, I hope, kka. I’m hope I’m pronouncing that right. Um, I’ll say Nash from now on, because it looks like you may have changed your name on the account since when you asked this question and when I’m answering it. But Nash asked: if someone had the entire Wiki for a game like Fallout New Vegas and approached a studio, would it even mean anything to them? Ideas are cheap, but what is a fully thought out game iname Universe worth?

item.title: How To Remedy Problematic Behavior
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to remedy problematic behaviors and these are on people on a team you’re on, in fact.

I’m going to talk about, kind of what kind of problematic behaviors you may encounter, what to do when you’re peer level with them, what to do if you’re above them. This actually comes from a uh view uh commenter and actually a channel member. Father Al win asks — you have Loosely discussed this prior, but when working within a team, what practices and habits that certain devs demonstrate do you consider problematic and how do you go about remedying them as both an equal team member and as a team lead? Okay, father aldwin, that’s a good question and because you are a channel member, it is being bumped up into the queue. So you asked on Saturday. I’m filming this on Sunday. Monday and Tuesdays have already unlocked for the master members, so this will be Wednesdays. Let’s get to it, first, let me Define problematic Behavior. It’s really hard for me to Define it. Um, it’s one of those things that you know. It’s like that definition of porn: it’s hard to Define, but I know it when I see it.

item.title: Time Estimation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about time estimation.

I’ve had received a lot of requests over the last year for people asking for a good method to estimate tasks, because this is a frequent contentious point between people and either the people they manage or their managers, where time estimates are just off and then upset situations happen. I’m going to talk about what I consider to be a good way of making estimates and problems of over and underestimating. Now a lot of people have asked. The most recent request was from aen Targaryen 1390, who asked: can you please do a video on time estimate, estimations and planning and how to handle situations where the boss says it needs to be done in X days and you say it will take 3x days, okay, one reason I put off answering this question for a long time is it’s very, very complicated.

item.title: Idea Battles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about idea battles.

This comes from a question asked by triplicata, who asked: hey, Tim, bit of a long-winded question I’ve had for a while now. In previous videos you’ve talked about sitting in a room for a long time having arguments in quotes about creative decisions. However, they’re not really fights where people are upset, but rather passionate debates where people go to bat for their ideas. I love those types of discussions. I like to call them idea battles. My question is: do you have any favorite idea battles or ones that stand out to you? Times where you enjoyed the conversation, times where you thought you were right but it turned out the other idea was better, or maybe times where the discussion led to a new idea that was better than the ones originally argued?

item.title: Unused GDC 1999 Roadtrip Presentation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about RPG 2000.

This is a presentation that I was going to give at the 1999 GDC road trip in Austin. Let me give you some context. I was going through my archives and on the inside — the archive of the very first machine I had at troyka — I think I had three different machines — I found a PowerPoint called GDC 99 road trip. I remember making this PowerPoint vaguely, but I did not go to the GDC 99 road trip convention in Austin, which leads me to believe — because, by the way, there were no other notes, no notes on this talk, no notes about the talk, no emails back and forth — that either I made this and never submitted it, or I submitted it or didn’t get in, or maybe even I got in and decided not to go. I have no memory, and what’s weird about this is I would have been making this in early summer of 99, as if I wasn’t busy enough. We were.

item.title: Ideas For A Generic Fantasy RPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about some ideas for a generic fantasy RPG.

Now I’ve already done some videos where I’ve talked my way through some notes I had on very specific IPS. These are IPS with you know, like I’ve always talked about, with defined settings and then a story, and then I’ve hinted at the mechanics I want to do.

But I found a document deep, in my archives. This one, this, the source of this document is troa in the early 2000s.

item.title: Channel Memberships
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Channel memberships.

Yeah, I’m going to start Channel memberships on Canon games, and I know what you’re thinking. Why Channel memberships? Why? Now? I can answer that this channel has expanded Way Beyond where I thought it would be. I thought this channel would be me telling my stories to a few hundred, maybe a thousand of you.

Well, that’s not what happened. Currently — and this is, uh, late June of 2024 — currently I’m getting almost a million views a month and 100, almost 150,000 people have chosen to subscribe, so they’re getting the videos notifications, uh, when a new video comes up. I’m getting a lot of comments, several hundred a day, and a lot of those have questions in them that people want me to answer, and I’m trying my hardest. I’m keeping up reading all of them, but I can’t always answer all of them. I just don’t have the time, well, I’ve mentioned I do client work. One of my clients is winding. Down, the work for them is winding down, they’re.

item.title: Writing Good NPCs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing good NPCs.

Now I’m going to start with the big caveat that I’ve said on here many times: I am not a good writer. I’ve tried to be good, I’ve worked on It’s something I can improve past a certain limited amount of. I can write lore text. I can write short narration, but personal one-on-one dialogue — no, I’m not that good at, but I am good at seeing good writing of that category.

item.title: Would I Work On Fallout Again?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer the question: would I work on Fallout again?

This: in the last few months this question has overtaken my most frequently Asked question — how do I get a job in the game industry — which I have made a video with an answer and then a video about the answer. So I’ve done — how to get a job in the game industry — and then making a demo which is the answer to that. So I want to answer. What I work on followed again, but I have to realize, you have to realize the answer is complicated, but not because of what you think. First of all, there’s a lot of different questions being asked. There.

item.title: So You Want To Make Games?
item.description:
It’s me, Tim, so you want to make games today?

I want to answer a question from zaching Tut who asks: I was wondering if you could do a video about things to consider if you’re thinking about getting into the gaming industry, H? Um, this is kind of a big question. Uh, the first thing I want to do is say that I’m to answer this question is really me asking you a bunch of questions. It’s a lot of questions that only you, each individual watching this, can answer. I’m going to break it down into two categories. First, I think you need to ask yourself if you even want to go into the game industry.

item.title: Coming Out Stories
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about some coming out stories that I have.

Yeah, I’m going to talk about being gay. This is the second of hundreds of videos I have where I’ve done that, but it’s Friday. It’s Friday, funay — right in the middle of June pride month, so I’m going to do it. If this makes you feel Oogie and you guys know who you are, just skip it and I’ll see you Monday. I’m not going to go on it, go over anything — game development today. So bye-bye anyway, so if you watched my other video about being a gay game developer, I didn’t come out until my 30s, which, just to put things in perspective, meant I was very closeted in the 80s and 90s when I was a teenager and a young man.

item.title: About My Notes, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my notes again.

I’m always mentioning my notes. I’ve done a whole video about my notes, linked below. It’s called about my notes, um, but I see a lot of comments. I shouldn’t say a lot. I see a frequent number of comments where people think, because I tell stories and there are people in them doing not nice things, that I’m still angry or that I’m holding a lot of grudges or that I tend to blame people a lot. And I don’t know what to do about the last one, because I think I’ve said a lot of things where I blame me and the things I’ve done badly. All I can tell you is one: I don’t really feel like there’s any point in staying angry about things and holding grudges, and I really don’t do that and I’m not doing that, despite what it may seem.

item.title: The Canceling of Fallout Van Buren...And Me
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the cancellation of Fallout Van Buren and me.

I was watching a documentary — and I’ll link it below — on the development of interplace Fallout by bun, which was 2B, Fallout 3 — it was going to be their first 3D engine Fallout and what happened? And it was a great documentary and I was like Wow, someone’s collected a lot of the facts all in one place, but there was one fact missing that I expected to be missing, which was my involvement in its cancellation. Now this is a story I’ve held off on saying. It’s like what I’m more than a year into this Channel and I held it off for a few reasons.

item.title: Fallout As A Work Of Art
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout as a work of art, and just before I get started, I’m talking about the whole series.

I’m talking about all the games and the TV show. I’m talking about it as an IP. Um, I’ve already done a video about games as art. I’ll link it below. You can kind of see this video as a continuation of that video, because I’m taking some of my ideas from that video and I’m kind of talking about it in more detail. Um, also, I want to clarify that making a game, actual game development — not the end product, but the development that itself — is both an art and a science, and I mean I this is something that is behind all the videos I do, and I’m constantly talking about this, so I’m not going to rehash it, but I will say this: people who try to treat Tre, the development process, like it was Art, are shocked when money factors in, are like: oh my.

item.title: Working In Teams
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about working in teams — something you will almost inevitably have to do if you go in the game industry — and I want to tell you why I’m talking about this.

So last night, poking around Netflix, I found a Duran Duran documentary. Now, I was really big in Duran Duran in the 80s and then they made this comeback in the 90s and it was interesting to watch the history of it.

item.title: Pets
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about pets.

It’s Friday, Funday, and I’ve had a lot of people ask me two different questions. The first was: what?

What are elements of your game were very personal to you, and why are there so many dogs in your game? And the answer to both of those questions are pets, or, specifically, animals. Animals have been really important in my games. They’ve been really important throughout my life in my family.

item.title: Touchy Subjects
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about touchy subjects.

I like that. This is actually me answering a question from sherner who asks: hey, Tim, I would like to know how do you handle touchy subjects in your games — religion, politics, violence, Etc. How do you know if you’re pushing too hard or going too far on those things? Or even should we bother about not going to those places at all?

item.title: Deep Learning
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about deep learning.

Now I’m going to tell you what I mean by that and where I learned about that. But as a quick aside, I did a video once about how I do humor in games and I said I often embed humor in my games in such a way that the people get it, get it and feel included. But the people who don’t get it don’t even know there was a joke and they don’t feel excluded. So I’m going to rip away the wall for a second here, the way I said deep learning. There are a few people that I used to hang out with at interplay that right now are going, but most of you are like I have no idea.

item.title: Fallout And Wasteland
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout and Wasteland.

Not Wasteland 2 or three, I’m talking about the original 1988 Wasteland. The reason I want to talk about this is I see a lot of confusing comments online about the influence that Wasteland had on Fallout. Some people think it was a direct influence. Some people think it was a really strong influence. Some people say I said it was an influence, so let me talk about that. I did briefly touch on this in a video I did on a link it in the description on Fallout influences and very clearly in there I say Wasteland was an influence, but I also say it wasn’t strongly an influence. Um, so let me go into more detail about that and right now I can just say, if people go, was wayon an influence on Fallout?

item.title: My Own Struggles With Nuance
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Nuance again.

Now I already have two videos about nuance and trying to keep an open and fair mind about things, but this video is going to be about my own struggles with doing that, because it’s hard to do. I’ve had trouble with it I think a lot of you have trouble with it. A lot of my co-workers have trouble with It’s just.

item.title: Brushes With Famous People
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my brushes with famous people.

I — this is, first of all, this is a Friday funay talk — doesn’t have much to do with development, but it occurred while I was a developer, but it’s fun and I I think it’s cool. So maybe you’d like hearing some of the behind the scenes, like celebrity people that I got to meet. Now, some of them I met and talked with some of them. I just saw um and wished I over and talk to um. But looking back on it, I’ve mentioned here and there in videos people I’ve famous people, celebrities that I’ve talked to and I realized, wow, I’ve actually seen and talked to a lot of famous people, uh, over my career. But I think that just happens when you have a really long career and you’re old.

item.title: Being Underestimated
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about being underestimated.

This is kind of the opposite of impostor syndrome. Did a whole video about It’s linked below: um, impostor syndrome is when you feel like you’re not good enough for you know, for whatever you’re trying to do, and I’ve talked about that and I’ve talked about I’ve have felt imposter syndrome. I felt it way further into my career than a lot of people probably thought.

item.title: Why I Directed One Last Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I directed one last time and specifically what I mean by that is why I stepped up to be a game director on The Outer Worlds, even after I said I wi it.

This is to answer a question from zombified gamer series who asks: I’m curious on what exactly happened that allowed you to step up and direct the The Outer Worlds. You mentioned that something happened and healed you over time. I’m genuinely curious if you could tell what that story on how you overcome, overcame the demoralization that you went through.

item.title: Learning Design From Other Disciplines
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about learning design from other disciplines, and by disciplines I can mean Hobbies, activities, anything you do besides video games.

So this is an answer to the question from Ezra Stanton, 8685, who asks: I’m curious, what other artistic disciplines do you think have the most to teach us about game design and what did it teach you about game design? Examples: drawing, painting, writing, animation, dancing, making, music, cooking, Etc. Ooh, that’s a good question. Um, I’ll talk about some of my hobbies or other things I do, and then I’ll talk to about, kind of how that all comes together in my design aesthetic, my design motifs — I’m not sure what I’d call it. Um, first of all, let’s talk about reading. I do a lot of reading, um, I’ve talked about some of it already on here, and books are amazing in that, you know, they can go anywhere, describe anything. In that sense they’re beyond movies, they’re beyond TV shows and because of that I get. A lot of neat ideas, especially when I’m reading some novel story with an interesting setting or interesting mechanic and I think, how would I do that in a video game? I talk about this in the video I have about Roger El’s Lord of Light and how I would turn it into a crpg.

item.title: "Git Gud"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about the phrase get good.

I hate this phrase. Actually there’s a phrase I used to hate more, called n said um. People would frequently on the net, in Internet news groups, end a post with enough said, as if they had said everything that was possible to say on the possible that was possible to say on the topic and there was no need for a reply because it’s been exhausted. Um, and then if someone did reply, and then they replied, I’d often see people go. I thought you said enough was said. I thought you said you’d said enough. So I I think that’s why that one went away. But this get good phrase, well, let’s.

item.title: Snapshots Through My Career
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to do something fun that I’m going to call snapshots through my career, and

this is kind of this kind of came up because I’ve done a few videos already: one where I showed scenes of a life in game development, which was a lot of pictures that mostly I took during game development — um, except for all the ones of that people took of me at my desk — and I’ve also done a video on data about my games and I’ll link those below, but they’re not very personal. So what I wanted to do was show you me at every stage of my career — I guess I’d call it like every game I made. So what I did is I went through all the photos — I have thousands of them — and I found a photo of me taken during the development of each one of my games. So I’m going to talk about the game and the year it came out and then show a picture of me at that. Age, so I thought it would be fun. It’s nothing huge, so feel free to skip it, but people seem to like the other ones so I thought I’d do this one.

item.title: Arcanum Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s walk through the Arcanum development timeline.

Few weeks ago — actually may even be a couple months ago — I did the Fallout development timeline. People really like that. In fact, stavos Nicola asked: can you do an Arcanum one as well? And I was like, yes, um, interestingly about doing this. So this timeline was a lot harder for me to put together than the Fallout. One was basically looking at my notes and ordering them chronologically.

item.title: Games Predicting Our Future
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about games predicting our future.

In particular, I want to ask, I want to answer several questions that G works AKA Nicole asked: I’ve been curious for years how all of you creators get that close to the truth and our reality. Just how do you, one, come up with the ideas for the story of your games? Two, what things in current life help you come up with the future in games? And three, do you have some crystal ball joke?

item.title: Relinquishing Creative Control
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about relinquishing creative control.

I’m going to answer a question from mjy media who asks: hey, Tim question, do you have tips on relinquishing creative control? I wrote a ttrpg book a couple years ago and handing off my writing to artists was the hardest thing to do. I had so many notes but I didn’t want to smother the artists. Some artist art didn’t turn out true to my vision and I’m so somewhat unhappy with around 20% of the art. I’m writing another book Now. Tips for this next round.

item.title: Troika's Conan The Barbarian Proposal
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about another game proposal I made.

This is back in June of 2004. I’ll talk about why I made it and what it was, but it was for. It was for the Conan, the Barbarian RPG. Um, I was doing some going through my files for reasons, and I found all kinds of interesting things, um, but one of the things I assembled across were was a cache of a bunch of proposals that we had written when vampire, the uh Masquerade bloodlines was winding down. I’ve talked about one of them, the B’s Gate 3 proposal, but this was one I totally forgot about This was entirely written by me. I will read you some of it, but let me give you a little backstory.

item.title: Favorite Chocolate
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about my favorite chocolate.

I did a chocolate video a few weeks ago. It was very well received. Um, a lot of people asked me — because my chocolate Blog has over 2,000 entries — what my favorite chocolates were. I’m happy to talk about chocolate and it’s Friday, so let’s do a fun Friday video. I’m going to start with my absolute favorite chocolate bar — just bar none. Then I’m going to talk about what I think the best brands are. I tried to make a top five but there was a four-way tie for the fifth slot.

item.title: Shopping Cart Theory
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about shopping cart Theory.

This is for narrative game narrative designers or budding game narrative designers out there. It’s just something for you to think about. It’s a very interesting way to think about and frame morality, both in real life and in video games. The shopping cart theory is a very simple philosophical situation that illustrates Ates people’s moralities in a very Stark, clear, simple, clean way. So of course, I love it because it’s simple, but it makes you think things and you realize quickly things aren’t as simple and straightforward as you might think. So what is shopping cart Theory? Basically what this is.

item.title: Games As Actions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about games as actions, and I’ll talk about what I mean by that.

But first I want to talk about the film director, Deni fenov. I really like his movies — you know arrival Dune. I like his cinematography, I like his approach to making movies, I like that his movies — and he’s talked about this — are about exploring the human condition, about making us think about big issues, and also that his movies are open — open to interpretation. I mean that’s like a checkbox of everything I like. But he had a quote that I heard recently and I really liked it, but it made me think a lot about games, and here’s the quote: I strive for visual storytelling, for images that convey meaning without relying on dialogue. Now, what I love about that is not only it a great line, but it also points out a way that movies transcend books. I mean books are great. I read a ton of books, I have a lot of books. I love reading them, when I read them I, you know, imagine all kinds of things in my head. But books are primarily words and when you are talking about books you tend to quote them. You tend to quote lines that you love, even if the lines are making you imagine something or lines are transporting you somewhere. Books are basically remembered from for having great lines.

item.title: Using Mechanics For Storytelling
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using Mechanics for storytelling, and

this is an answer to a question by Benjamin nun 11. Can you talk about gaming gameplay mechanics as storytelling devices? By that I mean where gameplay mechanic is directly reinforcing a character’s connection with the setting or the story.

So, uh, Benjamin, I love this question. It’s the main reason I like to do mechanic after deciding the setting and story, because I think mechanics should support those. Did a video about people who do mechanics.

item.title: The Horizontal Slice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the horizontal slice.

I will explain what I mean by that. I’m actually going to answer a question from John flyer 4873, who asks: do you have a particular balance of quest content that you try to reach? Is there a process that goes into determining how long SL complex the main quest is versus how many side quests there are and where they are in your world? Let me just say that I didn’t used to have any process for that, it was just feel it as you go, and you can kind of see that in Fallout and Arcanum, where there are big chunks of the world that don’t have anything in them, um, there are, there are clusters of places where there are a lot more quests than other places. Um, Temple, of course, was made um off a module and, even though we had trouble with some areas were too dense.

item.title: Fallout Easter Eggs In Other Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout, Easter eggs in other games.

I had a lot of fun doing a video a few months ago about Easter eggs in games in my games that referred, like um, to things we were talking about, like hidden Easter eggs in my games. But I also wanted to talk about times when I found Fallout being referenced in other people’s games. So I’m going to skip games that had Fallout stuff that was overtly connected, like you can buy Fallout skins in Minecraft, um, the new Magic, the Gathering cards that are Fallout based. I mean those, aren’t Easter eggs, those are overtly based on Fallout. But I’m also going to skip games from related companies.

item.title: Subverting Tropes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about subverting tropes because I did a video on tropes that I think get overused.

I’ll link that below, and dope nasty wanted to know if there were any tropes. I did like, so, okay, in that video I talked about how overuse of tropes bore me. Um, some people called me out and saying, hey, you’ve used those tropes yourself and I’m like, okay, I did. To be clear, well, first of all, the way I’ve done things has shifted over the decades. So, yeah, if you look at a game that’s 20 or 30 years old, you may go, hey, you did that there. I’m like, yeah, and I kind of wish I hadn’t. Um, but to be clear, I also said I, I usually let people get one use of these.

item.title: Game Development Contracting
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development Contracting.

This is to answer a question from Guerilla suplex who asks: what is it like Consulting for companies? Also, how involved are you with the development process when Consulting?

Good question, um, just to give a summary, up until July of 2020, I was an employee at obsidian. I had moved up to Seattle intending to remain an employee, but when I got up there they said: oh, we can’t do that. Uh, they weren’t set up yet to do to have employees out of state, so I switched to Contracting. This was not the first time I had contracted, um. If you watched my career summary video, I mentioned when I was at cybron — Pegasus was originally called and then it changed it same to cybron — when I was a teenager and in college, that was all part-time work during Summers and breaks and it was pretty much Contracting. And then when I started at interplay in 1991, I was a contractor. So I made barale construction, set for them under a 14-week contract and then when that ended, they hired me full-time in January of 92. So pretty much since ‘92 I’ve been an employee until 2020. Uh, I’m, 2020 was a weird Year all around, but so what I want to do, like I often do, is I will talk about pros and cons of Contracting so you can decide for yourself whether it’s better or worse than being an employ, being an employe of a company. Um, because it’s really up to you what kind of person you are and what risks you want to take and how good you are at uh, finding clients. So first I’ll do the pros of being a contractor, uh, first, you and — and I I mentioned this, uh, just to start off — don’t accept an exclusive contract. Um, most places won’t do this, but if someone does, say they want you as a contractor and they want you to be exclusive — meaning you can’t work anywhere else you — and you need to decide whether you want to do that — um, I would definitely ask for more money.

item.title: Another New IP Idea
item.description:
Hi everyone, this is Tim. Today I want to talk about another new IP idea.

I really had a fun time, um, in a video and I’ll link it below the video I did, uh, few weeks ago, where I talked about my idea for a new post aoch game where you played a little kid who survived some kind of Apocalypse, but and I had fun talking about that, but that was mostly setting and story. So I dug into my book and I found another game idea that I liked, that I never did anything with, so I’m going to throw it out here. And what’s different about this one is the setting is somewhat standard, but the it has a uni, unique mechanic that I think would make a really cool hook. Um, at least I think it’s Unique. I don’t know of any game that does this, so let’s talk about it. So this game is would be a fairly standard Fantasy game. Think, you know, Temple of Elemental Evil, think Arcanum without the Industrial Revolution. Think Lord of the Rings, think just a very standard Fantasy game.

item.title: Understanding Game Design Choices
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about understanding game design choices.

Specifically, I want to talk about why designers sometimes choose the feature set that they have for their game, and I want to talk about it because there are a lot of comments I see on the channel from people basically saying I don’t like this, I wish they hadn’t done this, was a bad choice, and it’s kind of like in that video I had about bad games. I want to talk about why games are sometimes made with choices the way they are that you yourself do not like and do not agree with. Um, now, remember I’ve said frequently too many videos to link to that. Whenever you have a question about something, the answer is money. So you know, why is this game like this money? Why didn’t they do this money? Why did they do this money? So, in essence, my answer for this question of why this design Choice was made is probably money, um.

item.title: This Is How You Roleplay
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a channel I discovered that really showed me

this is how to role playay. I figured it’s Friday, let’s have some fun. And I’m going to talk about this YouTube channel. I found it’s called games on hard mode.

Maybe you already know about it. I’m going to link everything below. Um, I basically discovered this yesterday. I watched one and then I watched several more and then I grabbed a link and I sent it to Leonard Bari and said: you have to watch this guy play games and I’m going to show you some clips. I’m going to talk about why I love it, each, clip.

item.title: Influential Books
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about influential books.

Now I’ve already talked about tabletop influences on my games during my career and what games I think are really good for you to learn from, along with my top five favorite games, and I separated those into different videos because I thought that was important to do, even though some people still think the ones that I think are good to learn from are my favorites. Um, but today I wanted to talk about what books exist that really influenced me, and some of them I read a long time ago. Some of them I’ve read recently. All of them I’ve reread recently. These are books that have stuck with me since when I read them originally.

item.title: Bartle's Player Archetypes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about B’s player archetypes, which is also known as the ble taxonomy of players.

This is in response to someone, J extra 1313, who asks: hi, Tim, there’s a well-known rule that there’s four types of players: Killers, social players, explorers and Achievers — the ble taxonomy of player types. Therefore, every game should have things in it: appease each player type to bring in the largest audience possible. How true is this taxonomy in your experience, and is following this philosophy helpful or harmful? Well, I’m glad you asked because this is something that I’ve known about for decades and I kind of agree with and I will talk about that. And, by the way, there’s a whole bunch of really interesting psychology. Um, you can read to learn more about video games. In fact, there is a book I read in the 80s by George and Elizabeth Loftus called the psychology of video games and even though, yeah, it was talking about Pac-Man and Space Invaders — all of it still applies today, so highly recommend that book.

item.title: Why I Hate Cultural References
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I hate cultural references.

Now, I almost said why I hate them in games, but I also hate them in TV shows and movies, pretty much any of my entertainment books. So let me talk about what I mean by the references and why I don’t like them, even just not in games, and what my advice would be for you to do if you absolutely cannot bear not having a comment about something. So when I mean cultural references, I just don’t mean you making reference to a song you like or a movie you like or a book. I’m talking about anything. Anytime you reference anything cultural, a famous person, uh, a band, a movie, a book, a TV show, an event.

item.title: Prequels
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about prequels now I did a video on sequels and it’s linked below.

But since I did that, I’ve had a number of people ask me about whether I would like to do a prequel, whether what I think of prequels. Um, and I think it was memes Lich who was the first one who asked, I kind of had to dive back through the comments and he asked: what do you feel about pre prequels, Tim? Let me give you the TLD DW, since I think a lot of people DW. Um, my feelings on prequels are very similar to my feelings on sequels, but I do like them more than doing a sequel. I mean I’m not going to, you know, change my tune here.

item.title: Jobs, Careers, And Callings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about having a job versus a career, versus a calling.

Now you may already know what these three different things are, but I’m going to Define them and I also want to talk about how, over the last 40 some years, I’ve watched the proportion of people in each of these three categories change over time in the game industry. Um, it’s something I’ve seen people make comments sometimes going: well, it’s just a job. You know you’re getting taken advantage of at your job, and I want to just point out that some people don’t just view this as a job, and it’s not a small number of people.

item.title: I'm Naïve And Optimistic
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about how I’m naive and optimistic.

This may seem like a weird thing to say and some of you are going: oh yeah, I can tell I’ve watched this channel. I can tell, um others of you are going what, and let me just talk to you about I’m telling you this because I want you to know this, so this can be part of the lens through which you view my own channel. I’ve always been like this. I have been. I’ve stayed this way despite some of the negative stuff that’s happened, um, and I’ve also stayed this way despite of a lot of good things happening too. I’ve been told this by many people.

item.title: Making A Demo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making a demo.

I’ve probably said that phrase in so many other videos, but I’m constantly asked. Even now, it’s been over a year since I’ve been doing this Channel and I’m still asked: hey, Tim, how can I get a job in the industry, despite there being a video titled how to get a go job in the game industry, which I’ll link below. In fact, all the videos I mentioned I’m going to link below, and I always answer this same thing: make a demo. Um, Tim, should I go to school? Make a demo?

item.title: My 100k Award
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me Tim, well, this thing just [Music].

Arrived. What

item.title: Industry Changes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about industry changes because there have been a lot of stuff going on: a lot of layoffs recently, a lot of concern about AI taking jobs.

People are freaking out and let me just make this clear at the beginning, so no one takes this all out of context later, even though I know some people are. It is bad to lose your job. I’ve lost my job, uh, in the past. It’s bad when you lose your job. That is not what I’m talking about here. I’m talking about news and YouTube, pundits and, you know, industry folks saying the industry is collapsing, it’s collapsing, it’s, horrible.

item.title: Player Expectations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about player expectations.

What I mean by that is, when you’re making a game, you have to somehow convey to people what this game is going to be, and they’re going to form expectations of what they’re going to get, and that is really really tough to manage. And I’m going to talk about, like, how you go about trying to do it, what some of the problems are and, uh, why sometimes new IP makes it easier and sometimes it makes it harder. So — and yes, I apologize, the dog is at my feet and snoring really loudly in this video, um, so let’s talk about how you try to manage player expectations. Until the game ships, uh, or get close to shipping, you will use, probably, interviews. Uh, marketing tends not to be done till later, um, but, interview interviews are a very narrow bandwidth, it’s. You know people want to come and talk to you about your game, but frequently they want to talk to you about other things too.

item.title: An Example Of Making A New IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making an IP an actual example.

Like I’ve talked about making and maintaining an IP, I’ve talked about the order to do it, in which is setting, story, system mechanics. I’ve talked about making what goes into a good setting. I’ve even talked about what your design doc should look like. Those are all videos I’ll link below.

Today, let’s do it. Let’s actually take something from one of my design idea books. I will talk about it, I will expound on it, I’ll show how the idea gets converted into an actual design that you can say now it’s an IP. So, and I will do it in the recommended order, talk about setting, story and systems. So my idea from this book was: I wanted to make a post-apocalyptic game — yeah, another post-apocalyptic game, but I didn’t want it to be nuclear war or any of the other things I’d use before. I had a new idea and what was going to be. Different about this one is: uh, a lot of people are gone, a lot of monsters, and I wanted to come up with more variation than what I got to do on Fallout, which was: here are some gos and giant bugs and mutants.

item.title: "It's A Terrible Time For Games"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

It’s a terrible time for games. I hear this phrase a lot, and not just recently. I’ve always heard this. I’ve heard this since was I? Since I was a kid, when I first got in the industry, when I went back into the industry, throughout my time in the industry and I’m hearing it now.

I’m hearing it’s a bad time for gamers. I’m hearing it’s a bad time for game developers. Bad.

item.title: Lore Drift
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about lore drift.

I’ve seen people complain online, especially recently, about lore changing. Um, yeah, I’m talking Fallout lot, but I also I’m talking about this for a lot of ips. I mean, it’s not just Fallout people talk about this, it’s um, Star Trek, uh Dune. For me it was the Conan stuff when Conan went from being a book to a movie. And I want to talk about all this.

item.title: The Missing R&D Department
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the missing R&D department, and by R&D I mean research and development.

This is a department that I think is missing at most, if not all, game companies, and it’s super important. This is the skunkworks group that really good ideas come out of um. If you’ve watched my Fallout videos, it’s, I think, how Fallout was created: accidentally, because the initial group was an impromptu, random Skunk Works that was thrown together on their own time outside of the normal channel. So by the time production really started, we had a really good idea of what we wanted to make.

item.title: Delegation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about delegation, the dreaded delegation.

Actually. This is an answer to a question from future Tech pilot who asks: would you consider talking about delegation and what amount of power other people are comfortable with receiving, like I’m sure you’ve assigned a task but had to step in when the job wasn’t done correctly? What is the D Dynamic of that interaction between boss and employee?

item.title: Fallout TV Show Review
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim. Let’s do a Fallout TV show review.

First off, there’s going to be spoilers. I don’t know exactly what.

I’m spitballing a lot of this, I’m just popping off the top of my esophagus. So I’m just going to say in advance: spoilers are coming up. If you haven’t finished the show, stop, come back when you finished it. Also, I know I’ve said multiple times that I don’t review things. I don’t like to review other people’s work, but I’m going to talk about the show. A lot of people are asking and at the end I’m going to link you to a review that is better than mine. That I watched, I liked a lot, I agreed a lot with it. So you can just go watch that one and go, okay, um, also, because I know I have a tendency to ramble, I’ll just say right now: I like the show there. For people who don’t want ramblings, you can just stop leave. Tim said he liked the show, there you go.

item.title: Error Handling
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about error handling.

Specifically, I want to answer a question from mudhole creation who asks: I’d love to hear how you, versus the industry, do error handling. Do you check parameters before you send them or do you check them in the function they’re sent to? Do you not use things like raw pointers so you don’t have to do checks using printer Trace functions? What about using a search exceptions, try, catches? Also, what are your thoughts on rules like not having not using raw pointers, globals or Reinventing the wheel?

item.title: Common Sources Of Programming Frustration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about common sources of programming frustration.

This is probably going to be a long video. It’s also me talking to programmers, so while it may be of interest to non-game development programmers, it’s probably only academically interesting to any of the game development people who aren’t programmers, although I there are some things in here I think some production people should look at, or, I’m sorry, just be aware of, um, because I got a couple production things to say at the end of the video. But let me also say that this is not going to be the same video as the one I did on challenges facing programmers in game development. That’s more of a team related thing about you know, the challenges of working with non-programmers. This is more of things that will happen when you’re programming. That will frustrate you, aggravate you, throw you into a rage and pretty much sometimes put a roadblock in on development.

item.title: Crunch, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim. I want to talk about crunch again, so consider this crunch part two.

I’ve already done a crunch video. Wow, did it generate a lot of commentary. I’ll link it below, and I’ve occasionally go back and look at comments. People are still commenting on it now and it reminded me of a story I wanted to tell which is crunch related. That happened to me in college, now I have a separate video, which I will also link below, where I talk about how school — both engineering school where I went for undergraduate, and my graduate school — um, how those prepared me for a career in game development, and so that video, along with crunch video, are linked below.

item.title: Career Advice From Adam Savage
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about some career advice from Adam Savage.

Yes, MythBusters, Adam Savage. I’m a huge MythBusters fan and, because I have a YouTube subscription, I’ve ended up watching quite a number of YouTube channels, and one of them is Adam Savage’s tested by the way, it’s a really good Channel. You should watch it. Uh, this particular video I’m making — after I just watched quit taking it personally — which I’ll link below, which is one of several pieces of advice that Adam Savage gave on your career. Basically, he gave career advice to people who were, uh, sending in questions, and I wanted to talk about it because, as I watch this video, I was like: oh yeah, that’s exactly from my own career.

item.title: Fallout TV Show
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. Okay, let’s talk about the Fallout TV Show, because apparently my game development stories are not interesting to you people, so let me talk about it.

Um, I’m going to have a lot of context for this. I got invited to the Hollywood Premiere, which was on April 9th. Got invited by Todd Howard. He emailed me, asked if I wanted to go. Uh, this is on March 26. I said totally yes. Um, uh, James Alman. Uh, hooked me up with the agency that was doing the tickets.

item.title: Managing Game Expectations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about managing game expectations, and let me explain what I mean by that.

I’ve already done a video on discoverability, which is the about the difficulty of people finding, about finding your game even exists, and all the videos I mentioned. I’ll put links in below. This is kind of the opposite of that I’m talking about.

Once someone finds your game, how do you let them know what kind of game it is they’re getting? And, just so you know, this isn’t the expectations that your team would have you. So I’ve already talked about maintaining a good vision for your game in my videos on how to be a good game director and how to write good design pillars.

item.title: Skill-based XP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about skill-based XP.

I did a video a few weeks ago where I talked about what I thought the best way to give XP was, which spoiler warning is: through quests, because that’s the way I thought most encouraged players of any build to advance through your game.
