This generated a ton of discussion — really good discussion, by the way. It was a perfect example of why I like doing this channel. I was reading all these comments, I responded to some of them and it made me think, so I thought, hey, let’s do a video on skill-based XP. Um, I now I’ve done a video on class based versus skill-based systems that you also may want to watch. It’s these videos will be linked below. Um, you just may want to watch it first to know what I’m talking about, but I’m at a high level.

item.title: Perseverance
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about perseverance.

Specifically, I want to talk about all the speed bumps I had in my career, all the times I thought about leaving the industry and where I think many of you probably would have left, and I wanted to make this positive because I wanted to point out I did persevere. But several times in on this channel, um, people have said, hey, you, had a great career and I’m like there was some speed bumps. And also recently I talked to someone who’d been in the industry as long as I have and we were both amazed that there were always times where it’s like, well, I guess this is the end, I guess this is where my career ends, and then it didn’t. So I’m going to go over some times where I think that happened and just talk about, like, what was going on in my head. So my first job was at Pegasus software, which changed it name to cybron, making Grand Slam.

item.title: Replaying Diablo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about replaying Diablo.

I’m not talking about Diablo 4 or three or two. I’ve been playing the original Diablo. Why have I been playing the original Diablo? I will tell you at the end of this video, not because I want any sense of suspense, but I think it won’t make sense until I tell you all the things that I noticed playing Diablo. So let’s get into that, first of all, this game holds up really well for coming out in 1996 and being, I think it’s 640 480. I wonder if it’s 32200.

item.title: Fallout Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do the Fallout development timeline.

I’ve been putting together notes — I’ve mentioned this before about the making a Fallout and I think I’ve created a pretty definitive timeline here. So it’s kind of long. It’s probably going to be kind of long. There are a few pictures, but not a lot.

So if you need to go get coffee or you need to go to the bathroom, do it now. Don’t pause me later. I’ll know if you pause me, but this is what I put together from probably like five years of notes. Um, maybe a little more. Let me start a little pre Fallout, which is important.

item.title: My Family History Book
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my family history book.

This is a second book I’ve written. I’ve mentioned my Memoirs and I said I’m not going to publish it. This one I did make prints of and send them to a lot of people. I want to talk about it and then I want to tell you why I’m talking about it. So years ago I moved to California in 198- 7. I bought a house in 1995.

item.title: Fallout 1 and 2 Box Covers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Fallout 1 and two box covers and yes, I’m calling fallout Fallout one, just so I don’t say I’m talking about the Fallout box covers and then everyone will get confused.

Now I’ve mentioned this here and there. I know I talked about it on the why I left Fallout 2 video. I think it came up in chats with Leonard, so I’ll link those below if you want even more context. But I wanted to pull, them out, pull out that discussion and make one particular video about it. And I wanted to call it out because otherwise this is the kind of thing I think gets lost in development.

item.title: Discoverability
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about discoverability.

Let me tell you what I mean by that. When I started working in the game industry in the 80s, you would be lucky if a new computer role playing game came out once a month. Usually they were only a few a year, so once one came out, you usually had to wait a couple months, maybe even longer, for another one to come out. That was true for almost all the genres, um, except the most simplest, like arcade, like games, where several of those came out a month. Now, compare it to now where, if you included dlc’s and expansions and the mobile market, you could probably play a new RPG every day, and I don’t think I’m exaggerating. So let’s take a look at this from both sides.

item.title: Damage Numbers, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about damaged numbers again, because I already did a video on damage numbers, but that video was mostly about how they should appear in the HUD.

And apparently what a lot of you wanted was: how do you actually pick damage numbers? And by that you meant the range of damage numbers, because once you have a range of damage numbers — like do you go from 1 to 10, one to 20, one to 100, one to a th000? Once you pick that range, that leads to a r, a subsequent range of damage, of numbers for hit points and bonuses and weapon damage and things like that. So I’ve talked about damage numbers as a HUD. I’ve also talked about the ranges you should use for skills and attributes. This talk is going to be very similar because a lot of the things I said in the skill and attribute talks, which I’ll link below, are still valid here. Um, so, before I go about talking about the method, I use to do numbers, number ranges for damage, let me talk about just some stuff about numbers. Big numbers make people happy, when they hit something and they see two, they’re not as happy as when they see 20 or 200 or 2,000 or 20,000. Now you may go wait, if the monster has 10 hit points, who cares if you’re doing two versus the monster has 10,000 hit points and you do 2,000?

item.title: Digipick vs Sonic Screwdriver
item.description: Did you pick Sonic Screwdriver? Did you pick sonic?

item.title: Chocolate
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today’s Friday. So let’s talk about something fun.

That I will loop back to game development at the end, and what I want to talk about is chocolate. Do not underestimate how much I’m into chocolate. I love chocolate, I love eating it, I love talking about it I have a whole blog, which I’ll link to, which has thousands of entries, each one with a different ch, where I talk about who gave it to me, where it came from, what cacao percentage it is. Is it milk, dark chocolate, white chocolate, rose chocolate, which is the special Fourth Kind of chocolate, which is kind of like the plasma of the Chocolate World. At one point I could eat a bite of chocolate and tell you within 5% what the cacao percentage was, and sometimes I would tell you what country it was from. If it was single origin, I could tell you: oh, this is Ecuador, this is Madagascar, this is Ghana, because there would be a particular flavor to it.

item.title: Games Without End & New Game Plus
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game endings and new game plus — two different things.

They’re kind of related. I’ll explain why I have two different questions I’m answering here. I’ll talk about the first one, talk for a bit and then, when I talk about new game plus, I’ll bring in the other person who asked. The first one is from Ian akamoto, who writes: would love to hear your thoughts on games that end and return the player to the menu upon completing the main quest line, versus games that allow the player to continue playing on. Wondering if you favor a specific camp and how a developer might approach this Choice. Okay, I think it’s pretty obvious that I favor the Game ending Choice, since it’s pretty much what my games all do. Um, part of the reason I did this is when you have an ending.

item.title: Programming Languages
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim.

Yesterday I talked about the evolution of Technology over my career and I started to segue in that video into programming languages and I said that could be its own video. Then I realized someone had asked me that exact question. Developer 67 asks: Tim, can you compare C, C++ and c and talk about your experiences making games with the languages, early on in your own engines and later on in unity for pillars, and also tell us what did you en enjoy more doing? That’s a good question and it ties in really good with yesterday’s video. Let me roll back a little so before I jump into this, just as a reminder, I started my programming Journey with basic on the Atari 800 and then that led me into assembly because I wanted to figure out how some of the games worked and the game. A lot of the good, really good games on the atar 800 were an assembly.

item.title: Technological Evolution
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about technological Evolution.

Specifically, I’m going to try to answer a question from bavid deckham 411, who asks: what was your experience with the technological Evolution we’ve gone through during your time making and playing games? Was it ever a source of stress or were you excited to keep up? I’ll give you the tldw: it was exciting and stressful it’s.

item.title: What Is Fun?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fun.

I’ve already done a video, and I’ll link it below, about balancing fun and realism in games, but I’ve got a lot of fun oriented questions. People want to know what I think about fun versus balance, what I think about games that force you to go, what the designer considers to be the F path, or just what is fun, Define fun. I’m going to take a stab at it same way I took a stab at def: finding what an RPG is. Let’s see how this goes, first of all, let’s talk about fun versus balance. I don’t think anybody’s going to be surprised that I prefer fun overbalance in any of my games, and you can even see some of my games were really unbalanced.

item.title: What Is An RPG?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what is an RPG?

Yes, I’m going to wade into this Minefield of trying to Define what a role playing game is, at least on a computer. I see people talk about this all the time. People look at some games — go: that’s not an RPG. And look at other games — go: that’s the quintessential RPG. And my whole career, and even before my career, I always viewed RPGs as a Continuum that there are some games that are more RPG like and others that are less. Especially lately, when games are coming out that people say have RPG elements, I’m like, okay, they never really Define RPG elements, but they talk about it. So what I want to do is, now that I’ve said, okay, I think RPG is a term that defines a Continuum of games, I want to talk about what that Continuum is, and you can think of this as a checklist of game features and the more of these features you have. The more I would consider you an RPG, and then I’ll talk about how other people view this when I’m done.

item.title: Designing Mechanics First
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about the idea of Designing mechanics first for your game before doing anything else.

This is actually in response to a question from this is fine. Who asks: you’ve mentioned time and again that it goes setting, then story and finally system mechanics. But what about the approach that some directors use, like Miyazaki Ora, to design mechanics, movement levels, ET first and then develop a fitting story based on one, the lore established in the background and two, the things that organically emerge while developing the game itself?

item.title: Bored Now...
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. I get asked to review games or comment on other games a lot and I’ve said I’m not going to do that. But today I thought I’d do something a little silly, and I mean this light-heartedly. But I’m going to talk about times I’ve been playing games, other RPGs — where I get bored. Bo I’m going to talk about — I’m not going to about particular games.

item.title: Easter Eggs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Easter eggs specifically.

I want to talk about Easter eggs that are in games that I worked on, that have something to do with me or that I’ve talked about before, but I don’t see very widespread that people have either talked about the Easter egg but not really much about it, which, by the way, is interesting. I find a lot of um people online love to talk about games as if the people involved with them are gone, are just they’re no longer around, and then they guess at things and they’re like we don’t know why this happened or it’s. I literally watched a video the other day that was like 10 big mysteries about early games, and by early games he met games in the mid to late 90s and he was wrong on most of them. I was like that isn’t why that was done that way, and they weren’t even games I had worked on. It was just I knew about them, so it was very strange. Um, and I get that a lot of them were probably clickbait, but these are Easter eggs that I kind of want to give more information about, just because they meant something to me. First one want to talk about is an Easter egg.

item.title: Avoiding Burnout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about avoiding burnout and specifically want to talk about how I avoided burnout.

I’ve been asked this question a lot like multiple, times and for a long time — as long ago as eight months ago by Caleb zwit- 719 — and 5 days ago by Commander 67. The reason I haven’t posted anything about it is I wasn’t sure what my answer should be, because I have experienced down times — and I’ve talked about this — where I didn’t want to be in a lead role, I didn’t want to run a project, but I never, ever burned out in the sense that I get from other people about: you don’t want to do game development anymore, you want to leave the industry. I mean, I’m retiring and I’m still making my toys. So I thought about this for a long time and I I think I have an answer, but I don’t want to it to sound trit, so I’m going to tell you what my answer is and then I’m going to talk for a long time saying kind of what I mean by my answer. So I think I avoided burnout because I always had fun.

item.title: Explaining Non-Linearity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about explaining nonlinearity and how this used to be really hard.

It’s a lot easier now still has some challenges. This is an answer to a question I got on the channel by Mr Lars cot1, who asks: you mentioned Force linearity as mistake number five in your 2016 reboot talk. Can you elaborate on how you’ve been challenged on The Stance during your career? It’s interesting how one of your main RPG design pillars might conflict with the views of other team members — Naro designers, cinematic creators, other programmers, Etc. And do you find that developing open-ended sandbox RPGs have become much more accepting by people in the game industry over the years? So, first off, that’s a good question and I thought I should begin by showing a clip of this talk just to kind of get everyone the same page about what Lars is referring to. So here it is, so the next uh problem I’ve had: um, I have a thing for linearity.

item.title: Research
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about research, specifically the research that you do when you first start a game.

I get a very detailed question on the channel from Lazar satvic, who asked: this might be a very lengthy topic, but I’m interested to hear you talk about the research phase of game development. I have a couple of sub questions about it.

Obviously, research is very important for games that are set into historical settings or games that want to accurately depict real modern-day cities. So how important was research in Fallout, Arcanum, in The Outer Worlds, which aren’t tied down that way? Does research only happen during the early stages of development or does it last throughout production? Can there be additional research during the later stages of production if the need arises? Which are some of the things you personally researched while designing your games?

item.title: Game Difficulty Settings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game difficulty.

Specifically, I got a question from Noah Calder 4279, who asks: I’d love if you could share your thoughts on difficulty in RPGs. Do you think customizable difficulties such as settings and Sliders work well in RPGs? Which are the best methods of making RPGs difficult, and should RPGs get easier or more difficult the further a player advances through the game? That is a set of good questions that all fall under game difficulty. So I thought, okay, time to do a game difficulty video. So just up front, I do like customizable difficulty settings. I think anything that makes a player turn the game into something they’d like more I’m not against. Frequently in my career I’ve had arguments with people — designers, programmers — who think that every time you put an option in a game it’s because you couldn’t make a decision, and I see where they’re coming from. There were sometimes I couldn’t decide what was better and I thought, maybe.

item.title: Canonical Endings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about canonical endings, and by canonical endings I mean the ending of the of a game that is considered to be the right ending, or the correct ending, or the true ending or, at the very minimum, the ending the developer expected you to have.

Now, for nonlinear role playing games, that there’s only one ending. Everybody has the exact same experience and then experiences the one ending that there is, and that’s the canonical ending. But for a lot of my games, which were nonlinear, multiple ending, very choice and consequence-based games, finding the canonical story ending can be tough.

item.title: Loading And Saving
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about loading and saving games.

Gotten a lot of questions about this so I’m going to answer it, but I want you to know this is a very high level answer. I think some people are literally asking me how to load and save their game, which I cannot answer because one it’s highly dependent on what language you’re writing in, what engine you’re using — even if it’s your own — and what decisions you’ve made about how you want load and save to work. So instead I’m going to describe a lot of the high level stuff, including the decisions you have to make before you even write a line of code. If you want to know the low-l stuff, look up serialization, for whatever your language and engine is, there should be a lot of tutorials on how to do serialization. That’s what you want, so let’s talk first about what kinds of save game systems there are and kind of the pros and cons for those. So the first one, and the one i’ I’ve liked using a lot, is: you can save anywhere, anytime. These are the hardest ones to write because you have to save the state of many, many things and sometimes things that it’s hard for you to get the state for. For example, if you can save anywhere in an RPG, can you save when a character is in the middle of an animation?

item.title: A College Story
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story about something that happened to me in college and before I tell the story I want to say the story itself is less important than the reason I want to tell the story which is to me.

It’s one of the stories about something that happened to me that when I tell people the exact same story, I’ve seen people draw wildly different conclusions about the story, including why I’m even telling it. And I’ll tell the story first and then I’ll talk to you about kind of the reactions I got and then a fun um epilogue to the story anyway. So this is a story about when I was in college. In took place my last year, my fourth year n, which would be 1987, and it was the last semester. I was in engineering school and I was enrolled in a class introduction to the Sea language, which some of you are already going.

item.title: Achievements
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about achievements.

I’ve gotten a lot of questions over the last several months about achievements, mainly asking me whether I like them or whether I don’t like them, what I think of them, and I’ve thought about this a lot because I was trying to figure out how to say it, how to crystallize my thoughts, because I know, like with a lot of my other videos, people are going to pick and choose what they hear in it and you know, waiting for, you know, an article. Tim Kane hates achievements, but let me tell you this: is my high level: I have a love hate relationship with achievements and to understand why you kind of have to look back probably 30 years for me to understand, like, how I view achievements. So in the ’90s and even the early 2000s, I was doing RPGs that had a lot of tracker variables. That’s how I did endgame slides. That’s how I did story reactivity. That’s how I had NPCs just react to the player — um, even if it’s just a bark over their head going — you don’t have clothes on — kept track of lots of variables. Sometimes they’d look at the player and look at values on them, but sometimes variables would be tracked globally. They did this thing, the player said this thing, the player found this thing.

item.title: Why Aren't There More Channels Like This?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about why there aren’t more game developers doing channels like this.

I’ve seen a lot of people ask or wonder in comments why there aren’t a lot more channels with people who’ve made games doing this and why I don’t have more people on this channel, and I’m going to break those up. I’m going to talk about why there aren’t channels like this. I’m also going to talk about why I haven’t done more chats because they’re related. And then I want to talk about kind of why it even took me 40 some years to make a to do something like this.

item.title: Bugs In RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about bugs and particularly bugs and RPGs.

My experience is all in RPGs so I’m sure a lot of what I’m saying will generalize, but I just want to talk about why RPGs tend to be buggy, and this actually comes from a question a lot of people had questions. This is when I picked rrams 1989 asked: will all RPG games always be very buggy on release?

item.title: Five Traits In A Good Producer
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about what makes a good producer.

This actually comes from a question from Phil sp26, who asks: I was wondering if you would mind sharing what makes a good producer. What are you looking for and advice for those who plan on heading into that role? That is a very good question, Phil, because I’m always surprised at what people think they wanted a producer, or even that they don’t know what a producer is. The producer is, he’s kind of at the top of the chain. Most people think the game director is, but in some ways the producer is because they’re handling everything that’s going on and making sure it’s getting done and interfacing with the publisher and interfacing with localization, interfacing with external groups that are doing work you may have, you know, maybe you’ve externed some of your art. They’re handling all that stuff, even if they have assistant producers who are directly handling it. They’re managing those people, so producers do a lot of stuff and you may think the answer I would give you is: I like a producer that does what I say. No, producers need to be a little tough because they work with all kinds of different people and they need to. They’re the ones who often put their foot down and say this has to be done or it won’t go into the game. Yeah, the game director does that a lot, but sometimes the producers — the one that even has to tell the game director you’re out of time, you’re out of money, or that person isn’t available and anymore they’re moving on to something else.

item.title: The Origin Of Troika's Name
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the origin of the name for troa games.

I thought I had talked about this before but I guess I haven’t, and I’ve had people ask as recently as doing yesterday’s video on what I would have speculated would have happened if troa games had still been around for Kickstarter. Theo rodk 782 asked what was the origin of tra’s name.

item.title: Troika And Kickstarter: What If?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troa games and Kickstarter.

No, Kickstarter did not exist when tro was there, but someone asked a question on the channel. I think it’s been asked multiple times. It’s also something I’ve thought about a lot. It’s something I’ve actually discussed a little bit. I I know I had this discussion with Leonard I talked about with a couple other designers at obsidian when I was there. But let’s dive into it. Um, nu4, Ed — I don’t know how to pronounce — that asks: if Kickstarter had been a thing while tro was alive, would you have tried to crowdfund and if yes, what? So I will give you the answers as briefly as I can and then I’ll dive into it. Yes, and Arcanum 2, so yes, if, crowdfunding had existed, troa definitely would have gone that path. Tra was a very small company, when we made arcum, we were 14 people and because of that, we were very efficient.

item.title: Sequels
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about sequels.

I get a lot of questions about sequels, what I think about people making sequels, what I think about sequels that come out much later by other teams, um. So I thought I’d just talk about, like, my take on sequels, and I’ve said this, i’ I’ve mentioned this in other videos: in general, I don’t like making sequels, um, at least not nearly as much as making something original.

item.title: "Bad Games"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about bad games — and I’m laughing even saying that, because I think it’s such a weird term and it can mean so many different things.

And just before I even dive into it, bear in mind I’ve already done a video on what I think makes a game good, which I’ll link below, and the data, the objective data — on my games, which I’ll also link below. So I kind of referen both of those in here, but you might want to watch them first to see where I’m going from in this one.

item.title: The "Best" OS For Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the best operating system to make games.

For now. This is an answer to the question that Romo bomb asked, which is: hey, Tim, can you please talk about the best OS to use, in your experience, for programming and designing? Does it even matter for the user in those professional grounds? Yes, but I’m not going to talk about what I think the best one is to be doing that work in, because that’s subjective. Some people like Mac OS, some people like Windows, some people like Linux and there’s a million osses out there. I’m going to answer in terms of pure practicality.

item.title: Remembering The Game Developers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the people in game development and specifically

, this is sort of an answer to a question that Bruce schlick bur asked on the channel. I worked at interplay at the same time as Bruce. We worked on different projects but we knew each other.

Um, he asked at the end of the day, is it about the process or the end result for you? And I said I replied to him in the channel and said, oh, it’s the process, it’s definitely the process. Um, there are early interviews with me in the ’90s and early 2000s where, when I was asked who my games were for, I would just be honest and go: we make these games.

item.title: Castles (1991) and Stonekeep (1995)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about castles and stonekeep, two games at interplay.

One came out in ‘95 or ’ 91 and the other came out in ‘95. I have a little bit of code in the second one but I really didn’t have a whole lot to do with it. But Sebastian Ola asked about stories about castles and ston keep and at first I thought I don’t have any, but then I thought you know what I kind of do, especially about ston keep. So if you’re interested in either those games or just what interplay was like in the early and mid 90s, this is a video about stories, because, guess what turns out, I really like talking, stories, if you didn’t, if you didn’t figure that out yet. So let me start with castles. Uh, castles came out in 1991. I did not start at interplay until 1991 and I was only a contractor during that time, which meant I would just come in every couple weeks and drop off.

item.title: Morality Systems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about morality systems in RPGs.

This is actually a question from the discording tales 8198, who asks: what is your point of view on alignment or Karma systems in RPGs, systems of morality, of moralities, maybe, IE going Beyond good or evil, but not relativistic per se, using different sets of values measured from their own viewpoints? Hm, that is a bit of a vague question, um, but it’s also a difficult one, because now we’re talking, philosophy and definition of Good and Evil, and let me talk about what I tried in a couple games and then what I’m thinking now. So back in Fallout, we tried to do reputation and we just did kind of an overall reputation. So it wasn’t necessarily good or evil, it was. This was a positive thing to do towards your rep and this was a negative thing, and it was done via script and it turned out to be really varied, because what one group of people in on the team thought was a good thing for you to do for your reputation, other people thought was a bad thing. And in the game itself there was: hey, if you do this would be positive only for this one group. It’s a negative for this other group. So I don’t think Fallout was a good first step. It was a, mish mash of ideas. I mean, we had reputation titles in there too, like a champion of some group or you know, the child killer was a reputation title. So when we did Arcanum, I wanted to systematize these things.

item.title: The Future Of Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the future of games, like where I think games are going to go.

I’ve talked before that there’s a lot of different YouTube channels I like watching and one of them is called like stories of old and I was watching it recently and they were talking about how movies have gone through a bunch of phases, from traditional to Modern to postmodern to meta modern. Um, I don’t pretend to understand all of that. I’m said many times, I’m not the best writer, but my understanding was Modern stories were modern. Movies had stories where the characters were unchanging and had very distinct values, and then the story was them going through an event. Postmodern was kind of tropes version. They like twists, you know it’s oh, it wasn’t who you thought, the killer wasn’t who you thought, or even fourth wall breaking — that happens a lot — where the viewer invited to watch the movie for what it is: a story. And then there’s metamodern, which I won’t claim to understand, but seems to be more of using those elements that postmodern use, like trop aversion or fourth wall breaking, to show you that it’s just a story, to instead tell the story more deeply than It’s Been Told before now.

item.title: Companions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about companions.

Got a lot of questions about Companions and how I feel like they should be used in RPGs and I don’t have a simple answer, which is why I put off answering this for such a long time, and I’ve gotten so many qu different questions from different people that I will try to cover them all in this one video. So it’s. It covers what — whether or not I think companions should be in games, and how many there should be and are they controllable, and what’s their involvement in the quest line, and blah. So let’s just dive in. There’s a lot of topics here. Companions are a nuanced topic, so, companions, let’s start with whether or not they’re controllable. In general, in isometric games, I lean towards them being controllable — that once they enter the party, yes, you control them.

item.title: How To Give Experience Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about how to give XP experience points.

This is kind of an extension of a video I did earlier — and I’ll link it below — on experience and level progression, but that was about how I recommended you give it out. But Gabriel matusovich — I hope that’s the right pronunciation — asked me how to give out XP such that different player builds would gain XP at the same rate. So no matter how you play the game, you’d roughly be the same level as you progress through it. So what we’re really talking here is the rate of XP gain in an RPG.

item.title: Data On My Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to look at data, specifically data for my own games.

I I hear all this discussion about my games — which ones are good, which ones are bad — but today I want to look at hard, factual, objective data, and by objective I mean I can get a number from it. Anybody who goes to look up that number will get the same number. True, you can argue about the numbers, but it’s about as objective as you can get in modern game industry.

item.title: Incorrect Assumptions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about incorrect assumptions.

What I mean by that is there’s a lot of people watching this channel now and I’m really careful how I say things, but I’m reading all the comments. I I don’t always respond, in fact, I frequently don’t respond. I try to and I, if I have time and I have something that I can say that doesn’t require a big video, I try to answer, but I’m seeing a lot to I try to answer, but I’m seeing a lot of comments that make assumptions about what I’m saying that aren’t right, and I’ve done videos about watching my videos with nuance and, um, what it’s like to be a game director and what it’s like to be a programmer and what it’s like to be a designer. But this is something different. This is me saying one thing and a listener or Watcher, um, thinking I said something else and then responding to that, and sometimes I read a comment that’s so wrong. I don’t know how to respond, like the base assumptions of the comment are incorrect, so I don’t know what to say.

item.title: Modding
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about moding.

I get a lot of questions about modding. Do I like mods? Do I play with mods? Will I review mods? So let me start off and say I love mods and I love people mod my games. Um, there’s just so much to say about modding. I had to write a list to make sure I covered it all, and by mods I’m talking about anything from people making new levels or areas to add into games they play, or to people to change a system mechanic or add a system mechanic or modify the UI or change what the skills or abilities do, or even add some or even delete some. I love these things because, first of all, it’s very creative and, second, it shows a kind of effort that goes above and beyond.

item.title: Job Interview Tests
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about job interview tests.

I’ve had a lot of requests to talk about this and I know people hate them. People have always stated all these reasons they hate them. I get it, but let me tell you why. In the middle of a sit down in-person job interview, we ask you questions and give you what the whole. I’ll go over what the whole range of tests are, from just asking you a question that you verbally respond to asking you to stand in front of a whiteboard and sketch out your answer. First of all, why do we do this?

item.title: Ego And Game Directors
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about ego and game directors and how those two things often seem conflated in people’s minds and I want to talk about.

I’ve talked about how to be a good game director, but I’m going to talk specifically about what it is about having a strong ego, but not being egotistical means for being a good game: d dor. So the game director I’ve talked about is the person who holds the vision for the game being made. So questions that arise: does this feature belong? Is this character a good character? Is this storyline good? Ultimately, those go up to the game director. Now, usually they have a lot of leads, a lead narrative, a lead artist, a lead uh system mechanics person, and those people are deciding a lot of this, but they’re doing it based on the director’s philosophy, and being a good game director means having a very clear philosophy of game design and communicating it to the team.

item.title: Grey Morality
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gray morality.

This has come up a lot in my games. It’s the situation where you feel like you’ve been presented with a number of options to finish a quest and there’s really no right choice, and sometimes there’s no wrong choice either. Both of them just lead to different things happening, none of which affect you or a lot of other people, and sometimes we, you It’s hard to even describe whether there’s a right or wrong choice, because it’s hard to Define what’s right or wrong.

item.title: Being Proactive
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about being proactive.

I talked about this about a month ago in a video called good team characteristics, where I talked about five characteristics I really liked in people that I enjoyed working with, and one of them was being proactive, and by that I meant if you see something that needs fixing, at the very minimum, ask about it. You can also offer to fix it. If you know how to fix it, perhaps you even do fix it, and I’ll talk about that in a minute, about fixing something that you see is broke: broken. As soon as you see it being broken, talk about when it’s a good idea to do that before you even ask anybody about it. But I wanted to talk about this because it was really really an important characteristic — and I see some people wrap that up in the whole passion argument about: oh, people think it’s part of passion and passion is bad because it makes you used by the corporation and whatever reasons people have for not liking their job or doing a lot of work on it or being lazy or whatever. Whatever your reason is for why you don’t like passion — great, this is something different.

item.title: Making Good Factions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about making good factions for your games.

This is something that every time I go into making a setting and remember — I think setting is the first step of what you need to do to make a game — I always try to think of what kind of factions are going to be there. And just to be clear what I’m going to talk about, I’m talking about designing the factions, not writing dialogues for particular characters. I’m not good at that — but I am talking about making factions that you’ve designed so that they seem like they belong in your world and have their own goals and are just fun to interact with. I’m going to talk about how I think you should go about doing that.

item.title: Damage Numbers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about damage numbers — yes, those little numbers that fly off of people when you hit them in games — because that’s actually an interesting topic to talk about, and

I got asked a question about it. Zandatsu asks Dam. Damage numbers are a feature that I feel sometimes gets treated as being insignificant. If you have any stories about the specific implementation of a small thing like this and how all those little choices get made, I’d love to hear about it in a video. Are some things like this left entirely up to one person to implement, or is there always multiple people coming to a consensus over what works best? Are there things that feel unimportant during development but, upon reflection, should have been given much more thought in terms of their implementation? I feel like damag numbers may be one of those things. You are right, damaged numbers are something that frequently — especially a, I’d say, in my older games — got left to like, isn’t? It obvious we’re just have a number fly up when they get hit and or, if we do that at all?

item.title: Quest Markers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Quest markers.

Specifically, I want to answer a question from Wes 9353, who asks a question about Quest markers. What do you prefer: pointing at the quest item or Quest enemy even if the enem is invisible, or a quest marker that turns into a search radius, or no Quest marker? Figure it out through dialogue or environmental storytelling?

item.title: How To Gate Non-Linear Stories
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to gate nonlinear stories.

This comes from a question from Galaxy 5 production who asks: when you’re programming a game, how do you make sure things happen in sequence according to a story, not only so that a, b and c happen before D, but also maybe events andf can happen at any time between events A and Z, or maybe, based on player decisions, events change, Etc. Interesting question I do have a video already on non-linear stories, but I didn’t really talk about the gating stage or gating events, which does happen. Um, I’ll give examples from Fallout and Arcanum as I go along. The just kind of explain those ideas and how it kind of changed, how I did things over time. But I want to make something clear and it’s probably just the wording you chose: but this kind of gating happens at your design stage.

item.title: Emergent Gameplay
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about emergent gameplay.

Jeffrey Sherman, 609, asks. Recently I have become entranced with emergent gameplay. After a bit of thinking on it, I feel like a fair amount of your games have offered it and I’m curious if it is considered when making a game and if it is, how much? And is it style dependent?

Uh, crpg, arpg, Etc. I like this question. I’m actually surprised I haven’t really talked about it. Um, I certainly haven’t made it the topic of a video, but I mean, I just start off by saying I love emergent gameplay and that’s obviously why it exists in all of my games. It’s something I saw happen on tabletop and it happens just so organically there. That was something I wanted to make happen in my computer games.

item.title: How To Receive Criticism
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to receive criticism.

I basically want to talk to you about what you should do when people are being critical of your work or even your — opinions. Now, if you watched this Channel at all, you know that I do not think my games are perfect, far from it. I’m not just talking about the bugs that are in them — and there were some doozies, there were some, you know, pretty horrific crash bugs and slowdowns — but I’m talking about even the design bugs that were present in the game when they shipped, that I didn’t even like when they shipped. And I think I’ve talked about things, you know in Fallout and Arcanum that I didn’t like when they went out the door. Um, some of them we changed, like the timer and Fallout, and some of them I never got to change because they were never prioritized.

item.title: Gameplay's Influence On Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game plays: influence on story.

Moji XOXO asks: how much does gameplay influence what stories are or can be told in video games? How do developers grapple with the tension of a story that wants to do one thing or go a certain way, while ensuring that gameplay stays consistently fun and engaging — engaging, throughout? That is a good question. I think I’ve touched on this in other videos a bit, but I’ll just restate it here. It is very, very challenging for video game narrative designers to stop thinking linearly. Most of them are used to writing stories, whether they’re for books or movies or TV shows, and those proceed linearly where games — at least RPG games, which is what I’m talking about here — do not, or mine don’t, and that’s something I want to talk about. So the first red flag to me goes off when I’m having meetings with narrative designers and they say things like: the player goes here: and the player does this and the first thing I say is what if the player goes there and does something else?

item.title: Scenes From A Life Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to show you scenes from a life spent in game development.

Let me tell you where this came from. I’m thinking, so do not hold me to this — I’m thinking about writing a book on game development. Basically, get rid of all the Memoir stuff, that gets people riled up and you know why don’t you give me credit, why did you tell the story about me? BL, just stuff about here’s how I recommend making games.

item.title: The Four Steps For Fixing Design Problems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about fixing design problems.

These are not bugs. I’m not talking about the game is, you know, near in completion and there are things that aren’t working right. I’m talking about you make a design, give it to your programmers and they’re like we gotcha, and they implement it and the artists all go: here’s your good stuff, put it in, here’s everything you asked for and it’s all in and working exactly the way you asked. But there’s a problem, now I’m going to use an example from the original Fallout game — uh, design problem that it shipped with, so I can use that as an example as we’re going through. So in the original Fallout you could buy an energy weapon skill. In fact, you could tag it right at the beginning of the game if you wanted to. The only problem is energy weapons aren’t available for at least the first half and closer to the first 2/3 of the game.

item.title: Game Design Specification Sections
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about all the different design specifications that a single game may need, because about a month ago I gave an example of how to write a design specification and gypsum generation asks: what are all the design specifications that a game might need?

Okay, that is a very long answer, so grab your coffee. Also, I’m going to put a big caveat on this and say the answer really depends on the game you’re making, because different games will have different sections in their design specification. So let’s pretend we’re making a fantasy RPG and I will tell you what the design specs should include. So I’ll break it down into. I’ll go down to what all the main sections of the design specification are and then for each one of those sections I’ll talk about subsections they should have. Why don’t you take a look at my writing design specifications for exactly the kind of details you will have to put in each one of those subsections?

item.title: Taking Games Seriously
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about taking games seriously.

I actually want to answer a question from rvic, although I’m wondering if that’s rvic asks: can games be taken too seriously? And my short answer is: yeah, sure, anything can be taken too seriously, but I want to make a longer answer than that. I almost just wrote yes and as a comment, but I want to take it, make a longer answer because, first of all, you’re asking me there’s very little I take seriously. Um, usually, on anything I’m willing to concede and go okay, um, but to make my answer clearer, I love when people deep dive into the lore and the setting and mechanics of my games. In fact, when we’re making it, that’s often we’re thinking about people doing that. We want people to do that, um it’s.

item.title: Implementing Endgame Slides
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk to you about implementing endgame slides.

I know I’ve talked about slides before and in a way this is going to be a companion video to the one I did last month on how to do the beginning of video games. But end slides is something that I’ve done in a lot of my games, starting with Fallout. My games are kind of known for having choice and content quence called CNC, which — just a side note — CNC used to refer to the game command and Conquer and for a brief period in the 90s, the CNC Music Factory. That song I got the power very popular in that Simpsons episode where Homer thinks Bart’s gay.

item.title: Code Organization
item.description: Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about how I structure code, and this was a question from a lot of different people, including greenia 84, who asked that I do it using an example of inventory, which I think is a good question or good uh specification, because inventory contains a number of interesting things about code structure and it’s common to a lot of RPGs, so I’ll talk about that now.

item.title: How To Pick The Right Project
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to pick the right project, and this comes from a question from Bridges, chroma 1832, who asks: can you talk about how project approval decisions are made in game companies?

Do they follow the will of a singular lead designer or do they get talked about a lot before any actual effort is put into it? Does something akin to military democracy existed game companies? Now, I wasn’t 100% sure, uh, what Bridges meant by that, so I asked for clarification. Do you mean deciding what project to make, which led to a yes and a further for smaller companies and Studios? When they have more Choice with their own ideas, how do they pick the right one within the company? What project do they make? So let me talk about that, because I’ve done that several times where I’ve been at the beginning of a project and having to decide what project to make, and I think every time it’s boiled down to the same thing and so the high level answer to this, the TLD, DW, is going to be: pick something that leads into your team’s um strengths and the size and time you have available to you. So I mean, if you know your team is going to be a certain size and made up of a certain number of individuals and you only have so much time — and by time I ultimately mean Budget — to make it there, that’s pretty much everything you need to know what kind of game you’re going to make. So let’s dive down into that, so by size slash time, I basically mean this is the point where you select the scope of your game, and by scope I don’t necessarily mean how long it will take to play the game. I mean it basically brows down to how many features you want in the game, the more features you have. The richer the game is, but also it takes a lot longer to make, not only because the individual features take a long time to work on, but there will often be a lot of interactions between those features. Once you put in lots of skills and lots of perks and lots of traits and lots of backgrounds, those are going to interact. And then it will bring up questions about: hey, there’s a bunch of bonuses that are very similar. Do they stack? Hey, these bonuses all work on aspects of this skill.

item.title: Enemy Progression
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about enemy progression.

Stagi Ayatollah asks: well, says a good topic for the youngans might be beneficial — is your thoughts on how to handle enemy scaling, something that’s fumbled a lot these days, IMO, perhaps in a border topic of how to efficiently handle enemies in general. I replied to this one and said I didn’t really want to talk about enemy scaling, but I would talk about enemy progression because I’ve mentioned in another video that I don’t like level scaling. I don’t like it when the enemies are adjusted automatically to fit your level and I also don’t like that how it’s often done, just as here are a bunch of extra hit points and better armor and better weapons, to the point where you’re wondering why is a bandit who’s literally carrying around 40,000 gold worth of weapons and armor attacking me?

item.title: The Story Of The Wig
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the story of the wig.

This is kind of a followup of a video I did last month showing me wearing a bag on my head, and it’s about this photo of me wearing a blonde wig and holding a miniature statue of the Oscars — Oscars. So this is actually in response to: uh, interuser commented: there’s also a picture of you in a blonde wig and, I think, a crown and you’re holding an award or something. Haven’t seen that in a while. Good, um, this photo — I don’t even remember how it got out or where it got posted, but, um, there’s context to it and with, well, with a lot of my stories, there’s a bit of context that leads up to explaining why, I’m wearing a blonde wig and holding a miniature Oscar, and I know it’s silly and I know this doesn’t have much to do with game development, but people like the other story, so I figured, okay, I’ll tell. This one too, besides, you got to have room in your life occasionally for something silly. So the context for this: when I worked at interplay, the producer on my first two games there — bar taale construction set and Rags to Riches — was Thomas R Decker. Tom also came to work at troa on Temple of Elemental Evil as a designer and producer and then as a producer on vampire Bloodlines.

item.title: Piracy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about piracy.

Dubie do 5000 asks: what are your feelings on video game piracy and anti-piracy DRM measures? Have your colleagues Express their own views to you on that topic? I’ll be honest, don’t really talk about piracy much anymore. I haven’t really talked about piracy with colleagues since gosh, I think, back in Fallout, when we were talking about whether to have DRM on the CD and I said that CD is full.

item.title: Top Five Modern RPG Masterclass
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about my top five modern RPG master class before I start.

New camera setup. It’s mounted, it’s a little higher, it’s a little farther back.

Let’s see how this goes. I’m playing around with things, so what do I mean by the top five modern RPG Master Class? Well, I did video about a month ago of my top five video games, and these are the games that I grew up with and the ones that I thought were the most influential in how I made games in my career. That’s not what these five RPGs are. These are going to be five role playing games that I think are the best to learn from, as certain aspects of them for game development and specifically for RPG development do I think these are the best games ever.

item.title: Skill Design in RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about skill design in RPGs in particular.

Queen of bad jokes 9413 asks: I wondered how you would approach skills for a new game. How do you choose? What skill types are there? How do you and would you level them? Is there a particular skill that has worked out for multiple games? How can you design for balancing? And are there skill tables too? I mean testing besides in the testing rooms and player testing in the alpha Queen.

item.title: Questions I Can't (Or Won't) Answer
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about questions I can’t or won’t answer.

There’s a lot of questions that get asked on this channel. I try to answer them either right there on the comment section or I’ll even do a video about them if it requires a longer answer, and I’ll talk about that. But I noticed there’s a lot of questions that I’m skipping, so I thought I kind of talk about why I do that and put it all here in one video, so if people are wondering why I’m doing it, they can look at this video.

item.title: Thoughts On A Temple Remake
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my thoughts on a temple remake, a remake of Temple of Elemental Evil, with things I would like to do that either I ran out of time to do or wish

I had done differently. Um, I’ve already talked about my thoughts on a Fallout remake — that was months ago and an Arcanum remake yesterday, so I thought I’d do a temple remake. I’ll link those other remakes below. I’m not going to do a remake video for vampire Bloodlines, though it’s not really mine to remake thoughts from the about. A remake on that should come from Leonard bski or Jason Anderson. So I’m just GNA complete my Trio of remake Thoughts with temple today. So let’s get started. I the first thing I’d do just the standard.

item.title: Thoughts On An Arcanum Remake
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about thoughts on an Arcanum remake.

Uh, I’ve had different questions about it I even touched upon this a little bit in the video I did on Arcanum as a Windows 10 app, where I actually have Arcanum up and running in Windows 10, and I talked about some things I might do about it, but I didn’t finish the thought. I didn’t do it as completely as I did say in the video called thoughts on a Fallout remake. So I’ll link those two videos below, but I want to do this one, which it will kind of collect all my thoughts on.

item.title: Design: Committee vs. Collaboration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about designed by committee versus design by collaboration.

In fact, I want to answer a question by cybercop 0083 who asks: you are critical of games by committee. Can you draw an approximate line between a committee product and a collaborative one? Yes, I can and yes, I am very critical of design by committee because I’ve never. I’ve never seen a committee make a good product. Um, and I’m not even talking games, but every good group that I’ve worked in they’ve done better.

item.title: The Origin of VATS
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the origin of Vats in Fallout and I’ll start by saying we didn’t call it bats in Fallout 1 and two.

That’s a Fallout 3 term, but it’s just what everybody calls it. Now, and this is me answering a question from Vladimir in Germany who asks: I always wonder how this feature that was later known as Vats came to be. Where did it come from?

item.title: Sometimes It's Just Business
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about that phrase — nothing personal, it’s just business — which is usually used by companies when they’re about to do something not too nice and they don’t want you to take it personally.

It’s just business. I want to tell some stories about friends trying to use me to get a job or getting a job through me, where it didn’t end up well, because the moral of the story is it really wasn’t personal, it really was business. Now, if you have a job in the game industry, I guarantee you probably have stories like this. A lot of people try to get in the industry through their friends or family members. I’m not even going to talk about the number of times I’ve been contacted by relative to either get them a job or one of their kids a job, or even how a woman I went to high school with 30 years ago contacted me once to get her son a job.

item.title: Story Vs Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Story versus game design or, as sherner asks, what is more important for a game, story or game design?

I know, ideally there should be a balance between the two, but that is so rare to find. What’s your view on it? Sorry, I got a tickle in my throat today. So let me say first of all, this is a good question, but maybe not for the reason you think I’m going to say it’s a good question.

item.title: Player Agency
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim, and let’s start off the new year by talking about player agency.

Now, what do I mean by player agency? Is that when a game agency? Player agency is that when a game allows the player to choose what to do and then the game reacts, especially the storyline of the game. And player agency the opposite of this would be when a game has a predefined, very narrow set of things the player can do, even though it feels like he should be able to do other things, and the storyline is predictable and unchangeable. You’re basically as the player, you’re just. You just move along that story, you’re just there for the ride.

item.title: Building Tools
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about building tools.

Shady Desperado 2590 asks: I have a lot of questions about building tools for other developers and teammates. What’s that process like? What do the various tools look like for different departments?

What problems can they cause and how can they be avoided? That is a set of good questions. I will try to answer them here. Um, you’re asking a lot of different questions at once, but I think I can answer them both at a high level and by using examples, — examples of games I worked on where I didn’t work on the tools, and then games I worked on where I did and what I learned from that. So let me start with like two really high level things that I think are super important for anybody on a team, but especially whoever’s making the tools.

item.title: Multiple Quest Solutions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about multiple Quest Solutions and I want to use an example from Fallout that I used in my talk at the reboot conference in 2017.

So that is rescuing Tandy from the Raiders. So this is the map. It happens on very simple map. I should point out one building couple tents — sorry, it’s shown here — after killing some guards or some Raiders.

item.title: Good Team Characteristics
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about people I like working with, and

this is an interesting question from postal 0311, who asks: could you please tell us about the characteristics of the people you enjoyed working with, the characteristics of the people you felt helped you achieve the most? Basically, what can we do and act to be better team members in a project? That is a great, question.

item.title: Job Interview Questions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about job interview questions.

I did a video called interview questions. I’ll link it below, but that was about press interview questions but a lot of people assumed is going to be about job interviews and asked for this video. So let me talk about questions I like to ask in job interviews and questions I’ve been asked and just job interviews in general. So I’ve been involved in many job interviews on both sides of the table. When I go to job interviews now where I’m hiring someone, I assume people there are going to ask design and code questions for them to work through.

item.title: A Christmas Lesson In CRPG Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim.

I hope you’re all having a good holiday. I’m secular but I do enjoy a good Christmas and I think if I could pull off a good Papa Hemingway, I think I can also pull off a halfway decent Santa Claus. Anyway, I thought that because today is Christmas, I would read an essay I wrote in 2002 at tra, and this was why we were making Temple of Elemental Evil. I wrote it during the holiday period. There it’s called a Christmas lesson in crpg design, so let me just read this to you now. I wrote this, like I said, 21 years ago. So there we go every year at tricky games. We have a gift change at Christmas time, or Hanukkah or Quanza, depending on who you ask.

item.title: Game Pitches
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today let’s talk about game pitches.

Yes, I look like Ernest Hemingway. A friend said I looked like Ernest Hemingway with this beard, so I put on the turtleneck and got the pipe and I’m leaning into it.

But let’s talk about game pitches for real. So wikka fish ask: could you go into more detail about pitching a game? I’m curious as to what the process may look like, as well as the materials. You should have prototypes, storyboards, Etc. In order to have a successful pitch. That is a really good question.

item.title: Game Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game lore.

I want to answer a question from armored spaz, who asks: how do you keep players informed about the game world, and how could you, how could the game be designed to make the player want to find out more about the game? I think this is a really good question. I almost didn’t answer it though, because it kind of moves into the narrative design territory that I have said in the past, I’m not that good at. But I think I’m good at thinking about narrative design. I’m just not good at implementing narrative design, so let’s talk about that also.

item.title: Analyzing Game Designs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about analyzing games.

Now I’ve talked about this before. I’ve mentioned on this channel that when I play video games these days I can’t turn off the analysis part of my brain. So frequently I find myself enjoying games less than I should just because I’m picking apart things and I’ve said I don’t want to go into specifics about Gam I do this with. But bengala fraka asks: could you make a video on how to analyze games critically with the objective of learning more about design?

item.title: Villains
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about villains in my appropriately lit villains Lair.

So I’ve done a video about bosses where I talked about bosses being encounters that tested how well the player — was uh, getting at the game using that particular character. But villains are different. Villains are bosses, but all boss bosses are not villains. Villains are the, characters that drive the story, and usually there at the end there’s a encounter with the villain who is a boss. But the villain is the one who made the whole story proceed. So I wanted to talk about what makes a good villain. I’m going to find them, kind of give some examples from my games, and then I want to talk about a villain that I did badly, that I want to redo. So let’s talk about what makes a good villain. I think the best thing about a villain is that their goal is not only believable, you think that they’re actually going to be. Able to achieve this goal — but it’s also justifiable in the sense that it kind of makes sense. It’s kind of something that you could be convinced is a good thing. Maybe the player can even be swayed to join the villain. I think that’s important.

item.title: Social Conflict Resolution
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about social resolutions in RPGs, and by that I mean combat resolutions that are social, not combat conflict resolutions that are social, it’s hard to talk about.

I’m going to answer a question from stavos Nicola, who’s answer, who’s asked several questions so far in this channel. He keeps asking good questions, so I’m going to keep answering them. And he asked asks: how do you feel about social, peaceful resolutions and RPGs? Many RPGs tend to use social persuasion based resolutions for Quest or challenges as a one roll skip combat Choice — rarely games and playy mini games or other forms of social combat that make these social encounters more interesting or even on par with the physical combat resolutions. Do you feel RPGs today sufficiently support the social pill pillar with engaging gameplay and, if not, how would you have liked it to be handled? So I think it’s pretty obvious that my answer is going to be that I like peaceful, social resolutions to conflict um. They’ve been part of every RPG I’ve made for the last 25 years.

item.title: Pacifism In Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about pacifism in games.

This comes from a question from am Brandt 7422, who asks: I wonder what you think about pacifist play Styles and how much implementing one costs on development time. So again, T DW. I love PL pacifist playthroughs and they shouldn’t take much extra work, if any at all, to support.

Uh, but before I dive into this, though, two quick things. Uh, YouTube changed the algorithm again, so I have almost no control over ads anymore. You may have heard about this, but I can’t control if they’re at the beginning or the end or if they’re skippable. The only thing I can control is whether or not I want them to be in the middle of the video, and I’ve turned that off, and that is the one control I still have, sorry.

item.title: Telemetry
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Telemetry in games.

Real Keet asks: hi, Tim, I’m interested in learning about how you handle Telemetry in games. How do you learn how people are playing your game so you can balance it or make other fixes? That’s a good question. By the way, if you’re wondering, it is very cold here. I’m freeing — um. Let me answer that.

item.title: Using Geometry To Constrain Dice Rolls
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about using geometry to constrain dice rolls.

First, before I get into that, because there’s a history and there’s a video and let’s do a thought experiment. Let’s say you’re making a new tabletop role playing game and you just want to use six-sided dice. And let’s say there’s a skill that requires you to roll two six-sided dice for that skill, you know generating two to 12. But you want it to be more interesting than that. You want something special to happen if you roll a one and you want something good to happen if you roll a six. And then you want something special to happen if you roll a one and a six. So looking at that, you’re like, wait a minute, I don’t want double bads if I roll two ones and I don’t want double Goods if I roll two sixes.

item.title: Writing Design Specifications
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about how to write design specifications.

Specifically, I’m going to answer a question from Thor RoR TV who asks: I was trying to write a design document for a solo project I’m working on and was wondering if you had any examples of how to properly write a specification that might be given to a programmer or an artist. Also, are there any pitfalls to avoid when writing speci ifications? That is a very good question, thoro TV, and it’s also a little hard to answer because there’s a wild, wildly huge range of design specifications. You might be writing a might be writing, you might be writing a system mechanic. You might be writing a user interface, you might be writing a — um, some narrative guidelines.

item.title: Beginnings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the beginning of a game.

I want to talk about why that’s so important. I’m going to answer a question from Shadow Dragon Dev, who asks: what are your thoughts on what makes a good or bad beginning for a game? Beginning not just as when you press new game, but also as in you first open the game program itself, that entire first impression a player gets at the very start of their experience with it.

item.title: Making And Maintaining An IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about making and maintaining an IP.

By IP, I mean intellectual property, now I’ve talked about writing design docs and how to make a good setting, so I’m going to link those two videos below. This is something different. I want to talk about intellectual property in general. Intellectual property is more than your setting. It’s more than your story. Those are specific to a game.

item.title: The Evolution Of Source Control
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the evolution of source control.

Is widely used control. Source control is widely used now, was not ever used in the 80s, at least no where I was. Source control is using software to maintain ownership of an asset that’s going into the game. This asset could be a code module, it could be a piece of art, it could be a script, but Source control manages the versions of those and who owns it and what the latest one is. So let me tell you how we did Source control in the 80s and even early 90s.

item.title: The Story Of The Bag
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story about a picture that floats around on the internet of me with a paper bag on my head sitting at my desk.

It’s this picture here, and as with everything, there is a story behind it and I know um people say they like hearing my stories, so this will test that. And also I’m directly answering or replying to l r I n fi loreny who said: for some reason I keep seeing a picture of you sitting at your desk with a paper bag on your head. There must be a fun story behind that one. There is, so let me give you some Contex text for this story. I’ve said — and you can watch the other Fallout videos, this was during Fallout.

item.title: My Work Pattern For Designing
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my work pattern for Designing, and you can consider this a companion video to the one I did on programming, which I’ll put a link in the description.

This is actually answering a question from post nuke Productions, who asks: you’ve done a few videos about what it’s like to program a game, so could you give us an idea of what a day working as a designer is like? I like this question, um, I’ll clarify it, though that I’m going to write down what I assume it’s like. Being a system designer. A level designer and a narrative designer would have a very different day. Level designers would work with artists more. Uh, narrative designers would be more interested in the quests and the storyline and spend a lot of their Day writing characters. So I’m going to go as if it’s a system mechanic designer, um, because that’s what I’m most familiar with. By the way, you might want to check out the work pattern. For programming, because a lot of things I’m going to mention here are very similar to what I do when I’m a programmer. It’s not that I view the same jobs as similar, it’s just when I’m, it’s just me how I do a job.

item.title: My Top Five Video Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about my top five video games ever.

I’ve been thinking about putting this list together for months, but I was never quite sure which ones to include, and then I realized I really had to go with the ones that were really influential for me and were also maybe the first of its type that I ever saw, the first, either one of its genre or one with that feature, and not I was originally putting in the games I had played the most, I had the most hours in, but I decided to go with ones that I thought were the most influential and the ones I really remember, and go back to um, the. In this list, however, there is not a single game that is newer than 24 years old, and one of them is 44 years old, so strap in the first one. I’m going to go chronologically, by the way, so these are my top five, but I’m not going to. Tell you what the order is to. Going to tell you what the order is, this is chronological.

item.title: Insignificant Features (That I Really Like)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about features that I really notice in games.

In fact, let me I want to answer a question from Mike Z 6289, who asks: what is a feature you see in games that excites you but would seem insignificant to the average gamer? I think that’s a really good question, although I’m not sure exactly who the average gamer is. But before I answer, let me just talk about, like, how I’m going to interpret this question. Um, I interpret this as, like, what features are personal to me that I notice and I really like, but are insignificant, and I think that’s an important term. Um, for example, colorblindness support is not insignificant to me. I need it. Um, to a lot of people they don’t know or care about that feature, Fe, but I can tell you there have been games that came out recently that are big games that supposedly were quite enjoyable, that I literally could not play because they involved solving puzzles with color.

item.title: Marketing The Outer Worlds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about marketing and specifically marketing on The Outer Worlds.

Now I’ve had a few other videos about The Outer Worlds and I know people want me to talk more about its development and the team and its design. But the thing is a lot of the things I would talk about are still ongoing with The Outer Worlds 2 and proprietary, so there’s not as much I talk about that game. So I’ve gone and talked about a lot of my other games. But then it occurred to me there was recently some news — you may have seen it — about the The Outer Worlds’s hitting 5 million sales. Now that doesn’t include game pass, because a lot of people on Xbox played it on Game Pass, so that’s not including people played it that way. This is people who bought — I think they call it — sell through, people who bought and have a copy of The Outer Worlds, whether it’s physical or digital, and that’s a lot. That’s. Um, I think it’s safe to say The Outer Worlds is my best selling game ever.

item.title: Arcanum As A Windows 10 App
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Arcanum.

I have talked about it before, but people often say: what do you spend your time doing? And I’m, like I’ve mentioned, I work for a couple clients, but I also love messing around with games as toys, and one of the toy games I mess around with is Arcanum, and I did that about. I want to say it was about six months ago. This is November of 2023.

item.title: Stop Making Excuses
item.description:
It’s me, Tim, and it’s tough love today on the Canon games Channel because I want all of you to stop making excuses and start making games.

Consider this my shilou, just do it. Motivational speech video, but I’ve told you, guys, I read the comments and I’m reading comments with so many different reasons that people are rationalizing why they’re not working on a game.
