That was in 91., so in 2004, when we were finishing up vampire Bloodlines — I think it was September of 2004 — I got approached by a friend of mine who had graduated from UCI with a PhD and was working for the US Department of Defense. He had developed the system and he wanted to know if I could take tempo of Elemental Evil and write an API programming interface so that an external AI could run the game. And I was like I think I could do that and he goes, we’ll pay you.

item.title: Why I Like Flawed Characters
item.description:
Hi, it’s me Tim.

I’ve gotten some great feedback on the dumb dialogue video where I talk about the origin of dumb dialogue being restrictions that I put on players in my original d campaign back in late 70s, early 80s. So I wanted to talk about why I like flawed player characters so much and why I think restrictions lead to better role-playing and just better game playing in general. So, after playing d in high school, I really got into groups in grad school and that’s where I first discovered disadvantages, which is in gerp’s, things you can buy for your character that aren’t good but they give you more points to spend somewhere else. I always wanted to do something like that.

item.title: Troika's Post Apocalyptic Demo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

What you are seeing is the only known video footage of epic, which was troika’s in-house post-apocalyptic game we started designing and implementing towards the end of vampire. Uh, of course it never came out. This footage isn’t from mine.

I’ll include the link below. It’s not from my archive. I don’t have any video footage. In fact, I don’t even have any code.

I have a lot of design docs and notes, but my epic folder seems very sparse. The first document I have for this game is dated January of 2003.. And the latest one is dated June of 2004., so let me tell you how I remember this, and I’ll also tell you one thing that someone at least disagrees with me on. But so in 2003 we were contacted by interplay who told us that Bethesda was bidding on the Fallout IP. And did we want to bid? We did. This is where our memories diverge.

item.title: Everything I learned from Disneyland
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do something a little different.

All the other videos have been me talking just off the top of my head, but several people have asked in comments about what Inspirations I have for game design and if any of those Inspirations have come not from games or books or movies, but just like everyday activities. And I realized not only do I have just such an inspiration, but I’ve actually talked about this professionally twice, once to the area Design Group I had at carbine, and then again just a couple years ago to, uh, the team on The Outer Worlds 2.. So I wanted to talk to you today about what would be called the top 10 area design roles, or what I instead called Everything I know about RPGs. I learned it, so this could be a little long.

item.title: Troika's Might & Magic 10 Design
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troika’s proposal from might Magic 10..

I’ll be honest, I’ve forgotten we had done this, but recently I did an interview with RAF colantonio and Peter salnikov on their YouTube channel, House of the dev — I’ll put a link in the description — and Ralph said that he always felt bad that Arcane was pitted up against Troika for might Magic 10.. You know, and I told him — I don’t even know if I knew that, but I told him, I mean, Publishers do that all the time. They like you know developers to compete for things. And I told him not to worry about it. We didn’t get it, but I went on to work on vampire anyway. This occurred right after Temple shipped.

item.title: Vampire Bloodlines - Cut Multiplayer Content
item.description:
Hi, it’s me Tim uh. Today I want to talk about vampire, the Masquerade Bloodlines, the Lost multiplayer content.

But first we got to mention two things. One: I’m trying to fix the technical issues. The note saw video glitching yesterday, a few days before that it was a Microsoft glitching, I’m sorry, my microphone glitching. I think I know what was causing those. Let’s find out, also, I got a ring light, although it’s Square, so I should be better lit now, and it really makes my eyes pop, which I know some of you can notice.

item.title: The True Purpose of Vaults in Fallout
item.description: Hi everyone, it’s me Tim. Wow, you are filling up my comments with so many good ideas for topics that I can’t even really keep up. I had planned today, uh, to either talk about Bloodlines, vampire Bloodlines and the cut content and some of our plans for the sequels, and then I thought, no, no, I’m gonna talk about music, and not just the great music in Fallout, but arcanum’s music and vampires music and the fact that I’m sad each and every day that I got to work with the composer, Johan Johansson.

item.title: The Origin of Dumb Dialog
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story.

One of my favorite stories. It’s the origin of dumb dialogue in Fallout, and not even just in Fallout, I mean.

Once we used it there, we did an Arcana we kept using. It most recently appeared in The Outer Worlds.

In fact, one of the narrative designers in a taipadar came to me one day and said: you know, dumb dialogue’s a lot of work, but whenever I get stuck I just think what would Tim say? I think he meant that as a compliment, anyway.

item.title: Troika Games' Lord of the Rings Demo (2001)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim.

After talking about Arcanum 2 Journey to the Center of Arcanum yesterday, it reminded me that after we worked on the sequel and it got rejected, Troika actually worked on Lord of the Rings, so I’m going to show you that demo in just a second, but I thought I’d give you some context. So when the sequel idea got rejected, Sarah approached us with Lord of the Rings, they just gotten the rights to the book. Now the movies had come out, they didn’t have any movie rights, but they had rights to the book and they wanted to know if we had any ideas.

item.title: Arcanum 2: Journey to the Center of Arcanum
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum.

So let me give you some background. Um, for those of you new, Troika games was a game Studio that I started with Leonard barski and Jason Anderson on April 1st 1998, and our first game that we shipped was Arcanum. Arcanum shipped in 2001 using a new isometric engine we made. However, even before it shipped, we started thinking about ideas for sequels and that we wanted to make the jump to 3D. And we were talking to valve, because the VP of valve, Scott Lynch, had been the VP who signed us to Sierra for Arcanum and we were talking with them about their Source engine and we really wanted to use it. So we put together a design dock, which I still have. I read through and I’m like: Whoa, We were very ambitious. Uh, Troika liked to do a lot of things. In fact, Arcana might commonly refer to as our kitchen.

item.title: Semi-Retirement
item.description:
Hi, it’s me, Tim.

I’ve gotten some really good feedback on the site, on this channel. The people at Reddit and Discord, I guess, discovered this Channel and posted links, so getting a lot more questions than I expected this early on. They’re really good questions. Um, there’s a lot of stuff I want to address in future videos, including how I got started in the industry.

Um, oh. What inspires me in my designs, why I left Fallout 2 and mid-development? Those are all future videos, but I thought I’d clear something up, because I’m getting a lot of questions that specifically have to do with either obsidian or Microsoft, and so I just want to clear it up. I have not been a city and employee since June of 2020, uh, when I moved up to Seattle. I do work for obsidian as a contractor. I also work for another company as a contractor for them. In both cases, I’m doing design, advice and review. I also work for a third company giving them both Tech and design advice.

item.title: Let's Talk Fallout 1
item.description:
Hot, hi, it’s me Tim. Uh, before I start, um, that power armor helmet was made by Josh J, an artist on Fallout 4, and he sent it to me. How cool is that? Uh, yeah, it barely fits over my big head though. Um, it’s really fun though. It’s cool to have something like that.

Thank you, Josh. Um, so the real Jacob 603 asked me some Fallout questions on the last video, so I thought I’d answer them in a video format rather than in text, and I’m going to call this — let’s talk — Fallout one — CU. I have a feeling Fallout questions will come up a lot, um, so he asked me what Fallout, what I think of the modern fallouts and the whole Fallout series and the new ones. Let me just caveat: I’ve never played Fallout, Brotherhood of Ste or um Fallout 76, and I barely played any Fallout tactics. Uh, mostly I didn’t play Brotherhood steel because when it came out um, I was super busy. Same thing with tactics um Fallout 76. Um, I think I understand it’s a great game. It’s just it’s online multiplayer, um mostly having to do with Base building, and those aren’t for me. So and, by the way, if, a game comes out and it has features you don’t like, don’t call it a stupid game.

item.title: My Favorite Sci Fi Novel
item.description:
Hi, it’s Tim. I’m back with the answer to the first topic that, uh, someone had a question about on my last video, and that was user Hyperion said that

I had mentioned that Walter Miller’s book I can’t go of Leibowitz was really influential on the design of Fallout setting asked what other sci-fi books I liked — um, of course I have a lot of books. Um, this is just a fraction of them. Uh, growing up in the 70s, obviously, Arthur C Clarke, Robert Heinlein, Isaac Asimov, Ursula the Gwynne were just hugely influential. When I got older, though, as a young man I guess I read more of Dan Simmons Hyperion series, Ian Banks culture novels, Rogers zelazny’s Amber novels — um, those are the kind of books I really liked to read. But I do have one absolute Freight favorite book of all time, and that would be Lord of Light by Roger zlosny. I love this book, I love it, I love it has everything I want in a book. It’s well written. It’s non-linear, it incorporates. It kind of reads as a fantasy book, but then it turns into a Sci-Fi novel, incorporates religion into the story in a really seamless way. It very gradually reveals what the world is, which I really like. I don’t like front loading everything on you. Um, I love this book so much that I not only own — this is my science fiction book club — but I’ve caught an early edition and I have the Easton press leather bound copy. That’s the only book I think I own multiple copies of.

item.title: Introduction to "Cain on Games"!
item.description:
Hi there, my name is Timothy Kane and I’ve been a professional game developer for more than 40 years. Back in the 80s, I made a game called Grand Slam bridge and most recently I made an RPG called The Outer Worlds. In between, I made a lot of other games.

You may have heard Fallout. You may have enjoyed games from Troika games. I worked on MMOs for a while and even worked on a game that was one of the top grossing games from Kickstarter. I’m going to use this channel to tell stories about what game development has been like for the last 40 years and hopefully have some insights and some fun, so I hope you join me. Thank you, bye.

item.title: Pick a topic!
item.description:
Hi, it’s me, Tim.

I’m trying to figure out what topics to talk about first. I’ve got a huge list. I’m not sure which ones to start with, so I thought I’d throw a bunch of them out there and you guys can leave a comment and tell me which one you think I should start with, or which one you find really interesting or you haven’t heard about somewhere else, so we can. I can start before I even got into gaming, or professionally, uh, back when I played d? D in high school as a DM, which is where I learned the most important lesson which drove all the rest of my game development, which is you can make the best story in the world but you can’t make your players actually follow it and you end up just having to go with the flow and inventing consequences for their actions and just moving forward.
